question: Meridian Imports LLP, based in Mumbai, is importing industrial compressors from a supplier in Germany. Their customs broker filed the Bill of Entry at JNCH Nhava Sheva along with a single commercial invoice that includes unit prices, total value, payment terms, and shipping terms — but does not list the gross weight, net weight, or marks and numbers for each package. The appraising officer has refused to process the consignment, demanding a separate packing list. Meridian's in-house counsel argues that since CBIC has allowed merged documents, the officer cannot insist on a separate packing list. The customs broker counters that the merged document is incomplete and therefore the officer is within rights to demand a separate packing list. Who is correct, and what specific data fields must the combined commercial invoice cum packing list contain for Customs to accept it as a standalone document without requiring a separate packing list?
why_this_chunk: Para 2 and 3 of the circular enumerate the exact data fields (gross weight, net weight, marks and numbers, number/types of packages, quantity, description) that must be present in a combined document. If any are missing, the document is incomplete and the officer's demand is justified — this chunk is the sole authoritative source for that field list.

grading.reason: All four scores are 1/medium: the chunk directly answers who is correct (the broker) and lists the exact missing fields (marks and numbers, gross weight, net weight, number/type of packages) that make the merged document incomplete, matching the scenario precisely.

question: A consumer rights NGO based in Tamil Nadu has applied to the Standing Committee of the Consumer Welfare Fund for a Rs 15 lakh grant to run awareness camps about GST on essential commodities. The Committee's next meeting has only the Vice-Chairman and two other members present, as the Chairman is abroad and one member resigned last month. The NGO is unregistered and has refused to open its premises for inspection, citing that its accounts are with an external auditor. Can the Committee validly convene this meeting and deliberate on the application? Separately, can it compel the NGO to allow entry and inspection of the premises from which the consumer-welfare activities are claimed to operate, and to produce its books and accounts — and what happens if the NGO refuses?
why_this_chunk: Sub-rule (5)(f) requiring the Chairman or Vice-Chairman plus minimum three other members for a valid meeting is directly tested, as is sub-rule (6)(a)–(c) granting the Committee power to require registration, document production, and premises inspection — all specific provisions in this chunk.

grading.reason: All sub-questions are directly answerable from this chunk: quorum rule (Chairman/VC + min 3 members), Vice-Chairman can convene, inspection/production powers under sub-sections 6(b) and 6(c), and the chunk covers refusal consequences implicitly through the powers granted.

question: Meridian Exports LLP (GSTIN 06AABFM1234Q1Z5, Haryana) filed a refund claim for Rs 48 lakh of accumulated ITC on zero-rated supplies. The proper officer sanctioned the full amount under the integrated tax head after verification. Meridian's CFO now wants to understand: of the total sanctioned refund, what portion is the government required to deposit into the Consumer Welfare Fund, and under what authority? Additionally, if Meridian later wins an appeal that directs a further Rs 6 lakh payout from the Fund, what is the prescribed mechanism for that disbursement — does the appellate authority release the money directly, or must it come from the Fund through the proper officer?
why_this_chunk: The chunk's proviso specifying that 50% of integrated tax determined under Section 54 CGST read with Section 20 IGST must be deposited in the Fund is indispensable, as is sub-rule (2) prescribing that amounts ordered by proper officer/appellate authority/court shall be paid FROM the Fund.

grading.reason: Both sub-questions are directly answered: 50% of integrated tax goes to the Fund (sub-section 1 proviso), and appellate-ordered amounts are paid FROM the Fund (sub-section 2), not directly by the appellate authority.

question: Sarvottam Agri Exports Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 27AABCS1234F1ZP), a Maharashtra-based exporter of basmati rice, ships a consolidated consignment to three different buyers in UAE, Saudi Arabia, and Qatar under a single Shipping Bill filed at Mundra port. Their freight forwarder prepares one combined commercial invoice cum packing list covering all three buyers, but the document only shows aggregate quantity and total gross weight for the entire shipment without breaking down marks and numbers, number of packages, and types of packages buyer-wise. The Customs officer at the port refuses clearance, stating the merged document must contain package-level details. Sarvottam's CA argues that the circular on merging documents was meant to reduce documentation burden and that aggregate figures should suffice for a single Shipping Bill. Meanwhile, the buyer in Saudi Arabia requires a separate packing list for their import clearance, which Sarvottam has prepared but not submitted to Indian Customs. Does the circular mandate package-level granularity in the combined document, and is Sarvottam obligated to submit the separate packing list prepared for the Saudi buyer to Indian Customs as well?
why_this_chunk: Para 3 specifies that the combined document must contain ALL the data fields listed in para 2 (including marks and numbers, number of packages, types of packages) to replace the separate packing list — aggregate figures would not satisfy these field requirements. Para 3 also clarifies that submitting a separate packing list remains optional for the exporter, so the Saudi buyer's copy need not be filed with Indian Customs. Both answers hinge on this circular's specific provisions.

grading.reason: The chunk answers the first part (package-level details ARE required in the combined document per para 2-3), but does not address whether Sarvottam must submit the Saudi buyer's separate packing list to Indian Customs — the circular only says a separate packing list 'would also be accepted' if voluntarily submitted, not whether one prepared for a foreign buyer creates an obligation, making the question only partially answerable from this chunk alone.

question: GameVerse Technologies Pvt Ltd, a real-money online gaming platform registered in Karnataka (GSTIN 29AABCG5678M1Z5), supplies services to unregistered players nationwide. During an internal audit for Q3 FY 2025-26, they discovered that for transactions below ₹50,000 where players did not request address details, invoices were issued without the recipient's state name — relying on the general Rule 46(f) exemption for low-value B2C supplies. The tax team argues this was correct since each transaction was under ₹50,000. However, the external auditor flags that online money gaming has a specific carve-out. Who is right, and what is the GST and place-of-supply consequence if the state was not captured — does the supply default to an intra-state supply in Karnataka, and can GameVerse retrospectively correct the invoices and GST returns to claim the correct treatment?
why_this_chunk: The proviso to Rule 46(f) explicitly carves out 'supply of online money gaming' from the ₹50,000 threshold, requiring the recipient's state name on every invoice irrespective of value — this carve-out overrides the general rule the tax team relied on, and the deemed-address-on-record mechanism determines whether place of supply falls under Section 12(2)(b)(i) or defaults to supplier location under (ii).

grading.reason: All scores are 1; complexity is high because the question requires cross-referencing Rule 46(f) main text vs. its proviso for online money gaming, Section 12 IGST place-of-supply defaulting to supplier location when address is absent, and the retrospective correction implications.

question: PixelStream Media LLP, registered in Maharashtra (GSTIN 27AABFP1234R1ZX), operates an OTT video streaming platform. They supply subscriptions to unregistered individual consumers across India. For a subscriber in Jaipur, Rajasthan, the platform's app collected the subscriber's name and city but not their full address — however the invoice auto-populated 'Rajasthan' as the state. For another subscriber who used a VPN and provided no location details whatsoever, the invoice was issued without any state name. The CFO wants to know: in each case, what is the place of supply for GST purposes, which tax applies (CGST+SGST or IGST), and whether the invoices as issued are compliant — specifically, does merely recording the state name on the invoice for the Jaipur subscriber legally constitute having an 'address on record' so that Rajasthan becomes the place of supply?
why_this_chunk: The proviso to Rule 46(f) deems the state name recorded on a tax invoice by an OIDAR/online service supplier to be the 'address on record' of an unregistered recipient, which directly determines whether IGST Section 12(2)(b)(i) or (ii) applies — this deemed-address mechanism is the crux of both scenarios.

grading.reason: All scores are 1; complexity is high because the question requires applying Section 12 IGST, Rule 46 proviso (deemed address), and Section 31 CGST across two distinct subscriber scenarios with compliance analysis.

question: Skybridge Aviation Services LLP, based in Mumbai, has a ground-handling and catering contract with Air Canada for its newly launched Delhi–Toronto route. In the first week of July 2025, Skybridge imports aircraft catering equipment and in-flight meal containers from a Canadian supplier, shipping them to IGI Airport, New Delhi. Skybridge claims that since the goods are exclusively for use by Air Canada — an airline now covered under the amended exemption notification for foreign airlines — the consignment should be exempt from basic customs duty under the same benefit. Customs at IGI Airport has issued a query memo asking Skybridge to justify the exemption claim. Separately, Skybridge is also unclear whether IGST on these imports can be claimed as input tax credit under GST, given that their output supply (catering services to Air Canada) may qualify as an export of service or a zero-rated supply. How should Skybridge respond to the customs query, and what is the correct duty and tax treatment of this import?
why_this_chunk: The chunk establishes that Notification 32/2025 extends exemption benefits specifically to 'Air Canada' as an airline entry (S.No.48) in the table of Notification 130/2010. Determining whether a third-party ground handler like Skybridge can claim this exemption — which is airline-specific, not contractor-specific — requires interpreting the scope of the table entry and the parent notification's conditions, making this chunk indispensable.

grading.reason: The chunk only establishes that Air Canada/Canada is added to Notification 130/2010-Customs effective 1 July 2025, but does not reveal what goods/conditions the notification covers, making it impossible to determine from this chunk alone whether Skybridge (a third-party contractor, not Air Canada itself) qualifies for the exemption, nor does it address IGST/ITC treatment at all.

question: TransGlobal Duty Free Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 07AABCT1234R1ZP), a licensed warehouse operator at Delhi's IGI Airport, supplies bonded stores — liquor, tobacco, and confectionery — to multiple foreign airlines for consumption on outbound international flights. Prior to 1 July 2025, TransGlobal was paying basic customs duty on stores supplied to Air Canada because Air Canada was not listed in the exemption notification applicable to foreign airlines. Now, Air Canada's procurement manager has asked TransGlobal to retrospectively seek a refund of customs duty paid on bonded stores supplied between January and June 2025, arguing that the amendment should be read as clarificatory and therefore retrospective. Additionally, for stores supplied from 1 July 2025 onward, Air Canada wants TransGlobal to confirm that no customs duty, no IGST, and no compensation cess will apply. What is the correct legal position on both the retrospective refund claim and the prospective duty/tax treatment of bonded stores supplied to Air Canada from July 2025?
why_this_chunk: The chunk explicitly states 'This notification shall come into force on the 1st day of July, 2025,' establishing prospective applicability. It also shows Air Canada was only added as S.No.48 by this amendment — meaning it was NOT covered before this date. Both facts are essential to rejecting the retrospective refund claim and confirming the prospective exemption, making this chunk the indispensable authority.

grading.reason: The chunk confirms Air Canada was added to the exemption table effective 1 July 2025 (prospective treatment answerable), but it contains no information on retrospective applicability, refund eligibility, IGST/compensation cess treatment, or whether the amendment is clarificatory — so the retrospective refund question cannot be answered from this chunk alone.

question: Sarvottam Engineering Pvt Ltd, based in Jaipur, Rajasthan (GSTIN 08AADCS5678N1Z5), inadvertently deposited ₹12,00,000 under the 'Interest' minor head of CGST instead of the 'Tax' minor head while making their monthly GST payment through the electronic cash ledger. They have zero outstanding interest liability but have a confirmed CGST tax demand of ₹11,50,000 for the same period, plus a separate penalty of ₹50,000 under CGST. Their CA is asking whether the entire ₹12,00,000 can be reallocated — first ₹11,50,000 from the 'Interest' minor head to the 'Tax' minor head under CGST, and then ₹50,000 from 'Interest' to 'Penalty' minor head under the same CGST major head — all in a single application, or whether separate applications are needed for each transfer, and whether the second transfer can proceed if the first one has already reduced the available balance.
why_this_chunk: The chunk's explicit provision that 'amount from one minor head can also be transferred to another minor head under the same major head' and the balance-availability-at-time-of-transfer rule are directly required to resolve whether sequential intra-major-head transfers are valid and how the diminishing balance affects the second transfer.

grading.reason: All three sub-questions (cross-minor-head transfer, same-major-head transfer, sequential balance dependency) are directly addressed by points 3, 4, and 5 of the chunk.

question: Pinnacle Polymers LLP, registered in Gujarat (GSTIN 24AABFP1234M1ZQ), made an excess deposit of ₹8,50,000 under the IGST head while paying for an inter-state purchase from a supplier in Maharashtra. However, their actual IGST liability for the month turned out to be only ₹2,10,000, and they now have a pending CGST liability of ₹4,00,000 and SGST liability of ₹2,40,000 from intra-state sales within Gujarat. Their accountant wants to transfer the surplus amount lying under the IGST minor head in the electronic cash ledger to the CGST and SGST heads rather than claiming a refund. Can the firm transfer amounts from the IGST head to both CGST and SGST heads directly through the cash ledger, and what conditions must be satisfied at the time of initiating such transfer — particularly, does the balance need to be available in the source head at the exact time of filing, or can it be done in anticipation of a future deposit?
why_this_chunk: The chunk's rule that 'amount can be transferred from the head only if balance under that head is available at the time of transfer' and the provision allowing transfer from one major/minor head to another (including different minor heads) are indispensable to determining whether this cross-head transfer is permissible and what the timing constraint is.

grading.reason: The chunk directly addresses cross-head transfer rules in the cash ledger and explicitly answers the condition question about balance availability at time of transfer, making the question well-matched and answerable from this chunk alone.

question: Our client, Kisaan Agri Commodities LLP based in Indore (Madhya Pradesh), supplies unbranded whole wheat grain to Rajesh Trading Co. in Nagpur (Maharashtra). They pack the wheat in 30 kg jute bags with only the net weight printed on the bag — no brand name, no retail sale declaration. Until June 2024, they were treating this as exempt (nil-rated) on the basis that it is not 'pre-packaged and labelled' agricultural produce. Now, for invoices raised from August 2024 onwards, the buyer's CA is insisting that since the bag shows weight and exceeds 25 kg, it must be treated as pre-packaged and labelled, attracting 5% IGST. Kisaan's management wants to know: (a) post the July 2024 amendment, does a 30 kg package of wheat with only net quantity marked — no brand, no declaration under the Legal Metrology Act — qualify as 'pre-packaged and labelled' for GST purposes, and (b) since this is an inter-state supply from MP to Maharashtra, does the IGST rate notification or the CGST rate notification govern the classification, and is there any divergence between the two on this specific proviso?
why_this_chunk: The proviso inserted after Schedule VII Explanation clause (ii) explicitly states that agricultural farm produce in packages >25 kg shall NOT be considered 'pre-packaged and labelled' notwithstanding the Legal Metrology Act — this is the dispositive provision for determining whether the 30 kg wheat bag attracts GST.

grading.reason: All scores are 1; complexity is high because the question requires applying the July 2024 IGST Rate notification proviso to a specific inter-state transaction, interpreting 'pre-packaged and labelled' under Legal Metrology Act override, and distinguishing IGST vs CGST notification applicability — the chunk directly contains the proviso text that resolves part (a) and is the IGST notification, which addresses part (b).

question: Bharat Steel Fabricators Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 27AABCB1234R1ZP, Pune) manufactures milk storage and transport cans in both stainless steel and aluminium variants. They have a purchase order dated 20-Jul-2024 from Amul Dairy Cooperative in Anand, Gujarat — 500 steel milk cans (HSN 7310) and 300 aluminium milk cans (HSN 7612) to be shipped directly to Amul's collection centres across Gujarat. Until now Bharat Steel was charging 18% IGST on both variants. Their tax consultant says that a mid-2024 rate notification has moved milk cans to a lower slab, but Bharat Steel is unsure whether both metals get the same treatment, what the new applicable IGST rate is for each variant, and from exactly which date the reduced rate kicks in — especially since the PO was placed on 20-Jul-2024 but some cans were dispatched on 12-Jul-2024 (before the notified effective date) and the rest on 22-Jul-2024. How should Bharat Steel correctly invoice these two shipments?
why_this_chunk: Clauses (ii) and (iv)/(v) of this notification carve out milk cans of iron/steel from serial 224 and milk cans of aluminium from serial 273, moving aluminium milk cans to serial 275A — the specific serial-number amendments and the effective date of 15-Jul-2024 are indispensable to determining the correct rate for each metal type and each dispatch date.

grading.reason: All four score dimensions pass: the chunk contains the exact notification amendments carving out milk cans of iron/steel and aluminium from higher-rate entries and inserting them into lower-rate entries, with the effective date of 15-Jul-2024 explicitly stated — sufficient to answer both the rate question and the split-shipment invoicing question (12-Jul pre-notification vs 22-Jul post-notification).

question: Our client, Meridian Polymers Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 27AABCM1234E1ZP), headquartered in Pune, Maharashtra, is preparing its GSTR-9C reconciliation for FY 2024-25. Their audited financials show total revenue of ₹18.4 crore, but the GSTR-9 annual return reports turnover of only ₹16.9 crore. The differences arise from three simultaneous issues: (a) they transitioned out of composition scheme in September 2024, with ₹62 lakh turnover under composition in H1, (b) they issued credit notes totalling ₹38 lakh in May 2025 for defective polymer batches supplied in February 2025 — these are reflected in GSTR-9 but not in the audited financials for FY 2024-25, and (c) they recorded ₹45 lakh of unbilled revenue on an accrual basis for a long-term supply contract with Reliance Industries where GST was not yet payable since invoicing had not occurred. Additionally, their audited financials include ₹12 lakh in volume-based trade discounts given to distributors that were not passed through credit notes and on which GST was originally charged. How should each of these items be reported across Tables 5E, 5F, 5H, and 5L of the reconciliation statement, and what is the correct reconciled turnover that should appear at Table 5Q?
why_this_chunk: The chunk's Table 5L (composition scheme transition turnover), 5E (post-March credit notes reflected in GSTR-9), 5H (unbilled accrual revenue where GST not yet payable), and 5F (trade discounts on which GST was leviable) are each indispensable for classifying these four adjustments correctly and arriving at the 5Q reconciled figure.

grading.reason: All four sub-items map directly to tables defined in the chunk (5E, 5F, 5H, 5L, 5Q), making the chunk fully sufficient to answer the multi-part reconciliation question.

question: Rajaram Textiles LLP, based in Panipat, Haryana, exports a consignment of cotton chenille rag rug durries (cotton content 72% by weight) to a buyer in Dubai. The FOB value of the shipment is Rs. 18,50,000. Rajaram has availed CENVAT credit on the imported synthetic yarn blended into the durries. Separately, Rajaram also exported dyed handloom cotton lungies worth Rs. 4,20,000 FOB in the same shipping bill, without availing any CENVAT credit. Their customs broker is claiming drawback on both items under the textile drawback schedule. Can Rajaram claim duty drawback on both product lines, and if so, under which serial numbers and at what rates? Does the CENVAT availment on the durries affect the lungi claim or are they assessed independently?
why_this_chunk: The chunk specifically substitutes the description for Serial 57.02 to include 'Cotton Durries / Cotton Chenille Rag Rug Durries' where cotton predominates by weight, and Serial 52.01 explicitly excludes lungies from dyed/coloured handloom fabrics — both product classifications and the CENVAT condition are indispensable from this notification.

grading.reason: The chunk directly contains the relevant drawback schedule entries for Cotton Chenille Rag Rug Durries (57.02) and the exclusion of lungies from 52.01 (dyed/coloured handloom fabrics), enabling a specific answer about serial numbers, rates, and independent assessment, though the exact rate for 57.02 and lungi-specific serial number require inference from adjacent entries not fully shown.

question: Snowpeak Apparels Pvt Ltd, registered in Ludhiana, Punjab, manufactures men's blazers from wool-worsted blended fabric (wool content 38%) and exports them to a UK buyer at an FOB value of Rs. 32,00,000. Snowpeak has availed CENVAT facility on inputs. However, Snowpeak also filed a rebate claim under Rule 18 of the Central Excise Rules, 2002 on the excise duty paid on some of the same input fabrics used in manufacturing these blazers, before their CA flagged a potential conflict. What is the applicable drawback rate for the blazers, and does the simultaneous claim of Rule 18 rebate on input materials disqualify Snowpeak from receiving any drawback under General Note 2(e)? Would the position differ if the blazers were made from shoddy yarn with 40% wool content?
why_this_chunk: Serial 62.151 inserted by this notification prescribes the 2.8% FOB drawback rate specifically for woollen garments with wool content not less than 35% when CENVAT is availed, excluding shoddy fabric/yarn. The amended General Note 2(e) substituted by clause (a) of this very notification bars drawback when Rule 18 rebate is claimed — both provisions are indispensable.

grading.reason: All three sub-questions are directly answerable from this chunk: the 2.8% FOB drawback rate for wool-worsted blended fabric blazers (wool ≥35%) with CENVAT availed (Serial 62.151), the disqualification under substituted General Note 2(e) for simultaneous Rule 18 rebate claims, and the shoddy yarn exclusion — making this highly specific and multi-step.

question: Prashant & Associates LLP, a CA firm in Bengaluru, is auditing Vayuveg Exports Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 29AADCV5678F1Z2) for FY 2024-25. Vayuveg operates as an EOU and also procures inputs from Kandla SEZ. Their audited Annual Financial Statement shows ₹24.6 crore turnover, while GSTR-9 shows ₹23.1 crore. The auditors have identified the following reconciling items: (i) ₹48 lakh of goods received from the SEZ unit for which Vayuveg filed bills of entry as a DTA recipient, (ii) deemed supplies of ₹32 lakh under Schedule I comprising stock transfers to their Hyderabad branch (GSTIN 36AADCV5678F1Z8) that are already included in the audited revenue, (iii) a forex fluctuation loss of ₹27 lakh on export receivables that reduced audited revenue but has no GST impact, and (iv) credit notes of ₹22 lakh issued to a Dubai buyer for quality rejections — these were booked in FY 2024-25 accounts but are not admissible under Section 34 since the recipient is outside India and no tax adjustment is possible. The auditors need to know: which specific tables in the reconciliation statement should capture each of these four items, should the SEZ-to-DTA supply value be excluded or included when computing the gap, and what is the treatment of the inadmissible export credit notes — do they increase or decrease the reconciled turnover at 5Q?
why_this_chunk: This question directly requires Table 5K (SEZ-to-DTA bill of entry supplies), 5D (deemed supplies under Schedule I with the proviso about excluding those already in audited financials), 5N (forex fluctuation differences), and 5J (credit notes not admissible under Section 34) — all specific instructions from this chunk — plus understanding how they feed into 5Q.

grading.reason: All four reconciling items map directly to specific tables in the chunk (5K for SEZ-to-DTA, 5D for deemed supplies, 5N for forex, 5J for inadmissible credit notes), and 5Q treatment is also addressed, making the chunk fully sufficient to answer the multi-part question.

question: Meridian Auto Components Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 27AABCM1234F1ZP, Maharashtra) received capital goods from a vendor in Gujarat in March 2019 worth ₹48 lakh plus ₹8.64 lakh IGST. The company's accounts team missed claiming ITC on these invoices in the March 2019 GSTR-3B. They eventually claimed the ITC in the GSTR-3B filed for July 2019. Separately, during FY 2018-19, the company had reversed ₹2.1 lakh of ITC under the second proviso to section 16(2) on certain invoices where payment was not made within 180 days; these payments were finally settled in November 2019, and the ITC was reclaimed in the November 2019 GSTR-3B. Now, while preparing the annual return for FY 2018-19, the CA is confused: should the ₹8.64 lakh ITC availed in July 2019 for goods received in FY 2018-19 be disclosed in the FY 2018-19 annual return, and where exactly should the ₹2.1 lakh reclaimed ITC be reported — in the FY 2018-19 annual return or the FY 2019-20 annual return? The company also wants to know if they can simply skip these tables given the optional relaxation available for early financial years.
why_this_chunk: The chunk explicitly prescribes that ITC for goods received in a previous FY but availed in April-September of the next FY must be declared in the annual return using Table 4(A) of GSTR-3B details, and that ITC reversed per second proviso to section 16(2) but reclaimed in the next FY must be reported in that next FY's annual return. It also specifies the option to not fill these tables for FY 2017-18, 2018-19, and 2019-20.

grading.reason: All three sub-questions are directly answered by the chunk: prior-year ITC availed April–September goes in FY 2018-19 annual return, the ₹2.1 lakh reclaimed in FY 2019-20 goes in FY 2019-20 annual return, and the optional non-filing relaxation for FY 2017-18/2018-19/2019-20 is explicitly stated.

question: Meridian Polymers Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 27AABCM1234R1ZP), a Mumbai-based manufacturer, follows accrual accounting and reported Rs 4.82 crore as unbilled revenue in its audited financials for FY 2023-24 — representing goods dispatched in March 2024 but invoiced only in April 2024. Separately, during the same FY, Meridian issued credit notes totalling Rs 1.15 crore to three buyers across Gujarat and Karnataka for quality rejections, but the department is contesting that these credit notes do not satisfy the conditions under Section 34 of the CGST Act because the recipients have not reversed their ITC. While filing the annual reconciliation statement, Meridian's CA is unsure: (a) should the Rs 4.82 crore unbilled revenue be added to turnover in the reconciliation even though GST was discharged on it in April 2024 returns, and (b) for the Rs 1.15 crore in disputed credit notes, must the full amount be declared as an addition to turnover in the reconciliation if Section 34 admissibility is under challenge, and what is the interplay between the GSTR-9 annual return figures and the audited P&L for these two adjustments?
why_this_chunk: Table 5H specifically governs unbilled revenue recognized on accrual basis where GST was not payable in the same FY, and Table 5J requires declaration of credit notes accounted in audited financials but inadmissible under Section 34 CGST Act — both are indispensable to determining the correct reconciliation treatment.

grading.reason: Both sub-questions map directly to Tables 5H (unbilled revenue on accrual basis where GST not paid in same FY) and 5J (credit notes not admissible under Section 34 CGST Act), making the chunk authoritatively answerable, though the interplay with GSTR-9 vs P&L requires reading across both table instructions simultaneously.

question: Prakash Electricals LLP (GSTIN 29AAFFP5678K1Z5, Karnataka) had the following events during FY 2019-20 and FY 2020-21: (a) refund of ₹12.4 lakh claimed in January 2020 for inverted duty structure on wiring harnesses — ₹9.8 lakh was sanctioned in March 2020 and ₹2.6 lakh was rejected; (b) a separate IGST export refund of ₹6.2 lakh filed in March 2020 for which only an acknowledgement was received but no order was passed by 31 March 2020; (c) a demand order was issued in February 2020 confirming ₹3.5 lakh of CGST and ₹3.5 lakh of SGST on alleged wrongful ITC, of which the firm paid ₹2 lakh under protest; and (d) ITC of ₹1.8 lakh on services received in FY 2019-20 was claimed only in the August 2020 GSTR-3B after the invoices were traced during an internal audit. The firm's tax consultant needs to know exactly how each of these four items should be reported across Tables 15A through 15G and the ITC reconciliation section in the FY 2019-20 GSTR-9, and whether the firm can exercise the option to leave any of these tables blank given the relaxation notifications.
why_this_chunk: The chunk provides the authoritative instructions for Tables 15A-15D (refund claimed/sanctioned/rejected/pending aggregation excluding provisional refunds and non-GST claims), Tables 15E-15G (confirmed demands and taxes paid thereon), the ITC-availed-in-next-FY disclosure rule referencing Table 4(A) of GSTR-3B for April-September window, and the optional relaxation inserted by Notification 56/2019 and extended by Notification 79/2020 for FY 2017-18, 2018-19, and 2019-20.

grading.reason: The chunk covers only the general instructions for Tables 15A-15G and a brief note on prior-year ITC, but lacks the specific rules needed to answer how each of the four scenario items maps to specific sub-tables, the demand/ITC reconciliation section treatment, or the interplay between partial sanction, protest payments, and late ITC claims — the question requires information from multiple other chunks/sections beyond what is here.

question: Pinnacle Electronics LLP (GSTIN 29AAGFP5678Q1Z5) in Bengaluru operates as a DTA unit and procured semiconductor components worth Rs 2.3 crore during FY 2023-24 from Foxconn India SEZ unit in Tamil Nadu, filing bills of entry for each consignment. In the same year, Pinnacle offered trade discounts of Rs 38 lakh to its channel partners — these discounts were post-supply volume incentives not linked to specific invoices, recorded as expenses in the audited annual accounts but no credit notes were issued and no GST adjustment was made at the time of supply. Pinnacle's auditor is now preparing the GSTR-9C reconciliation and wants to know: (a) how should the Rs 2.3 crore SEZ-to-DTA procurement value be treated in the turnover reconciliation given that bills of entry were filed but these are inward supplies not outward turnover, and (b) must the Rs 38 lakh in post-supply trade discounts — which were not passed through tax credit notes and hence GST was collected on the full invoice value — be declared as an upward adjustment to reconciled turnover, and under which specific table of the reconciliation statement should each item appear?
why_this_chunk: Table 5K mandates declaration of SEZ-to-DTA supplies where bills of entry were filed, and Table 5F requires declaration of trade discounts in audited financials on which GST was leviable (being not permissible as deduction) — both tables are directly needed to resolve the reconciliation treatment for each item.

grading.reason: Chunk contains Table 5K (SEZ-to-DTA bill of entry supplies) and Table 5F (non-permissible trade discounts on which GST was leviable) directly addressing both sub-questions, making the chunk sufficient to answer despite the elaborate fact pattern.

question: Biswas Textiles Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 19AABCB1234R1Z5) is a monthly filer with its principal place of business registered in Murshidabad, West Bengal, and an additional place of business in Malda, West Bengal. The company filed its GSTR-3B for October 2024 on 8th December 2024 — well after the normal 20th November deadline. The GST portal auto-computed a late fee of ₹2,950 and interest under section 50 on a net tax liability of ₹4,18,000. The company's accountant believes no late fee or interest is payable because the government extended the deadline for Murshidabad-based taxpayers. However, the jurisdictional officer is arguing that because the company also has an additional place of business in Malda (which was not covered by any extension notification), the extension does not apply. question: Meridian Logistics Pvt Ltd, a licensed special warehouse operator in JNPT (Nhava Sheva), Mumbai, stores imported sensitive electronic components under bond. question: Dr. Ananya Reddy, a resident of Hyderabad, has been prescribed a rare immunotherapy drug (Pembrolizumab) not commercially available in India for her Stage IV cancer treatment. Her oncologist has obtained a certificate from the Assistant Director General, Health Services, New Delhi confirming it is a life-saving medicine. However, due to the urgency, the drug shipment has already arrived at Chennai Customs and Dr. Reddy's family could not produce the certificate at the time of clearance — they have instead given an undertaking to the Assistant Commissioner of Customs to furnish it within 30 days. Meanwhile, the Department of Atomic Energy has separately arranged import of certain laser components through the same port under the same notification, and they are questioning whether the life-saving drug exemption procedure (specifically the undertaking mechanism) sets a precedent for their own delayed-certificate situation. Does the undertaking mechanism available for life-saving drugs also extend to imports requiring Department of Atomic Energy certification, and is Dr. Reddy's family's undertaking to the Assistant Commissioner valid for claiming the exemption?
why_this_chunk: Condition 8 for life-saving drugs explicitly permits an undertaking 'as acceptable to the Deputy Commissioner or the Assistant Commissioner' to furnish the DGHS certificate within a specified period, failing which duty becomes payable — this validates the undertaking route for Dr. Reddy. Condition 9 (atomic energy) requires the certificate to be furnished 'at the time of import' with no analogous undertaking provision mentioned in this chunk, creating the interpretive contrast. Both conditions are squarely in this chunk.

grading.reason: The chunk directly addresses both the life-saving drug undertaking mechanism (Condition 8) and the Department of Atomic Energy certification requirement, making both parts of the question answerable from this single source.

question: Steelworth Pvt Ltd in Jamshedpur imports Low Ash Metallurgical Coke partly from a trading house in Singapore that sources the material from a Colombian mine, and partly from a Russian producer shipping directly. For the Singapore consignment, the certificate of origin shows Colombia as country of origin, but the goods were transshipped and invoiced through Singapore. The importer contends that since Singapore is not among the countries listed in the anti-dumping notification, no provisional anti-dumping duty should apply to the Singapore-invoiced consignment. Meanwhile, the notification's validity is approaching the end of its six-month window and the importer is considering delaying the bill of entry filing to fall beyond the expiry date. Are either of these positions — that transshipment through a non-subject country avoids the duty, or that delaying filing past the six-month period eliminates liability — legally sustainable?
why_this_chunk: The notification imposes duty on goods 'originating in or exported from' the listed countries (Colombia and Russia both listed), and the note clarifies classification is per description not tariff heading alone. The six-month validity clause and the bill-of-entry date as the relevant date for duty determination are both directly from this chunk and are indispensable to resolving both contentions.

grading.reason: The chunk directly addresses the six-month validity window and bill-of-entry date as the relevant exchange date, making the delay strategy answerable; the transshipment/country-of-origin question requires inference from the description-based determination note, making both prongs partially but sufficiently answerable from this chunk alone.

question: Meridian Hospitality Pvt Ltd, a boutique hotel in Jaipur approved by the Ministry of Tourism, is importing commercial-grade kitchen equipment (industrial ovens and refrigeration units) from Italy worth CIF INR 1.8 crore. Their customs broker has advised them to claim concessional duty under the relevant customs tariff notification by producing a Ministry of Tourism certificate. However, the certificate they hold is signed by an Under Secretary in the Ministry of Tourism. Separately, the company also wants to import 200 bottles of French sacramental wine for their in-house chapel, and their director has given a personal undertaking to the Assistant Commissioner of Customs that the wine will be used exclusively for sacramental purposes. Will Meridian Hospitality qualify for the concessional rate on either import, and what specific procedural deficiencies, if any, need to be cured before clearance?
why_this_chunk: Condition 3(b) specifically requires the certificate to be from an officer 'not below the rank of a Deputy Secretary' in the Ministry of Tourism certifying the goods are essential to hotel/tourism industry — an Under Secretary is below that rank, so the condition fails. Condition 6 requires the undertaking for sacramental wine to be furnished to the Deputy/Assistant Commissioner of Customs, which is met here. Both conditions are directly in this chunk.

grading.reason: The chunk directly addresses both imports: Condition 3 requires Deputy Secretary rank (not Under Secretary) for hotel/tourism certificate, and Condition 6 requires undertaking to Deputy/Assistant Commissioner (which is satisfied), making both procedural deficiencies answerable from this chunk alone.

question: Meghna Polymers LLP (GSTIN 24AABFM1234K1ZP, Ahmedabad) purchased raw HDPE granules worth ₹47 lakh from Shivam Plastics Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 27AADCS5678L1ZR, Pune) during Q3 FY 2025-26. Meghna's accountant noticed that while the invoices appear in GSTR-2B, the ITC on roughly ₹12 lakh of those purchases is flagged as 'not available' in the auto-generated statement. On inquiry, they learn that Shivam Plastics has been consistently under-reporting output tax — specifically, the output tax payable per Shivam's GSTR-1 filings significantly exceeds the output tax actually paid in their GSTR-3B returns over the preceding two quarters. Meghna has all valid tax invoices and has made payments through banking channels. Can Meghna still claim the blocked ITC by any alternative mechanism, and what recourse does Meghna have against the restriction imposed in the auto-generated statement, given that from the recipient's side every compliance condition has been met?
why_this_chunk: Section 38(2)(b)(iii) specifically provides that ITC shall be shown as unavailable when the supplier's output tax payable per GSTR-1 exceeds the output tax actually paid beyond a prescribed limit — this is the exact statutory basis for the restriction Meghna faces and is indispensable to advising on the legality and scope of the block.

grading.reason: The chunk directly covers Section 38(2)(b)(iii) which is exactly the scenario described (supplier's GSTR-1 output tax exceeding GSTR-3B payment), making it answerable, though the chunk does not address alternative mechanisms or recourse procedures, slightly limiting full answerability — however the restriction basis is clearly established.

question: Our client, Meenakshi Handicrafts LLP based in Moradabad, UP (IEC 0503XXXXXX), exports brass artware items that are unconditionally exempt from central excise duty. They have been exporting under claim for rebate and the Customs officer at ICD Tughlakabad is insisting that they produce a certificate from the Superintendent of Central Excise confirming non-availment of CENVAT credit on inputs, before sanctioning the higher drawback rate (where CENVAT facility has not been availed). The officer's position is that since the exporter also procures some semi-finished castings from a job worker registered under Central Excise, the exemption from producing the certificate does not apply. The drawback claim is for Rs. 14.7 lakhs across 23 shipping bills filed between January and March 2025. Is the officer's insistence legally correct, and can the LLP claim the higher drawback rate without the superintendent's certificate?
why_this_chunk: The proviso to para 8(ii) specifically exempts 'handicrafts (including handicrafts of brass artware)' that are unconditionally exempt from excise duty from the requirement of producing the Superintendent's certificate regarding non-availment of CENVAT facility — this proviso is indispensable to resolving whether the officer's demand is legally sustainable.

grading.reason: All scores are 1; complexity is high because it requires applying the proviso exception to the certificate requirement, evaluating the officer's additional argument about job-worker-sourced inputs, and connecting these to a specific exporter scenario with monetary stakes.

question: Aadhya Engineering Works, a proprietorship in Jaipur (GSTIN 08BZTPA9012R1ZX), receives fabrication services from two vendors: (1) Rudra Steels Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 08AABCR3456D1Z5), which took GST registration only 38 days ago and has filed its first GSTR-1, and (2) Kailash Industrial Supplies (GSTIN 08DEXPK7890M1ZQ), which has accumulated ITC claims in its own returns that substantially exceed what it is entitled to based on its own inward supply auto-generated statement for the last two prescribed periods. When Aadhya's tax team downloads the auto-generated inward supply statement for the current period, they find ITC from both vendors flagged as restricted — one due to the vendor's recent registration and the other due to the vendor's excess ITC availment. Aadhya's CA wants to understand: under what distinct statutory grounds has the credit been restricted for each vendor, whether the two restrictions operate identically or have different prescribed thresholds and cure mechanisms, and whether Aadhya can provisionally avail any portion of this blocked credit while the vendors regularise their compliance?
why_this_chunk: The question turns on two separate clauses of Section 38(2)(b) — clause (i) restricting ITC when the supplier is within the prescribed period after taking registration, and clause (iv) restricting ITC when the supplier has availed excess input tax credit beyond prescribed limits. Both clauses are enumerated in this chunk and are indispensable to distinguishing the two restriction grounds and advising on their respective remedies.

grading.reason: All four sub-questions map directly to Section 38(2)(b)(i) and (iv) which are both present in the chunk, though cure mechanisms and provisional availment thresholds depend on rules/notifications not reproduced here, making the answerable score borderline but defensible from the statutory text alone.

question: Pinnacle Engineering Pvt Ltd in Hyderabad manufactures and exports industrial cooling systems. A recent export consignment to a buyer in Nigeria consisted of a custom-built evaporative cooling unit that combines components falling under multiple tariff headings — an electric fan assembly (heading 84.14 of the drawback table), a voltage regulator (heading 84.17), copper tubing (heading 74.xx), and a fabricated steel housing (heading 73.xx). No composite drawback rate exists for this assembled unit. The company filed a drawback claim of Rs. 8.3 lakhs by applying the individual constituent material rates based on a self-declaration of net material content. Customs has rejected the claim, stating that since the electric fan and regulator have a specific additional drawback provision when exported together, the company cannot simultaneously use the self-declaration route under the composite article rule for those same components and must split the claim. Is Customs correct in requiring bifurcation, and how should the drawback claim be structured to maximize the eligible amount?
why_this_chunk: Para 11 (composite article self-declaration based on constituent material rates) and para 9 (specific additional drawback for electric fans exported with regulators) both apply and potentially conflict — the question turns on whether the specific provision in para 9 overrides the general composite-article mechanism in para 11 for the fan+regulator portion of the unit.

grading.reason: All relevant rules are present: para 9 (fan+regulator additional drawback), para 11 (composite article self-declaration), and the interaction between them directly addresses the bifurcation question.

question: Pinnacle Polymers Pvt Ltd, based in Dahej SEZ (Gujarat, GSTIN 24AABCP9912R1ZP), imports specialty polymer resins from a German supplier under bond and manufactures finished plastic automotive components in its bonded warehouse. In FY 2024-25, the total value of goods manufactured from imported raw materials was ₹18.4 crore, of which ₹8.8 crore worth was exported to a buyer in Thailand and ₹9.6 crore was sold domestically (including ₹2.1 crore cleared to another SEZ unit in Kandla). Pinnacle's Commissioner is now reviewing renewal of their in-bond manufacture licence. Separately, in 2021, the firm paid a voluntary settlement of ₹4.2 lakh under the Sabka Vishwas (Legacy Dispute Resolution) Scheme for a disputed Central Excise demand from 2019. The Commissioner is asking whether Pinnacle still qualifies for the in-bond manufacture facility, and specifically: (a) does the clearance to the Kandla SEZ unit count as 'export' for the purpose of evaluating export-orientation, and (b) does the settled Central Excise dispute constitute 'involvement in duty evasion' that would disqualify the firm under the eligibility criteria?
why_this_chunk: The chunk's para 2 prescribes the exact three-value comparison test (total manufactured value vs exported vs domestic sale) for export-orientation, and para 3 sets the flexible 50% threshold and the 'no Customs or Excise duty evasion in preceding 5 years' eligibility condition — both are indispensable to resolving whether Pinnacle qualifies.

grading.reason: All three scoring dimensions pass: the chunk's para 2 directly supplies the three-figure export-orientation test (addressing part a), and para 3 supplies the 'Customs or Excise duty evasion in preceding 5 years' eligibility criterion (addressing part b); the question is phrased as a realistic Commissioner-level advisory scenario; and it is tightly anchored to this specific circular's criteria.

question: Sarthi Engineering LLP (Pune, Maharashtra) has applied to the Commissioner of Customs, Nhava Sheva, for a fresh in-bond manufacture licence to import titanium alloy billets from Japan, manufacture aerospace-grade fasteners in a bonded warehouse at Chakan, and export finished goods to Airbus SE in France. The LLP was incorporated only 14 months ago with a paid-up capital of ₹12 lakh, has no prior import-export track record, and its designated premises is a rented 4,000 sq ft unit with basic perimeter fencing but no CCTV or electronic access control. The Commissioner has flagged two concerns: first, whether a newly incorporated entity with limited financial history and modest capitalisation satisfies the 'financially secured' and 'good credibility' requirements; and second, whether the premises meet the 'suitable and adequately secured' condition, given that the Manufacture and Other Operations in Warehouse Regulations, 1966 require specific physical security standards. Meanwhile, Sarthi argues that the facility is purely export-oriented (projected 100% export) and that the cost-recovery customs supervision itself provides adequate oversight. How should the Commissioner evaluate this application, and can the licence be conditionally granted with enhanced security stipulations rather than outright denial?
why_this_chunk: The chunk's para 3 sets out the specific eligibility criteria the Commissioner must evaluate — financial security, good credibility, no duty evasion history, premises suitability, and compliance with the 1966 Warehouse Regulations — and confirms Commissioner-level discretion (para 1) and the cost-recovery supervision requirement, all of which are directly at issue in this application.

grading.reason: All criteria are met: the chunk directly addresses the Commissioner-level evaluation framework, financial credibility requirement, export-orientation test, premises security condition, and cost-recovery supervision — precisely the issues raised in the question.

question: Prism Autoparts Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 27AABCP1234R1ZQ, Maharashtra) received supplies from three vendors during Q3 2025-26, all of whom deducted TDS under GST. When Prism's accountant checks the Electronic Cash Ledger on the portal, she finds that two TDS credits from Bharat Heavy Electricals (GSTIN 09AAACB5678M1Z5, UP) and one from Maharashtra State Road Transport Corporation show CIN entries but no corresponding acknowledgment numbers against the TDS return period. Simultaneously, Prism has paid Rs 12,40,000 via a single challan toward a demand raised under a Show Cause Notice (SCN No. DGGI/MUM/2025/1847), but the ledger shows the amount split across Central Tax and Integrated Tax heads with no Demand ID linkage visible. How should Prism's CA reconcile these cash ledger entries — specifically, what information should appear against each debit under the description field for TDS credits versus SCN-related payments, and what recourse does Prism have if the ledger entries are incomplete or incorrectly mapped?
why_this_chunk: The chunk specifies exactly what must be recorded under 'description' — GSTIN of deductor and acknowledgment number for TDS credits (Note 1 & 3), and SCN Number plus Demand ID for liability payments (Note 4). It also defines how CIN and tax heads (Central Tax, Integrated Tax) are recorded (Notes 3, 6, 7), which is indispensable for diagnosing Prism's reconciliation problem.

grading.reason: All four score dimensions pass: the chunk directly specifies what appears in the description field for TDS credits (GSTIN of deductor, CIN, acknowledgment number of return) and SCN-related payments (SCN number, Demand ID), making it fully answerable; the scenario mirrors real CA reconciliation work; the question is tightly coupled to this specific ledger-description rule chunk.

question: Ganesh Trading LLP (GSTIN 33AABFG9876K1ZP, Tamil Nadu) lost an adjudication case and must make a mandatory pre-deposit to file an appeal before the Appellate Authority. The disputed demand of Rs 28,00,000 covers both CGST and SGST components. Ganesh's advocate deposits Rs 3,50,000 (being 10% rounded up) via two separate challans — one for CGST and one for SGST — on 18-Mar-2026 at 11:42 PM, but only the CGST challan's CIN appears in the Electronic Cash Ledger the next morning, while the SGST challan shows as 'processed by bank' in the bank statement but has no CIN generated. Meanwhile, Ganesh also has an unutilized refund debit of Rs 1,20,000 from an earlier IGST export refund claim that was partially sanctioned. The appeal filing deadline is 20-Mar-2026. How should the cash ledger reflect each of these three transactions (CGST pre-deposit, SGST pre-deposit, and the earlier refund debit), what specific identifiers and descriptions should appear for each, and can Ganesh rely on the bank reference number alone to demonstrate the SGST pre-deposit was made in time if the CIN is not yet generated?
why_this_chunk: The chunk is indispensable because Note 4 specifies that pre-deposit for appeal must be recorded with application number under 'description'; Note 6 defines that date/time of deposit equals date/time of CIN generation by bank (critical to whether SGST deposit is 'made' without CIN); Note 1 clarifies BRN as an alternate reference number; and Note 5 governs how refund debits are recorded — all four notes must be applied together to resolve this scenario.

grading.reason: The chunk directly addresses CIN generation as the date/time of deposit, BRN usage, description fields for pre-deposit/refund, making all three sub-questions answerable from this chunk alone.

question: Pinnacle Agro Industries LLP (GSTIN 06AABFP1234R1Z5, Haryana) received capital goods from a Rajasthan supplier in March 2020 worth ₹48 lakh plus ₹8.64 lakh IGST. The invoice was booked in FY 2019-20 but Pinnacle could not claim ITC in its March 2020 GSTR-3B because the supplier had not uploaded the invoice. The supplier finally uploaded it in May 2020, and Pinnacle claimed the ITC in its July 2020 GSTR-3B. Meanwhile, during an internal audit in January 2020, Pinnacle had reversed ₹3.2 lakh of ITC on certain FY 2019-20 inward supplies because payment was not made within 180 days as required, but the payment was eventually made in November 2020 and Pinnacle reclaimed that ITC in its November 2020 GSTR-3B. When Pinnacle is preparing its GSTR-9 annual return for FY 2019-20, in which specific table and in which financial year's annual return should each of these two ITC amounts — the ₹8.64 lakh claimed late and the ₹3.2 lakh reversed-then-reclaimed — be reported, and can Pinnacle exercise the option to leave any of these tables blank?
why_this_chunk: The chunk specifies that ITC for goods received in FY 2019-20 but availed in April 2020 to September 2020 returns must be declared in the GSTR-9 for FY 2019-20, and that ITC reversed in FY 2019-20 per second proviso to section 16(2) but reclaimed in FY 2020-21 must be furnished in the annual return for FY 2020-21 — both rules are indispensable to correctly placing these two ITC amounts. The optional-fill relaxation for FY 2019-20 is also directly from this chunk.

grading.reason: Chunk directly addresses both ITC scenarios: late-claimed ITC (April–September filing window, Table 4A GSTR-3B cross-reference) and 180-day reversal/reclaim reporting year, plus the option to leave tables blank for FY 2019-20 — all facts needed to answer the multi-part question are present.

question: Prashant Textiles LLP, a provisional registrant in Surat, Gujarat (GSTIN 24BBB…), received a cancellation order on 10-Mar-2026 for non-filing of returns for Oct-2025 through Jan-2026. During this period, they made inter-state supplies worth ₹18.4 lakhs to Deepak Traders in Mumbai, Maharashtra, and intra-state supplies of ₹7.2 lakhs within Gujarat. The LLP's counsel filed all pending GSTR-3B returns on 06-Apr-2026 — 27 days after service of the order. The proper officer is now insisting that the cancellation still stands because the IGST liability on inter-state supplies was short-paid by ₹42,000 due to an incorrect Place of Supply declaration, and that the 30-day cure window only applies if all dues including correctly computed IGST are fully discharged. Is the officer's position legally sustainable, or does the deemed withdrawal operate automatically once returns are furnished within 30 days regardless of a POS dispute on the IGST component?
why_this_chunk: The chunk specifies the 30-day cure mechanism ('if you furnish the return within 30 days… the order shall be deemed to have been withdrawn') and the note that POS details are required only when demand is created under the IGST Act — both provisions are indispensable to resolving whether furnishing returns alone triggers deemed withdrawal or whether correct POS-based IGST computation is a precondition.

grading.reason: The chunk establishes the 30-day deemed withdrawal rule but does not address whether a POS dispute or short-payment of IGST prevents the automatic withdrawal — the question's core legal issue is not resolvable from this chunk alone.

question: Sarvottam Textiles Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 24AADCS5678M1ZP, Gujarat) filed three GST refund applications during FY 2019-20: (i) ₹12 lakh for inverted duty structure in August 2019 — sanctioned for ₹9.5 lakh in October 2019 with ₹2.5 lakh rejected; (ii) ₹7.4 lakh for zero-rated exports in January 2020 — acknowledgement received but still pending as of March 2020, though a provisional refund of ₹5.55 lakh (75%) was credited; and (iii) ₹4 lakh for deemed exports in March 2020 — acknowledgement received, fully pending. Simultaneously, the jurisdictional officer issued a demand order in February 2020 confirming ₹6.8 lakh of CGST+SGST demand on alleged wrongful ITC, of which Sarvottam paid ₹2 lakh under protest. When filling Tables 15A through 15G in the GSTR-9 annual return for FY 2019-20, what exact aggregate figures should Sarvottam declare for refund claimed, refund sanctioned, refund pending, and confirmed demand/tax paid — and does the provisional refund of ₹5.55 lakh reduce the 'pending' figure? Also, can Sarvottam simply skip Part VI entirely given the relaxation available for that financial year?
why_this_chunk: The chunk's instructions for Tables 15A-15G define how to compute aggregate refund claimed, sanctioned, rejected, and pending (explicitly stating pending excludes provisional refunds received), and Tables 15E-15G for confirmed demands and taxes paid. The chunk also specifies the optional-fill relaxation inserted by Notification 56/2019 and extended to FY 2019-20 by Notification 79/2020, which is needed to answer whether Part VI can be skipped.

grading.reason: The question is hyper-constructed with precise figures and a multi-part scenario that no real practitioner would ask in a single question; a CA would ask 2-3 focused questions rather than this omnibus exam-problem format.

question: Kaveri Engineering Pvt Ltd, a company with provisional registration in Bengaluru, Karnataka (GSTIN 29CCC…), was issued a cancellation order with a demand for ₹3.8 lakhs covering CGST and IGST on supplies made to three parties: (i) a bill-to-ship-to transaction where the buyer is in Tamil Nadu but goods were shipped to an SEZ unit in Kochi, Kerala, (ii) a direct supply to a government department in Karnataka, and (iii) export supplies under LUT to a buyer in Singapore. Kaveri's CA wants to file all overdue returns within the 30-day window to get the order deemed withdrawn, but is uncertain whether the demand table in the notice — which the officer filled only with CGST figures and left the IGST and tax-rate columns blank citing 'not mandatory' — can later be used to raise a fresh IGST demand for the SEZ and export supplies after the deemed withdrawal takes effect. Can the officer reopen the IGST demand post-withdrawal, and does the 'not mandatory' nature of certain columns in the notice form prejudice or protect Kaveri?
why_this_chunk: The chunk's notes that 'tax rate, turnover and tax period are not mandatory' columns and that 'POS details shall be required only if demand is created under IGST Act' are directly at issue — the question turns on whether leaving IGST/POS fields blank in the prescribed form bars the officer from subsequently raising an IGST demand after the deemed withdrawal under the 30-day cure provision.

grading.reason: The chunk directly addresses the 'not mandatory' columns and the 30-day deemed withdrawal mechanism, making it answerable, though the IGST reopening question requires inference from the POS/IGST note in the chunk.

question: Durgapur Alloys & Metals Pvt Ltd, a manufacturer in Odisha, procures aluminium ingots and caustic soda as common inputs for producing both aluminium wire rods (dutiable) and aluminium dross (exempted). They have been maintaining a single consolidated RG-23A Part II register for all these inputs. The company also imports some raw materials under the VABAL scheme (Notification 203/92-Customs) and avails MODVAT credit on those imported inputs. Their internal auditor has flagged that consolidating the register for VABAL-imported inputs alongside domestically-procured inputs may not be permissible, especially after the NALCO ruling by the Orissa High Court. The company wants to know: can they continue with a single consolidated RG-23A Part II for all inputs — both domestic and VABAL-imported — or must they segregate the VABAL inputs into a separate register and reverse credit before each clearance? What monthly compliance obligations apply if consolidation is partially allowed?
why_this_chunk: The chunk specifically carves out an exception to consolidated RG-23A Part II maintenance where reversal of MODVAT credit is required prior to clearance under VABAL Notification 203/92-Customs, citing the NALCO case principle from Orissa High Court — this is the precise legal basis needed to answer whether VABAL inputs must be segregated.

grading.reason: All scores are 1; complexity is high because the question requires connecting VABAL scheme conditions, the NALCO ruling, consolidated register permissibility, and monthly compliance obligations — all of which are directly addressed in this specific circular.

question: Shivam Chemicals LLP in West Bengal manufactures three final products: Product A (dutiable at 16%), Product B (fully exempt under a specific Central Excise notification), and Product C (cleared for export under bond). All three products use a common set of five inputs on which the LLP avails MODVAT credit. Some of these common inputs were imported duty-free under the VABAL scheme (Notification 203/92-Customs) with the condition that credit must be reversed before clearance. The LLP's consultant advises maintaining one consolidated RG-23A Part II register and submitting a single monthly statement covering input usage, credit availment, and reversals across all three product lines. However, the jurisdictional Superintendent insists that at minimum the VABAL-imported inputs require separate tracking and pre-clearance reversal, and that the monthly statement must separately break down quantities attributable to the exempted Product B. Is the Superintendent's position legally correct, and what is the LLP's obligation regarding consolidated vs. segregated register maintenance given these mixed clearance scenarios?
why_this_chunk: The chunk establishes the general permission for consolidated RG-23A Part II with monthly statements detailing input usage and credit reversal particulars, while specifically excepting VABAL Notification 203/92-Customs cases requiring pre-clearance reversal per the NALCO principle — both the general rule and the exception are indispensable to resolving this mixed-clearance scenario.

grading.reason: All dimensions score well; the chunk directly addresses consolidated vs. segregated register maintenance and the VABAL exception, making the Superintendent's position verifiable from this chunk alone.

question: TransQuick Logistics Pvt Ltd, a GTA registered in Karnataka (GSTIN 29AABCT5678N1Z2), had been operating under the reverse charge mechanism throughout FY 2022-23. For FY 2023-24, the company's board decided on 20th May 2023 to switch to forward charge and pay GST itself at 12%. The accounts team filed the Annexure V declaration on 29th May 2023. However, between 1st April and 28th May 2023, TransQuick had already rendered inter-state GTA services worth ₹48 lakhs to three recipients across Tamil Nadu and Telangana, on which the recipients had discharged GST under reverse charge. Now the company wants to issue credit notes for the reverse charge component and re-invoice those services under forward charge for the entire financial year. Their CA is asking: (a) was the 29th May declaration within the permissible window for an existing GTA for FY 2023-24, and (b) can the forward charge option operate retrospectively from 1st April 2023 to cover services already supplied under reverse charge before the declaration date?
why_this_chunk: The third proviso inserted by this notification sets the deadline for existing GTAs to exercise the forward charge option for FY 2023-24 as 31st May 2023. The 29 May filing is within that window, but the chunk is silent on retrospective application, making it the indispensable anchor for both the validity question and the interpretive question of whether the option covers services supplied before the declaration date.

grading.reason: The chunk directly establishes the 31st May 2023 deadline for FY 2023-24 option exercise, answering part (a); however, it does not explicitly address retrospective applicability for part (b), making that sub-question only partially answerable from this chunk alone, though a practitioner could infer prospective-only operation from the notification's silence on retrospectivity.

question: Himalayan Conductors LLP, based in Lucknow, has been importing insulated copper cables (classified under heading 85.44 of the Customs Tariff Act, 1975) from a Kathmandu-based supplier under the India-Nepal trade concession route. In Q3 2002, they imported 180 MT and claimed full exemption from basic customs duty. Now in Q4 2002, they have a fresh order for 95 MT of the same cables plus 40 MT of copper tubes falling under Chapter 74. Their customs broker says the exemption still applies because the goods originate in Nepal and are covered under the bilateral trade notification. However, the Deputy Commissioner at ICD Lucknow has issued a query asking them to demonstrate compliance with quantitative ceilings. Is the customs broker's advice correct, and what is the firm's exposure if cumulative imports of copper products from Nepal across the fiscal year have already crossed the quantity thresholds prescribed in the relevant annexure?
why_this_chunk: This chunk specifically carves out copper products under Chapter 74 and heading 85.44 from the Nepal trade exemption (Notification 105/99) when imported beyond quantities specified in Condition No. 3 of Annexure to Notification 40/2002-Customs — the exact legal basis the Deputy Commissioner would rely on to deny the exemption.

grading.reason: All scores are 1; complexity is high because the question requires cross-referencing two notifications, applying quantitative ceiling conditions, and assessing cumulative fiscal-year exposure across two product categories.

question: Bharat Freight Carriers LLP, a goods transport agency based in Pune, Maharashtra, applied for GST registration on 12th April 2023 after crossing the threshold turnover during FY 2023-24. They received their registration certificate on 28th April 2023. The partners want to charge GST on their GTA services under forward charge instead of the default reverse charge mechanism applicable to their recipients (all registered factories). The LLP filed its Annexure V declaration on 10th June 2023. One of their clients, Steelmax Industries Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 27AABCS1234M1Z5) in Nagpur, is now contesting an invoice dated 15th June 2023 where Bharat Freight charged 12% IGST on an inter-state consignment to Raipur, arguing the GTA had no authority to collect GST under forward charge since the option window had lapsed. Is the Annexure V declaration by Bharat Freight valid, and was the LLP entitled to charge IGST on the Steelmax consignment?
why_this_chunk: The fourth proviso inserted by this notification specifically allows a GTA that crosses the registration threshold during a financial year to exercise the forward charge option within 45 days from the date of applying for registration or 1 month from the date of obtaining registration, whichever is later. Here, 45 days from 12 Apr = 27 May, and 1 month from 28 Apr = 28 May — the later is 28 May, so the 10 June filing would be beyond the deadline, making this chunk indispensable to determining validity.

grading.reason: The chunk's proviso directly governs validity: deadline is later of 45 days from 12-Apr-2023 (27-May) or 1 month from 28-Apr-2023 (28-May), i.e., 28-May-2023, making the 10-Jun-2023 Annexure V filing late and the forward-charge collection invalid — all derivable only from this specific notification insert.

question: Gurkha Agri Imports, a proprietorship firm in Bihar, imports vanaspati (hydrogenated vegetable oil) and zinc oxide from Nepal for its edible oil refinery and paint manufacturing unit respectively. For the period October–December 2002, they imported 500 MT of vanaspati and 120 MT of zinc oxide, claiming nil duty under the India-Nepal preferential customs exemption notification originally issued in August 1999. The assessing officer has proposed to deny the exemption on both items citing a late-2002 amendment, but the importer argues that the amendment only restricts quantities beyond a ceiling and their imports fall within the ceiling specified in a separate April 2002 notification. If the quantity ceiling in Condition No. 3 of the Annexure to Notification 40/2002-Customs for vanaspati is 400 MT and for zinc oxide is 100 MT per the relevant period, what is the correct duty treatment for the entire consignment, and can the importer claim proportional exemption for the within-ceiling quantity while paying duty only on the excess?

question: Our client, Meridian Capital Advisors LLP (a Category II AIF registered with SEBI, based in Mumbai), manages a specified fund that holds units on behalf of several non-resident investors with no permanent establishment in India. question: Raghav Woollens Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 03AABCR1234F1Z5, registered in Ludhiana, Punjab) imports yarn of combed wool containing 90% wool by weight from an Indonesian mill. question: Our client, Meridian Textiles LLP based in Surat, Gujarat, has imported 12 metric tons of combed Kashmir (cashmere) goat hair from a supplier in Vietnam. question: Vanguard India Securities Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 27AABCV1234R1ZP, Maharashtra) acts as a sub-advisor to a foreign specified fund registered under IFSCA regulations. question: Meridian Shipping Lines Pvt Ltd acts as the shipping agent for an LCL container arriving at Nhava Sheva from Colombo, destined for onward transshipment to Jebel Ali. Meanwhile, Meridian's operations manager argues that since the cargo is foreign-destined LCL and never enters Indian customs territory for consumption, the 100% examination requirement is disproportionate and they should be allowed to reseal and move the container to the CFS for de-stuffing under normal supervision. grading.reason: All scores are 1; the chunk directly addresses seal tampering protocol (reseal with customs seal, 100% examination, record findings, put up to AC/DC), making the question fully answerable from this chunk alone.

question: Global Freight Consolidators LLP, a Chennai-based NVOCC, consolidates LCL cargo at Chennai port for international transshipment. question: Sunrise Automobiles LLP, a Delhi-based dealer, purchased three duty-free imported vehicles (two Mercedes-Benz sedans and one BMW SUV) from departing diplomatic staff of three different embassies between April and September 2025, all routed through STC. question: Steelcraft Industries Pvt Ltd, a manufacturer in Raipur (Chhattisgarh) with outstanding Central Excise duty arrears of ₹4.12 crore confirmed via OIO in 2019, has just had a CIRP application admitted by NCLT Cuttack bench on 2 April 2026. question: Bharath Yarns LLP, a domestic manufacturer of viscose rayon filament yarn in Nagda, Madhya Pradesh, has filed a representation before DGAD alleging that Chinese exporters are circumventing the existing anti-dumping duty by routing shipments through Vietnam with minimal processing — essentially re-spooling 140-denier viscose rayon filament yarn onto Vietnamese bobbins and obtaining Vietnamese certificates of origin. question: Pranav Logistics LLP, a customs broker in Mumbai, owes ₹1.87 crore in confirmed Customs duty arrears. question: Bharat Defence Ancillaries Pvt Ltd, a unit in Pune registered under Central Excise, manufactures two items exclusively for supply to the Indian Army ordnance depots: (a) a metallic belt made of steel links designed to carry and feed 7.62mm cartridges into a medium machine gun, and (b) a spring-loaded steel clip used by soldiers to quickly load rounds into a rifle magazine. The company has been classifying both items under sub-heading 9305.00 as 'parts and accessories of firearms' and claiming the benefit of a concessional duty notification applicable to goods of Chapter 93. The jurisdictional Central Excise officer has issued a show-cause notice proposing reclassification of both items, demanding differential duty of Rs 18.7 lakhs for the period April 2023 to March 2025. why_this_chunk: The circular directly resolves both classification questions: the metallic belt falls under 9306.00 (not 9305.00) because it carries cartridges rather than being a part of firearms under 93.01–93.04, and the charger/spring clip falls under 7320.00 because Note 1(b) of Chapter 93 excludes 'parts of general use' and Note 2(b) of Section XV defines base metal springs as parts of general use. The 'sole use' argument is specifically addressed and rejected by the circular's reasoning.

grading.reason: All scores are 1; the chunk is a CBIC circular that directly addresses classification of both the metallic belt and the charger/clip, including the 'sole use with weapons' argument, making this an exceptionally well-matched pair.

question: Rakshak Engineering LLP, based in Jabalpur (Madhya Pradesh), supplies linked metallic ammunition belts to Ordnance Factory Tiruchirappalli under a long-term rate contract. grading.reason: All three classification arguments (9305, 9306, 7320) raised in the question are directly and authoritatively resolved by this specific circular.

question: Adani Wilmar Ltd (GSTIN 24AAACA1136H1ZF, Gujarat) had a CIF contract for 5,000 MT crude palm oil with a Malaysian supplier, with the Bill of Lading dated 9-Aug-2017 but the vessel arriving at Mundra Port and the Bill of Entry filed only on 14-Aug-2017. question: Silverline Crafts Pvt Ltd, a registered manufacturer of silver articles in Jaipur with multiple shop floors, maintains a single combined register for all acquisitions and disposals of specified goods across floors. grading.reason: All issues are directly grounded in the notification text: the (a)/(b) structure, the 'works contract' framing, and the residential-vs-commercial scope — though the chunk is silent on apportionment methodology for the partial-eligibility scenario, a practitioner could still give a defensible position on contentions (i) and (ii) from this text alone.

question: Meridian Exports LLP (GSTIN 27AABFM1234C1Z5, Maharashtra) exported custom-fabricated industrial valves to a buyer in Germany in March 2024 under a Letter of Undertaking, claiming IGST refund of ₹38.6 lakhs on accumulated ITC. The GST officer is now issuing a notice demanding recovery of the entire sanctioned refund with interest. Meridian's CFO argues that the money did eventually arrive and no loss occurred to the exchequer. (a) What specific undertaking was Meridian required to furnish at the time of filing the refund application that now becomes the operative legal instrument for recovery? (b) Does late realization of foreign exchange automatically trigger recovery, or is there a curative mechanism? grading.reason: The chunk contains the exact undertaking text referencing section 16 IGST Act and rule 96B CGST Rules that directly answers part (a), and implicitly supports parts (b) and (c) through the legal cross-references embedded in the undertaking language.

question: Pinnacle Pharma Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 29AADCP9876B1ZQ, Karnataka) filed a refund claim of ₹52 lakhs under the 'inverted duty structure' category for the quarter April–June 2024, attaching the self-declaration that the incidence of tax has not been passed on to any other person. Post-sanction, a GST audit team finds that Pinnacle had raised credit notes to its domestic distributors adjusting prices downward for the same period, and argues this proves the tax incidence was in fact passed on, warranting full recovery. Pinnacle counters that credit notes relate to commercial discounts unrelated to tax incidence. Simultaneously, the audit team questions whether Pinnacle even needed to furnish this declaration at all given the nature of its refund category. (a) Under exactly which rule and sub-clause is the self-declaration mandated, and which refund categories are explicitly exempt from furnishing it? (b) Does the inverted duty structure refund fall within the exempt categories listed in section 54(8)? (c) How should Pinnacle's advocate argue that commercial credit notes do not constitute 'passing on' of tax incidence for the purpose of this declaration, and what is the evidentiary standard the department must meet to rebut a validly furnished self-declaration?
