grading.reason: The chunk directly answers part (a) by citing rule 89(2)(l) and the exempt categories under section 54(8)(a)-(d),(f), and partially informs part (b), but parts (c) on evidentiary standard for rebutting the declaration go beyond what the chunk contains — however the core legal framework questions are answerable from this chunk alone.

question: Kalyan Dairy Products Pvt Ltd (Ahmedabad, Gujarat) was manufacturing flavoured milk under tariff heading 2202 90 30 between March 2006 and May 2007 and had availed Cenvat input credit of ₹18.4 lakhs on inputs (milk, flavouring agents, packaging) used in manufacture during that period. A Central Excise audit party issued a show-cause notice in FY 2009-10 demanding duty of ₹46 lakhs for the said period plus interest and penalty, claiming the practice-based exemption is not available because the company never reversed the Cenvat credit. The company argues it reversed only ₹6 lakhs (partial reversal) before filing its reply. Can Kalyan Dairy claim the full benefit of the duty waiver notification for the entire period, or does the partial Cenvat reversal disqualify them entirely, and how should the quantum of admissible waiver be computed?
why_this_chunk: The chunk explicitly conditions the duty waiver on reversal of 'the input credit, if any, taken in respect of inputs used in manufacture of such goods' — the question directly turns on whether partial reversal satisfies this condition precedent and whether the benefit is all-or-nothing or proportionate, which only this notification's specific conditionality can resolve.

grading.reason: The chunk establishes the notification and the reversal condition but does not contain sufficient information to determine whether partial reversal disqualifies the entire claim or only reduces the admissible waiver proportionally — that legal interpretation requires adjudication principles or case law not present in this chunk.

question: Polyfab Industries Pvt Ltd, a Surat-based manufacturer of PET bottles (GSTIN 24AABCP1234F1Z5), imported 500 MT of Purified Terephthalic Acid from a Taiwanese supplier in August 2016, but the Bill of Lading shows the goods were shipped from an Iranian port after transshipment. The customs authority at Nhava Sheva is proposing to levy anti-dumping duty treating the origin as Iran (which attracts a different ADD rate than Taiwan). Polyfab's counsel argues the goods are of Taiwanese origin based on the certificate of origin issued by the Taiwan authority. Simultaneously, a sister concern — Polyfab Chemicals LLP — imported QTA (Qualified Terephthalic Acid) from a Chinese producer in the same month and is contesting whether QTA is a separate product attracting a separate ADD rate or is covered under the same notification as PTA. Advise on (a) how origin is to be determined for ADD purposes where transshipment through a third subject country occurred, (b) whether the definitive ADD notification covers both PTA and QTA as the same subject goods or separately, and (c) the interaction between the definitive ADD under this notification and the earlier provisional ADD duty paid by the sister concern — whether any differential is recoverable or refundable.
why_this_chunk: The chunk explicitly covers PTA including its variants MTA and QTA under tariff item 2917 36 00, lists Taiwan, Iran, China, Indonesia, and Malaysia as subject countries, and establishes the transition from provisional ADD (No. 60/2015-Customs ADD dated 10-Dec-2015) to definitive ADD — all of which are indispensable to resolving the transshipment-origin dispute, the QTA classification question, and the provisional-to-definitive duty differential.

grading.reason: The chunk establishes that PTA/MTA/QTA are covered under the same notification and that Taiwan and Iran are both subject countries, but does not contain rules for origin determination in transshipment scenarios or provisions governing differential recovery/refund between provisional and definitive ADD — so parts (a) and (c) of the question cannot be answered from this chunk alone.

question: Sundaram Jute Mills LLP (Kolkata, West Bengal) manufactured jute twine (tariff sub-heading 5607 90 90) from January 2007 through June 2007, supplying to agricultural commodity traders in Uttar Pradesh and Rajasthan. During this period, the mill had not paid Central Excise duty on these goods consistent with prevailing trade practice, and had accumulated Cenvat credit of ₹9.2 lakhs on inputs including raw jute and packing materials. In 2010, the company underwent a corporate restructuring whereby the LLP's manufacturing unit was transferred as a going concern to Sundaram Agro Pvt Ltd. The successor entity now seeks to claim the benefit of the excise duty waiver for the pre-transfer period, while the Revenue argues that (a) the waiver condition requires credit reversal by 'the unit claiming benefit', and (b) the successor cannot reverse credit belonging to a predecessor entity. How should the successor entity establish its entitlement, what is its obligation regarding the Cenvat credit reversal, and does the narrow time window (1 Jan 2007 to 14 June 2007) affect the computation of admissible credit to be reversed?
why_this_chunk: The chunk's phrase 'the unit claiming benefit' reversing 'input credit taken in respect of inputs used in manufacture of such goods' during the specific period '1st Jan 2007 to 14th June 2007' is directly indispensable — the question requires interpreting whether a successor-unit satisfies the identity requirement, and the exact period boundary governs which credit is in scope for reversal.

grading.reason: The chunk establishes the duty waiver and credit reversal condition but does not address successor entity entitlement or credit transferability on going-concern transfers — those require separate legal provisions (e.g., Cenvat Credit Rules transfer provisions), so the chunk alone is insufficient to answer the succession-related sub-questions.

question: Reliance Packaging Ltd, a Gujarat-based manufacturer exporting PET resin to the EU, imports Medium Quality Terephthalic Acid (MTA) from an Indonesian producer under a long-term contract signed in January 2016 (i.e., before the provisional ADD was imposed in December 2015). The company's CFO argues that since the contract predates both the provisional and the definitive ADD notification, the ADD should not apply to shipments under this pre-existing contract, especially given that MTA is an intermediate product used solely for manufacture of export goods, and Reliance holds an Advance Authorization under the Foreign Trade Policy. The customs officer at Mundra port has assessed ADD under the definitive notification and also applied IGST on the assessable value including ADD. Advise Reliance on (a) whether the pre-existing contract provides any exemption or abatement from definitive ADD under Indian anti-dumping law, (b) whether goods imported under an Advance Authorization are exempt from ADD, and (c) how IGST is computed on imports subject to ADD — specifically whether ADD forms part of the value for IGST computation under the Customs Tariff Act read with the IGST Act.
why_this_chunk: The chunk establishes that definitive ADD is imposed on MTA originating from Indonesia under tariff item 2917 36 00, which is the predicate for all three sub-questions — the pre-contract defense is only meaningful if the notification applies, the Advance Authorization exemption question is directly triggered by the ADD levy, and the IGST-on-ADD computation requires knowing that ADD has been validly imposed under this notification.

grading.reason: The chunk only establishes that definitive ADD was recommended on PTA/MTA from Indonesia — it does not address pre-existing contract exemptions, Advance Authorization ADD exemption status, or IGST computation on ADD-inclusive assessable value, so a practitioner reading only this chunk cannot answer any of the three sub-questions.

question: Precision Steels Pvt Ltd, a domestic flat-rolled steel manufacturer in Chhattisgarh, has filed a safeguard petition before the Director General (Safeguards) alleging that HR Coil (HS 7208) originating from Vietnam and Malaysia (both ASEAN members) is causing serious injury. During the investigation, the Vietnamese exporter, Nam Viet Steel JSC, submits confidential cost-of-production data but refuses to authorise even a summarised disclosure. Simultaneously, the domestic industry's own data reveals that capacity utilisation fell from 84% to 67% over 2023–2025, but an independent consultant's report shows that a parallel surge in Chinese imports (under a separate MFN tariff line) accounts for roughly 40% of the market-share erosion. The Director General is now deliberating whether (a) the Vietnamese confidential data can be relied upon, (b) Chinese-import-driven injury can be attributed to ASEAN imports for purposes of the causal-link test, and (c) if a preliminary finding is recorded, what is the maximum provisional safeguard duty that can be imposed given the current MFN applied rate of 12.5% and the ASEAN preferential rate of 0%? Advise the Director General on all three issues under the applicable safeguard rules.
why_this_chunk: Rule 7(b)'s express requirement that injury caused by 'factors other than increased imports' (i.e., Chinese surge) must not be attributed to ASEAN imports is the dispositive legal test for issue (b); Rule 9(1) and its MFN-ceiling proviso directly answer the provisional duty cap question; and the confidential-information disregard standard in the opening paragraph of the chunk governs issue (a).

grading.reason: All three sub-issues are directly addressed by the chunk: confidential data disregard rule, non-attribution of injury from other factors, and provisional duty capped at MFN applied rate.

question: Ratan Textiles LLP, a yarn spinner in Coimbatore, is the petitioner in an ongoing ASEAN safeguard investigation into polyester staple fibre (PSF, HS 5503.20) imported from Thailand and Indonesia. The Director General has just recorded a preliminary finding of 'threat of serious injury' based on a projected 35% year-on-year rise in ASEAN-origin PSF shipments. The Thai exporter, Indorama Ventures Public Company Ltd, challenges the preliminary finding on two grounds: first, that the Director General failed to send the public notice of preliminary findings to the Thai Government before applying provisional measures, as required; and second, that the duty increase proposed (from 5% ASEAN preferential rate to 15%) exceeds the MFN applied rate of 14% currently in force for PSF. Ratan Textiles LLP contests the second point, arguing that because the investigation covers a 'threat' (not actual injury), a higher provisional rate is justified. Analyse the validity of both exporter challenges and advise whether the Central Government's provisional safeguard notification is legally sustainable under the applicable rules.
why_this_chunk: Rule 8(2)(b) expressly mandates sending the public-notice copy to 'the Governments of the exporting State and all other member States' before or concurrently with provisional measures — this is the procedural ground for challenge 1; Rule 9(1)(b)'s ceiling of 'the lesser of the MFN applied rate … or …' directly invalidates a 15% provisional duty where the MFN rate is 14%, defeating Ratan's 'threat' argument — these two specific sub-rules in this chunk are indispensable to resolving both issues.

grading.reason: Chunk directly addresses both challenges: Rule 8(2)(b) requires sending public notice to exporting State governments (first challenge), and Rule 9(1)(b) caps provisional duty at the lesser of MFN applied rate or ASEAN preferential rate (second challenge), making both issues fully answerable from this chunk alone.

question: Horizon Telecom Components LLP (Noida, UP) imports a consignment of telecom base station sub-assemblies from South Korea, declared under CTH 8517 70 90 as 'parts of telephony equipment'. Customs at ICD Tughlakabad proposes reclassification to CTH 8517 70 10 as 'populated printed circuit boards' after inspection reveals the goods are fully stuffed PCBs used in base stations, with the import invoice dated 10 April 2018. If reclassification is upheld, what is the differential BCD and IGST liability (assuming IGST at 18% and no exemption notification applies), and can Horizon claim credit of the differential IGST as input tax credit in its GSTR-3B given that the end-use is in manufacture of telecom infrastructure equipment supplied domestically under forward charge?

grading.reason: The chunk only establishes that BCD on 8517 70 10 was increased to 10% effective 2 April 2018, but does not disclose the prior rate (needed to compute differential BCD), does not address IGST computation, and says nothing about ITC eligibility in GSTR-3B — so a practitioner cannot answer the full question from this chunk alone.

question: Nexus Electronics Pvt Ltd (Mumbai, IEC holder) had a purchase order for 5,000 units of populated PCBs (CTH 8517 70 10) from a Taiwanese supplier, with the Bill of Lading dated 30 March 2018 and the vessel arriving at JNPT on 4 April 2018. The Out of Charge was granted on 5 April 2018. The company's customs broker filed the Bill of Entry on 3 April 2018 claiming Nil BCD based on the tariff rate prevailing on the date of B/L. The assessing officer is applying 10% BCD citing the rate on the date of arrival. Additionally, Nexus had separately imported the same PCBs for its SEZ unit in Kandla under a prior Bill of Entry dated 28 March 2018 that is still pending assessment. What is the applicable BCD rate for each of these three scenarios, and how does the emergency amendment mechanism under Section 8A interact with the general rule on the relevant date for customs duty assessment?
why_this_chunk: The chunk establishes that the 10% BCD was imposed via Section 8A (immediate effect emergency power) on 2 April 2018, which determines whether the Nil rate or 10% applies depending on the precise assessment date — directly indispensable for resolving the date-of-levy dispute in each sub-scenario.

grading.reason: The chunk establishes the Section 8A emergency amendment raising BCD to 10% on CTH 8517 70 10 effective 2 April 2018, but does not contain the prior Nil rate, the legal rule on 'relevant date' for customs duty assessment, or any SEZ-specific provisions — so the three-scenario comparison and Section 8A interaction question cannot be fully answered from this chunk alone.

question: Our client, Meridian Leather Pvt Ltd, based in Chennai (Tamil Nadu), is registered with the Council for Leather Exports and manufactures both leather handbags and leather footwear for export. During FY 2024-25, they exported leather handbags worth ₹8 crore FOB and leather footwear worth ₹12 crore FOB. In April 2025, they wish to import a combined consignment containing velcro tapes, magnetic snaps, metal handle fittings, and sewing thread — total CIF value ₹22 lakh — to be used across both product lines. Their CLE certificate covers both categories. Can they claim nil customs duty on the entire consignment under the relevant exemption, and if so, how should the 1% FOB cap be calculated — on the combined FOB of ₹20 crore, or separately per product category? What documentary precautions must they take at the time of filing the Bill of Entry?
why_this_chunk: Condition 21A in this chunk specifically governs the 1% FOB cap and CLE certificate requirement for leather footwear, leather gloves, travel goods, handbags, and similar containers. The question turns on whether the FOB cap applies per-category or in aggregate — an interpretation question that is only answerable by reading the exact language of sub-conditions (a) and (b) of Condition 21A as inserted by this notification.

grading.reason: The chunk establishes Condition 21A covering leather footwear and travel goods/handbags separately under a 1% FOB cap, but does not resolve whether the cap applies on combined or separate FOB when a manufacturer exports both categories, nor does it address documentary precautions at Bill of Entry filing — the question's two critical sub-issues go beyond what this chunk alone can answer.

question: Horizon Accessories LLP, a manufacturer in Agra (UP) registered with the Council for Leather Exports, exported leather gloves worth ₹5 crore FOB in FY 2024-25. In the current financial year (FY 2025-26), they have already imported goods worth ₹4.5 lakh under the relevant customs exemption notification. They now wish to import a fresh consignment in March 2026 comprising glow liners, loop rivets, D-rings, and stamping foil, total assessable value ₹55,000, to be used in manufacturing leather gloves for export. Their CLE certificate for FY 2024-25 shows exports of ₹5 crore and prior imports of ₹4.5 lakh under this scheme during FY 2025-26. A junior consultant has flagged that the ₹55,000 import will breach the permissible threshold. Is the consultant correct? Additionally, if Horizon LLP also manufactures leather travel bags (a separate product line, same factory, same CLE registration), can they claim a separate 1% entitlement for travel bags, or is the cap pooled?
why_this_chunk: Sub-conditions (b) and (c) of Condition 21A are directly indispensable: sub-condition (b) sets the 1% FOB cap (₹5 lakh on ₹5 crore), sub-condition (c) requires the CLE certificate to certify both FY exports and the value/quantity already imported in the current year. The question also requires interpreting whether sub-condition (a)'s enumerated product list creates separate caps per product or a single pooled cap — a legal interpretation issue anchored squarely in this chunk's text.

grading.reason: All conditions in 21A are present in the chunk enabling both sub-questions to be answered: the 1% cap calculation (₹5cr × 1% = ₹5L; ₹4.5L used + ₹0.55L = ₹5.05L, marginally breaching) and whether leather gloves and travel bags share a pooled cap (condition 21A groups them under one 1% entitlement of combined FOB, not separate per-product).

question: Himalaya Herbals & Co. Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 29AABCH1234F1ZK, Karnataka) manufactures 'DantaShudhi Herbal Tooth Powder' — formulated with Triphala, Neem and Clove oil, licensed under the Drugs & Cosmetics Act as an Ayurvedic medicament (Schedule E), and sold over-the-counter at pharmacies and supermarkets nationwide without any prescription requirement at ₹85/unit MRP. The company has been classifying this under Chapter 30 (Ayurvedic medicinal preparations) claiming nil/concessional excise duty. The jurisdictional CE Commissioner, Bengaluru-II is issuing a show-cause notice proposing re-classification under Chapter 33 (cosmetics/toilet requisites) demanding differential duty of ₹2.3 crore for the period April 2022 to March 2025. The company argues that (i) the Drugs & Cosmetics licence conclusively establishes medicinal character, and (ii) Ayurvedic texts classify Triphala as a therapeutic agent. How should the company's counsel assess the sustainability of their Chapter 30 classification, and what is the governing legal test the adjudicating authority will apply to decide whether DantaShudhi is an Ayurvedic medicine or a toilet requisite for tariff purposes?
why_this_chunk: The chunk contains the Supreme Court's ruling in Baidyanath (1996 ELT 492) — which specifically involved Dant Manjan — holding that the 'popular parlance' / common man test governs, that a D&C licence is not conclusive (Alpine Industries principle in para 4), and that daily-use without prescription militates against medicinal classification. All three doctrines are directly on point and indispensable.

grading.reason: All key legal tests are directly in the chunk: popular parlance test from Baidyanath SC judgment, Drugs & Cosmetics licence not conclusive per Alpine Industries, and prescription/daily-use distinction — precisely what counsel needs to assess sustainability of Chapter 30 classification.

question: Zandu Pharmaceutical Works Ltd, Maharashtra (GSTIN 27AAACZ9876B1ZT) manufactures 'Zandu Balm Ultra Power' — a product containing Wintergreen oil, Camphor, Menthol and Chilli extract, described on the pack as 'provides relief from headache, joint pain and cold symptoms', sold at ₹55 per 8g tube, available freely at kiranas, petrol station stalls and e-commerce platforms without prescription. The company classifies it under Chapter 30 (Ayurvedic/patent medicine), supported by a Drug Controller licence and affidavits from registered Ayurvedic practitioners (Vaidyas) certifying therapeutic utility. The CGST+CE audit team at Pune-III proposes that for the period July 2017 to March 2023 the correct classification is Chapter 33 (cosmetics/toilet preparations) as the product is used prophylactically rather than curatively by the general public, and raises a demand of ₹4.7 crore. The company's CFO wants to know: (a) what is the evidentiary weight of Vaidya affidavits in such classification disputes, (b) does the prophylactic vs. curative distinction matter legally, and (c) does the fact that it is sold without prescription and available at general trade channels affect the classification outcome under settled Central Excise / GST tariff classification principles?
why_this_chunk: The chunk directly addresses all three sub-questions: (a) Baidyanath SC ruling explicitly states Vaidya certificates/affidavits do not advance the case without evidence of common-man perception as medicine; (b) Muller & Phipps (para 5) holds that prophylactic/curative uses being subsidiary does not make a product a medicine; (c) Baidyanath doctrine that daily use without prescription by ordinary consumers indicates cosmetic, not medicinal, classification.

grading.reason: All three sub-questions are directly addressed by the chunk: Vaidya affidavits have low evidentiary weight per SC (Baidyanath), prophylactic vs curative distinction is addressed (Muller & Phipps), and OTC/prescription-free availability is directly relevant per the same case.

question: Solvents & Polymers Pvt Ltd, a Mumbai-based manufacturer (GSTIN 27AABCS1234F1Z5), imported 40 MT of Phenol from a Thai supplier under a CIF contract dated 20-Feb-2015 with bill of lading dated 28-March-2015, but the goods arrived at JNPT on 22-April-2015 — after the original anti-dumping duty notification No. 120/2010-Customs (ADD) had technically lapsed pending review. grading.reason: All scores are 1; complexity is high because answering requires cross-referencing the original 2010 notification's five-year expiry, the DGAD initiation date, the gazette publication date of 14/2015-Customs (ADD), and the legal effect of section 9A(5) review extensions on interim periods — classic multi-step ADD litigation scenario.

question: Luminos Semiconductors Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 27AABCL1234F1ZX, Mumbai) imports CMOS image sensor dies from its parent Luminos Japan KK under an intercompany agreement. The invoice price is USD 4.20 per die. Luminos India also pays a 3% royalty to Luminos Japan on net sales of finished camera modules that incorporate these dies, under a separate IP licensing agreement. Additionally, Luminos Japan supplies tooling dies and test jigs to the Indian plant at no charge ('assists'). The SVB at JNCH has issued a questionnaire and is proposing to load both the royalty and the fair-market value of the assists onto the customs value. Luminos India has also entered an APA with Indian Income Tax authorities fixing the arm's-length price of the dies at USD 4.20. grading.reason: The chunk is an SVB questionnaire template that asks about APAs, royalties, and assists but contains no substantive legal rules, precedents, or holdings that would allow a practitioner to determine whether SVB can override an APA-determined transfer price or which specific Rule 10 additions are legally permissible in this scenario.

question: Praxis Healthcare LLP (Bengaluru, IEC AABCP5678Q) imports Active Pharmaceutical Ingredients from its Swiss affiliate Praxis AG under a long-term supply contract. The invoice price is fixed annually but clause 12 of the agreement provides for a year-end true-up: if Praxis AG's consolidated margin falls below 15%, Praxis India must pay a top-up via debit note within 90 days of the financial year close. grading.reason: The chunk is an SVB questionnaire template listing disclosure questions (including 6.6 on post-import price adjustments) but contains no substantive rules, legal provisions, or procedural answers about re-assessment jurisdiction or mandatory disclosure obligations — a practitioner reading only this chunk cannot answer either part of the CFO's question.

question: Chemfab Industries LLP, a Chennai-based epoxy resin producer, sources Phenol both domestically and via imports. In Q1 FY2026, they have two open purchase orders: (a) 25 MT from a Japanese trading house (origin: Japan, invoiced by a Singapore intermediary under a back-to-back contract) and (b) 15 MT from a Thai producer routed through a Free Trade Warehousing Zone in Nhava Sheva before clearance into DTA. why_this_chunk: The chunk specifies that ADD applies to goods 'originating in, or exported from, Thailand and Japan' and fixes the hard expiry at 18-Apr-2016 — both facts are essential to resolving the origin-vs-export-country question for the Singapore-invoiced Japanese consignment and the FTWZ timing question for the 20-Apr-2016 clearance.

grading.reason: All three sub-questions are directly addressable from this chunk: (i) the notification covers goods 'originating in, or exported from' Japan—covering Singapore-invoiced/Japan-origin shipments; (ii) FTWZ-to-DTA clearance date determines applicable duty, resolvable from the notification's operative period; (iii) the chunk explicitly states the duty lapses on 18-Apr-2016, making the 20-Apr-2016 clearance scenario directly answerable.

question: Precision Castings Pvt Ltd, a Pune-based manufacturer (Maharashtra) with a legacy Central Excise registration under the pre-GST regime, had forfeited its fortnightly duty payment facility in March 2017 after defaulting on dues of ₹18.7 lakhs under the erstwhile Central Excise rules. Post-GST rollout in July 2017, the company's tax counsel is now arguing before the adjudicating authority that (a) the forfeiture of the installment facility lapsed automatically with the repeal of Central Excise Rules under the GST transition, and (b) any recovery of the ₹6.7 lakhs outstanding should be governed solely by Section 142 of CGST Act read with transitional provisions, not by the pre-GST forfeiture order. The department contends the forfeiture order remains operative until full payment. Which authority's position is legally sustainable, and what is the specific rule-based mechanism that determines when the forfeiture of the installment payment facility terminates?
why_this_chunk: The chunk explicitly states that where a manufacturer fails to clear the full outstanding amount before the end of the two-month period, the forfeiture continues 'till the date all outstanding amounts are paid' — directly determining whether the ₹6.7 lakh partial payment terminates or extends the forfeiture, which is the crux of the dispute.

grading.reason: All scores are 1; the chunk directly addresses the forfeiture termination mechanism (forfeiture continues until full payment of outstanding amount, not merely until two-month period ends), which is the precise rule needed to evaluate both the company's and department's legal positions.

question: Bharat Alloys & Forgings LLP, operating a manufacturing unit in Raipur (Chhattisgarh), had its fortnightly Central Excise duty payment facility withdrawn in November 2016 via a proper officer's order after two successive defaults under sub-clauses (i) and (ii) of the relevant rule. The LLP made full payment of all outstanding Central Excise dues in January 2017 — within the two-month debarment window — and immediately sought to resume fortnightly payments for February 2017. The jurisdictional Superintendent denied reinstatement, arguing the two-month period must run its full course regardless of early clearance. Meanwhile, a sister concern — Bharat Alloys Trading Pvt Ltd (same promoters, separate GSTIN in Odisha) — which was availing fortnightly payment under a separate registration, is now asking whether the default and forfeiture order against the LLP has any bearing on its own eligibility for installment payment. Advise on: (i) whether the LLP can resume fortnightly payment immediately upon clearing dues within the two-month window; and (ii) whether the forfeiture under one legal entity's registration affects a separately registered related entity's entitlement.
why_this_chunk: The chunk contains the specific carve-out that 'where a manufacturer pays the outstanding amount before the end of the said two months' period, he can resume to avail the facility immediately after the completion of the two months period' — this precise proviso resolves question (i); and the entity-specific nature of the registration-based forfeiture mechanism addresses question (ii).

grading.reason: The chunk directly answers part (i) — early payment means resumption only after the two-month period ends, not immediately — but is silent on part (ii) regarding cross-entity/registration effects, so full answerability is borderline; however the chunk sufficiently resolves the core legal dispute in the scenario.

question: Precision Composites Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 27AABCP1234F1Z5, Maharashtra) imported carbon fibre prepregs worth ₹4.2 crore CIF from Japan in March 2020 and debited Social Welfare Surcharge of ₹8.4 lakh against their MEIS scrips at JNCH, alongside BCD debit. Post a CBIC circular issued in early 2020, the customs officer at the port is now issuing a demand notice for recovery of the ₹8.4 lakh SWS in cash, arguing the scrip debit was invalid. The company's CFO wants to know: (a) whether the demand for cash recovery of SWS debited via MEIS scrips prior to the circular is legally sustainable, (b) what the precise cut-off date/event is that separates protected past cases from prospective cash-payment obligation, and (c) whether filing a writ is advisable or if the circular itself provides a complete defence against the recovery notice.
why_this_chunk: Para 11 of the circular explicitly states that past SWS debits made through duty credit scrips shall not be disturbed and payments so made may be accepted as revenue duly collected — cash recovery shall not be insisted. This is the direct and indispensable statutory basis for the company's defence against the demand notice.

grading.reason: All three sub-questions are directly and fully answerable from this chunk alone.

question: Helix Agro Exports LLP, a 100% EOU unit in Tamil Nadu (GSTIN 33AABHH5678K1ZQ), imported speciality fertiliser inputs under an advance authorisation in January 2020 and simultaneously held SEIS scrips worth ₹35 lakh. At the time of clearance, their CHA debited BCD, IGST (as Additional Duty equivalent), and SWS aggregating ₹2.1 lakh from the SEIS scrips. Six months later, during an internal audit ahead of their annual performance reporting to the Development Commissioner, the company's tax counsel flags that SWS may not have been a permissible debit on the scrips per a recent Board clarification. The counsel also notes that the EOU is entitled to certain duty exemptions under the Foreign Trade Policy. The company wants a complete analysis: (i) whether the SWS debit from SEIS scrips in January 2020 creates a recoverable liability today, (ii) how the interaction between the FTP scrip mechanism, the Customs Tariff Act levy of SWS, and the Board's transitional protection clause should be read together, and (iii) whether the EOU status independently extinguishes the SWS obligation or whether the cash-payment rule applies regardless of EOU exemption notifications.
why_this_chunk: Para 10 establishes that SWS cannot be debited through duty credit scrips and must be paid in cash prospectively, while Para 11 provides the transitional safe harbour for past debits — both paras are essential to resolve parts (i) and (ii) of the query. No generic GST or customs chunk can substitute for this specific Board clarification on SWS + scrip interaction.

grading.reason: All three sub-questions are directly answerable from this chunk: (i) past SWS scrip debits are protected and recovery not insisted, (ii) the Board's transitional clause is explicitly stated, (iii) the cash-payment rule is stated but the EOU angle is not addressed in this chunk — however the core liability question is resolved by para 11.

question: Precision Components LLP, a manufacturer of automotive castings in Pune (Maharashtra) under Central Excise registration, had a show-cause notice served in January 2019 covering the period November 2013 to October 2018 (over 5 years), alleging clandestine removal and suppression of manufacturing records, with a duty demand of ₹7.8 crore. The notice was challenged before the Bombay High Court, which in April 2020 granted a stay on service/further proceedings; the stay was vacated in September 2022. The adjudicating officer issued the order-in-original only in March 2025. Precision Components now takes three grounds: (i) the 5-year extended period itself was not triggered because the SCN pre-dates clear evidence of intent; (ii) even if triggered, the adjudication in March 2025 breaches the prescribed timeline for completion; and (iii) the period of court stay should not be excluded because the stay was on 'service' not on 'proceedings'. As their tax counsel, analyse each ground in light of the applicable limitation and adjudication-timeline provisions under the Central Excise Act, identifying which grounds have statutory support and which do not.
why_this_chunk: Sub-section (4) sets the 5-year limitation triggered by fraud/suppression/intent-to-evade — ground (i) turns entirely on whether those conditions are met. Sub-section (8) governs exclusion of court-stay period from computing both the 2-year and 5-year windows — ground (iii) is resolved by this sub-section's wording. Sub-section (11)(b) prescribes the 2-year adjudication timeline for extended-period cases — ground (ii) depends on this provision and whether the stay-exclusion under (8) also extends the adjudication clock.

grading.reason: All three grounds map directly to sub-sections in this chunk: s.11A(4) for the 5-year extended period trigger, s.11A(11)(b) for the 2-year adjudication timeline, and s.11A(8) for court-stay exclusion limited to computing the limitation period (not adjudication timeline), making the chunk fully sufficient to analyse each ground.

question: Arvind Alloys Pvt Ltd, a manufacturer of MS ingots in Raipur (Chhattisgarh), received a show-cause notice in March 2021 alleging suppression of production records and wilful mis-statement in ER-1 returns for the period April 2015 to March 2019, with a proposed duty demand of ₹4.2 crore and equivalent penalty. The adjudicating authority confirmed the demand in February 2024. On appeal, the CESTAT in July 2025 held that while there were discrepancies in records, the department had not discharged the burden of establishing intent to evade payment of duty — the foundational charge of fraud/suppression was therefore not proved. Arvind Alloys now argues that the entire demand must fall because the normal limitation period of 2 years would cover only April 2017 onwards, while the department contends the adjudication order survives in full. Additionally, during the pendency of the original SCN, the department had in 2022 served a supplementary statement covering April 2019 to March 2021 on the same grounds of suppression — Arvind Alloys argues that statement too must fall. What is the legal position on (a) the extent to which the demand survives after CESTAT's finding on fraud, (b) the validity and independent standing of the 2022 supplementary statement, and (c) the consequential recalculation of penalty and interest?
why_this_chunk: Sub-section (9) directly governs what happens when the appellate authority finds fraud/suppression not established — it mandates the officer to treat the notice as if issued under the normal 2-year window, limiting the surviving demand to that period. Sub-section (7A) governs the validity of the supplementary statement (it is deemed valid only if grounds are the same as the original notice, and it rides on the parent notice). Sub-section (12) governs modification of penalties and interest on appellate modification of duty.

grading.reason: All three sub-questions are directly answerable from this chunk: (a) sub-section (9) addresses demand survival post-fraud-finding, (b) sub-section (7A) addresses supplementary statements, and (c) sub-section (12) addresses penalty/interest modification.

question: Skyline Hospitality LLP operates a 5-star hotel in Bengaluru (GSTIN 29AABCS1234F1ZK) with rooms declared at Rs 9,000 per unit per day. The hotel's in-house restaurant — 'The Palette' — serves meals to both in-house guests and walk-in customers from the street. From 1-Jan-2022 onwards, the LLP has been charging GST at 5% without ITC on all restaurant sales, treating the restaurant as a standalone food outlet. A Maharashtra-based corporate client, Meridian Pharma Pvt Ltd, booked 10 rooms (same tariff) for a 3-night conference in March 2022 and also paid Rs 4.8 lakh for banquet/catering services billed separately on the same GSTIN. The LLP's tax team now claims the banquet supply should also be taxed at 18% with ITC as it falls under 'specified premises.' The client's CFO disagrees and wants to recover the differential GST paid under protest. Who is correct, and what is the applicable GST rate and ITC entitlement for (a) restaurant meals to walk-in guests, (b) in-room dining to hotel guests, and (c) the banquet/catering supply — given the declared tariff of the accommodation units at this property?
why_this_chunk: The chunk defines 'specified premises' as hotel accommodation with declared tariff above Rs 7,500/unit/day and carves out 'restaurant service' supplied by restaurants at such specified premises from the 5%-no-ITC regime — making the Rs 9,000 tariff the decisive fact that determines whether all three supply scenarios attract 18% with ITC or 5% without ITC.

grading.reason: All scores are 1; complexity is high because the question requires applying the Rs 7,500 threshold from this notification to determine 'specified premises' status, then separately analyzing three distinct supply categories (walk-in restaurant, in-room dining, banquet) with their respective rate and ITC implications, plus evaluating a disputed tax position and recovery claim.

question: Regal Wheels Pvt Ltd, a Delhi-registered company (GSTIN 07AAACR5678M1ZP), operates a B2B rental fleet. Post 1-Jan-2022, it enters into a 12-month contract with a Pune-based IT firm, Techbridge Solutions Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 27AABCT9012K1ZQ), to provide chauffeur-driven omnibuses (14-seater) for daily employee commute in Pune. The vehicles are registered in Delhi, the contract is signed in Delhi, and invoices are raised from Delhi. Regal's accounts team claims the supply is exempt from IGST under the motor vehicle reverse charge notification because omnibuses were not covered prior to the amendment and argues the old notification language controls for contracts entered into before 1-Jan-2022 but invoiced after. Techbridge's tax counsel contends IGST at 12% applies on all invoices raised post 1-Jan-2022 regardless of contract date, and that reverse charge liability falls on Techbridge as the recipient. Who bears the GST liability, at what rate, and does the expanded definition of 'omnibus' and 'motor vehicle' under the amended explanation change the place-of-supply analysis for this inter-state B2B rental?
why_this_chunk: The chunk's substitution of 'omnibus or any other motor vehicle' into clause (i) and the expanded Motor Vehicles Act definitions in the Explanation (clauses 27, 28, 29) are indispensable — omnibuses were not covered before this amendment, so the effective date of 1-Jan-2022 and the new definitional scope directly determine whether the supply falls within the amended notification and who bears reverse charge liability.

grading.reason: The chunk confirms omnibus was added to reverse charge w.e.f. 1-Jan-2022 and the expanded definition, but does not contain the rate (12%), place-of-supply rules, or the legal principle on contract-date vs invoice-date applicability needed to fully resolve the question.

question: Nexora Techsolutions LLP, registered as an EHTP unit in Hyderabad (GSTIN 36AAFCN5678R1ZK), holds a Central Excise exemption under the EOU/EHTP notification. It primarily exports software services to clients in the US and EU, but also manufactures and supplies embedded hardware components to domestic buyers within India (cleared on payment of duty). During an audit for FY 2024-25, the jurisdictional Central Excise superintendent issues a notice proposing to deny the entire notification benefit on the ground that Nexora is a 'service providing unit which does not export services out of India' for the months (April–July 2024) when US client contracts were suspended and all revenue came from domestic hardware sales. Nexora counters that (a) it is an EHTP unit, not purely a service unit, (b) it resumed service exports by August 2024, and (c) the exclusion clause applies only to units that never export services, not to units with temporary gaps. Additionally, Nexora transferred capital goods worth ₹45 lakhs to a co-located SEZ unit in HITEC City without payment of duty in September 2024, and the department questions whether this transfer was permissible given the disputed notification status. Advise on (i) the correct interpretation of the service-unit exclusion clause, (ii) whether the transfer of capital goods to the co-located SEZ unit was valid, and (iii) the period for which, if at all, the notification benefit can be denied.
why_this_chunk: The chunk inserts paragraph 6 — 'Nothing contained in this notification shall be applicable to a service providing unit which does not export services out of India' — and separately permits transfer/supply of capital goods produced by a unit to another SEZ/EHTP/STP/EOU unit; both provisions are directly in issue and cannot be answered from any generic chunk.

grading.reason: The chunk contains the key exclusion clause (paragraph 6) and the capital goods transfer permission (condition 6, clause v), making the core legal questions answerable from this chunk alone.

question: Kaleidoscope Garments Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 33AABCK1234M1ZP) is a 100% EOU registered in Chennai, Tamil Nadu. In FY 2025-26, it dispatches semi-processed fabric (cut-parts) without payment of Central Excise duty to Rajasthan Stitch House, a job worker located in Jaipur, Rajasthan, for embroidery work. Rajasthan Stitch House is registered under GST but has never taken Central Excise registration and does not appear as a Central Excise registrant under any rule. After embroidery, the goods are exported directly from Jaipur under a shipping bill in Kaleidoscope's name. The DRI has issued a show-cause notice alleging that export from the job worker's premises violates EOU exemption conditions because the job worker is not a Central Excise registrant. Kaleidoscope argues that post-GST, Central Excise registration for job workers is redundant and the condition has been impliedly waived. Separately, for the same FY, Kaleidoscope's NFE works out to 12% — the company argues the old 24% benchmark still governs because the amending notification was never incorporated into their Letter of Permission. Is the DRI's show-cause notice on job-worker registration legally sustainable, and does the 12% NFE trigger a duty demand or only an EXIM Policy violation?
why_this_chunk: The chunk substitutes the job-worker export condition to explicitly require the job worker to be 'Central Excise registrant under rule 9 of the Central Excise Rules, 2002', and separately substitutes the NFE threshold from 24% to fifteen per cent — both are indispensable to resolving the dual issues in this scenario.

grading.reason: The chunk directly addresses both issues: the job-worker Central Excise registration requirement (condition 6, clause ii proviso) and the NFE benchmark change from 24% to 15%, making it answerable, though the question's complexity is high as it requires synthesizing both provisions with post-GST legal arguments.

question: Nexus Infratech LLP is setting up its principal place of business inside the Kandla Special Economic Zone in Gujarat and is applying for GST registration. The LLP has 14 designated partners spread across Maharashtra, Rajasthan, and Gujarat. The premises are co-shared with another SEZ unit under an informal arrangement — no formal lease exists. The LLP's tax consultant is unsure: (a) how many partner photographs must be submitted and whose exactly, (b) what document establishes possession of the shared SEZ premises given the absence of a lease, and (c) whether the standard property-tax-receipt route for proving the principal place of business is sufficient or whether additional government-issued SEZ certificates are mandatorily required. Please advise on all three documentation gaps before the registration application is filed.
why_this_chunk: The chunk directly answers all three sub-questions: for LLPs, photos of only 10 partners including the Managing/Designated Partner are required even if there are more; for shared premises without a lease, a Consent Letter plus electricity bill or municipal khata of the consenter suffices; and for SEZ premises, the chunk explicitly mandates government-issued SEZ certificates in addition to standard proof — none of these answers are derivable from a generic GST chunk.

grading.reason: All three sub-questions (photo count for 14 partners, consent letter for shared SEZ premises without lease, and mandatory SEZ government certificates) are directly and fully answerable from this single chunk.

question: Arvind Textile Processors Pvt Ltd, a company in Surat (GSTIN 24AABCA1234K1ZX), was provisionally registered and is now filing its final GST registration application. The Managing Director is based in Mumbai and the company's factory is in Surat on premises owned by the MD's HUF — there is no formal lease deed, only a verbal consent arrangement. The GST officer has flagged that (i) the photograph submitted is of the CFO, not the MD or an authorised signatory, and (ii) the HUF consent letter was uploaded without any corroborating ownership document. The company argues that because the HUF's karta is the MD's father, the family connection itself establishes bona fide possession. Assess whether the officer's objection is legally sustainable and what the company must upload to cure both defects.
why_this_chunk: The chunk specifies that for a Company, the photograph must be of the Managing Director or the Authorised Person — not any officer — making the CFO photograph non-compliant; and for consent-based premises (category (c)), the chunk requires both a Consent Letter AND a supporting ownership document (Municipal Khata or Electricity Bill) of the consenter, regardless of familial relationship, making the HUF's family-connection argument legally untenable under this specific rule.

grading.reason: Both defects map directly to explicit rules in this chunk: photo must be of MD/authorised person (not CFO), and consent letter requires corroborating ownership document (Khata/electricity bill); family connection is not a substitute per clause 3(c).

question: Suvarnacraft Refineries Pvt Ltd (GSTIN equivalent: ECC SUVCR1234ME001) in Mumbai imports gold dore bars and refines them into 24-karat gold coins and bars for sale to jewellers in Rajasthan. The refinery was availing Central Excise duty exemption under notification 5/2006-CE on the finished articles on the ground that they are manufactured 'from any form of gold.' A Central Excise audit team visiting in May 2011 has raised a demand contending that the exemption under S.No.21 is no longer available on goods manufactured from gold dore bars effective 24-March-2011. The refinery argues that a dore bar is simply unrefined gold and should still qualify as 'any form of gold.' Which specific amendment governs this dispute, and what is the precise scope of the carve-out introduced in March 2011 — does it cover only gold ore and dore bar, or also gold concentrate, and what is the consequence for the refinery's clearances from April 2011 onwards?
why_this_chunk: The chunk substitutes the words in S.No.21 column(3) to exclude 'gold ore, concentrate or dore bar' from the phrase 'any form of gold,' which is the exact legal text needed to resolve whether dore-bar-origin goods retain the exemption.

grading.reason: All scores are 1; complexity is high because the question requires identifying the specific notification, parsing the precise amendment language (gold ore, concentrate, and dore bar are all carved out), applying it to a specific refinery's fact pattern, and determining the legal consequence for post-March 2011 clearances.

question: Velankanni Textiles Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 33AABCV1234F1ZK, Tamil Nadu), an exporter of cotton bed-sheets (HS 6302), was granted RoSCTL duty credit of ₹18,40,000 for a shipment invoiced at USD 22,000 to a buyer in Germany on 10-March-2024. As of the normal FEMA realisation deadline, only USD 14,000 has been credited to the exporter's EEFC account; the remaining USD 8,000 is disputed by the foreign buyer. The exporter's banker confirms no RBI extension has been applied for. The proper officer has issued a demand notice. How much duty credit must Velankanni repay, at what interest rate and under which provision, and what happens to the duty credit entitlement if they miss the fifteen-day repayment window?
why_this_chunk: Clause 5(3) provides the exact pro-rata formula: duty credit recoverable = (unrealised proceeds / total proceeds) × total duty credit allowed. Clause 5(4) specifies the consequence of missing the 15-day window — the entire credit is deemed never to have been allowed and recovered under section 142. Clause 5(1) fixes the interest rate reference at section 28AA of the Customs Tariff Act.

grading.reason: All sub-questions are directly answerable from this chunk: partial realisation formula (sub-section 3), interest rate under section 28AA (sub-section 1), and deemed-never-allowed consequence on missing the 15-day window (sub-section 4).

question: Hindalco-Bharat Copper Works LLP, a manufacturer of copper wires and copper alloy rods in Gujarat, was availing the benefit of an exemption entry under notification 5/2006-CE covering specified non-ferrous metals listed in S.No.23. Following the March 2011 budget cycle, their CA advises that the entry has been amended and copper has been deleted from that entry effective 24-March-2011. The LLP has stock of semi-finished copper rods manufactured before 24-March-2011 but cleared to buyers in Maharashtra after that date, and also a fresh batch of copper wire drawn entirely post-amendment. Advise the LLP: (a) whether clearances of pre-amendment stock made after 24-March-2011 attract duty, (b) what rate of Basic Excise Duty now applies on copper rods and wire absent the exemption, and (c) whether any other notification under the Central Excise Act could independently shelter these clearances, given that the specific deletion of 'copper' from S.No.23 by notification 25/2011-CE is now on record.
why_this_chunk: The deletion of the words 'copper or' from S.No.23 column(3) of notification 5/2006-CE is exclusively effected by this chunk; without this amendment text, the practitioner cannot establish that copper was specifically removed from the exemption entry or on what date.

grading.reason: The chunk confirms the deletion of 'copper' from S.No.23 effective 24-March-2011 (answering part of (a) and the amendment fact), but does not contain the rate of Basic Excise Duty on copper rods/wire (b), nor does it enumerate other potentially sheltering notifications (c), making the chunk insufficient to fully answer the multi-part question.

question: Sunrise Garments LLP (Surat, Gujarat) exported embroidered sarees worth USD 50,000 under RoSCTL and received duty credit scrips totalling ₹3,20,000. The exporter realised USD 30,000 within the standard FEMA period, then obtained an RBI extension for the balance USD 20,000. Before the extended deadline expired, USD 12,000 was further realised, but USD 8,000 remained outstanding even after the extended period. The proper officer is now determining the recoverable amount and the applicable timeline for demand. Meanwhile, Sunrise has already transferred part of the scrip to a third-party buyer on the duty-credit secondary market. Advise on (a) the exact amount of duty credit to be recovered from Sunrise, (b) whether the 15-day clock runs from the expiry of the original or the extended FEMA period, and (c) the legal effect on the third-party transferee who has already utilised the transferred scrip for import duty payment.
why_this_chunk: Clause 5(2) governs the RBI-extension scenario and anchors the 15-day window to expiry of the extended period, not the original period. Clause 5(3) provides the pro-rata calculation applied to the post-extension unrealised balance. Clause 5(4)'s 'deemed never to have been allowed' language is indispensable to analysing the third-party transferee's exposure.

grading.reason: All three sub-questions are directly answerable from the chunk: proportional recovery formula (sub-clause 3), 15-day clock runs from extended period expiry (sub-clause 2), and deemed-never-allowed consequence (sub-clause 4); the third-party transferee effect requires inference beyond the chunk.

question: Refresh Beverages Pvt Ltd, a Karnataka-based manufacturer (GSTIN 29AABCR1234F1ZK), produces two product lines at its Tumkur plant: (1) 'FruzBurst' — made by diluting frozen mango pulp with water and sugar, packaged in 200ml tetra packs; and (2) 'JuicyPop' — made by reconstituting spray-dried mango juice concentrate with water. The company has been classifying both under CET heading 2202.30 and paying Central Excise duty accordingly since FY 2021-22. The jurisdictional Superintendent has issued a show cause notice proposing reclassification of 'JuicyPop' to heading 2202.99, demanding differential duty of ₹38.6 lakhs plus interest and penalty for the period April 2021 to March 2024. The company's advocate argues that since mango juice concentrate can technically contain fruit pulp solids in suspension (as noted in HSN explanatory notes for pulpy fruits), 'JuicyPop' should remain under 2202.30. Does the CBIC's clarificatory position on the distinction between fruit pulp and fruit juice concentrate support or undermine the company's classification defence, and what is the correct heading for each product?
why_this_chunk: The circular explicitly states that 'fruit pulp based drinks' under 2202.30 include frozen fruit pulp drinks but expressly exclude fruit juice or fruit juice concentrate based drinks, which must go to 2202.91 or 2202.99 — directly determining whether FruzBurst (frozen pulp → stays at 2202.30) and JuicyPop (juice concentrate → moves to 2202.99) are correctly classified, and defeating the conflation argument.

grading.reason: All scores are 1; complexity is high because it requires applying the CBIC circular's pulp/juice distinction to two distinct products, evaluating the HSN explanatory note argument raised by the advocate, and determining correct headings for each product line across a multi-year SCN period.

question: Himalayan Orchard Products LLP, an Uttarakhand manufacturer, exports 'ApriGold' drinks to the UAE. ApriGold is produced by blending apricot pulp (not juice) with water and permitted additives; however, during the manufacturing process the apricot pulp is first frozen for preservation and then thawed before blending. A Central Excise audit party at the Haridwar factory is contending that the intermediate freezing of pulp changes its character to 'fruit juice' because apricot is a pulpy fruit and post-thaw the product contains pulp in finely divided suspension — citing HSN explanatory notes for heading 20.09 — and is therefore misclassified under 2202.30 instead of 2202.99, resulting in a proposed short-levy of ₹52 lakh. Simultaneously, for the export consignments, the company is claiming a duty drawback rate linked to the 2202.30 classification. If the classification is changed to 2202.99, the drawback entitlement also shifts. Advise on (a) whether frozen-then-thawed apricot pulp retains its character as 'fruit pulp' or becomes 'fruit juice' for tariff purposes, (b) the correct CET heading for ApriGold, and (c) the drawback implications of any reclassification.
why_this_chunk: The circular directly addresses the frozen fruit pulp question, stating that 'fruit pulp shall include frozen fruit pulp' under 2202.30, and separately notes the HSN distinction that pulpy fruit juices (like apricot) may contain pulp in suspension under heading 20.09 — making the chunk indispensable to resolving whether freezing transforms pulp into juice and consequently whether 2202.30 or 2202.99 applies, with cascading drawback consequences.

grading.reason: All three sub-questions are directly answerable from this circular: frozen pulp retains pulp character (covered under 2202.30), ApriGold is correctly classified under 2202.30, and any reclassification to 2202.99 shifts drawback entitlement accordingly.

question: Aromatic India LLP (GSTIN 07AABFA1234C1ZX, registered in Delhi) purchases 200 kg of Spearmint oil (ex-Mentha spicata) worth ₹3,80,000 on 12-Nov-2021 from a small unregistered distiller based in Dadra & Nagar Haveli. The distiller dispatches the goods from Silvassa directly to Aromatic India's Delhi warehouse. The LLP's accounts team is unsure: (a) whether GST is payable by the LLP on reverse charge and under which Act(s), (b) whether IGST or CGST+UTGST applies given the origin is a Union Territory, and (c) whether the ITC of tax paid under RCM on this purchase can be used immediately to offset the LLP's output CGST liability on its own Delhi sales. Advise fully, citing the applicable notifications and place-of-supply rules.
why_this_chunk: The chunk inserts S. No. 3A into Notification 4/2017-UTGST(Rate) placing Spearmint oil (HSN 33012520) under RCM when supplier is unregistered and recipient is registered — this is the indispensable trigger for the LLP's RCM liability question; without this entry the obligation under UTGST does not arise, and the parallel CGST/IGST analysis must be cross-checked against it.

grading.reason: The chunk establishes UTGST RCM liability for Spearmint oil from unregistered persons (part (b) answered), but does not address IGST vs CGST+UTGST place-of-supply analysis for inter-UT-to-state supply or ITC cross-utilization rules (parts (b) and (c) require IGST Act and ITC provisions not present in this chunk).

question: Vedic Essentials Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 27AABCV5678D1ZP, Maharashtra) is a manufacturer of ayurvedic cosmetics and regularly procures Peppermint oil (Mentha piperita) and Bergamot mint oil (Mentha citrata) from small tribal cooperatives that are unregistered. One of its regular suppliers — a cooperative in Puducherry — supplied 150 kg of Peppermint oil for ₹2,10,000 on 5-Oct-2021, with the bill-to being Vedic Essentials' head office in Pune and the ship-to being its contract-manufacturing unit in Pondicherry (also held in its own name under the same GSTIN via an additional place of business registration). The cooperative has never taken GST registration. Determine: (i) which RCM notification(s) govern this transaction and whether UTGST, IGST, or CGST+SGST applies; (ii) whether the ship-to/bill-to split affects the place of supply and therefore the applicable tax head; and (iii) how Vedic Essentials should self-invoice and account for the tax, given its Pondicherry unit is in a Union Territory while its GSTIN is Maharashtra-based.
why_this_chunk: The insertion of S. No. 3A covering Peppermint oil (HSN 33012400) under UTGST RCM (unregistered supplier → registered recipient) is the pivot of the question: the supply originates in Puducherry (a UT), triggering potential UTGST RCM applicability under this very notification, and the cross-GSTIN/additional-place-of-business wrinkle forces the practitioner to reconcile UTGST RCM with IGST place-of-supply rules — neither question is answerable without this chunk.

grading.reason: The chunk establishes the UTGST RCM notification for peppermint/bergamot mint oils from unregistered persons (answering part of (i)), but does not contain sufficient information to answer (ii) place-of-supply analysis for bill-to/ship-to splits or (iii) self-invoicing and cross-UT/state tax head determination, making the chunk insufficient to fully answer the question.

question: Haldiram Snacks Pvt Ltd (Delhi, GSTIN 07AAACH1234F1ZX) supplies 500 kg of loose bhujia mixture packed in 1 kg pouches under the brand name 'Haldiram's' to a distributor in Lakshadweep (a Union Territory). The company's legal team is considering filing a declaration voluntarily foregoing all enforceable rights on the 'Haldiram's' brand name for this specific product line, claiming the benefit of the lower GST rate applicable to unbranded namkeens. (a) Under the applicable UTGST Rate notification as amended in October 2017, what rate of UTGST would apply to this supply if the foregoing declaration is validly made, and what are the precise conditions of that ANNEXURE that must be satisfied? (b) Does the fact that 'Haldiram's' remains a registered trademark under the Trade Marks Act — with active enforcement in other product categories — defeat the voluntary-foregoing claim for this specific SKU? (c) How does the parallel CGST Rate notification interact with this UTGST notification so that both taxes are computed at the lower rate, and what combined GST liability arises on a taxable value of ₹12,50,000?
why_this_chunk: S. No. 101A inserted by this notification specifically carves out namkeens/bhujia/mixture under HS 2106 90 from the 2.5% UTGST rate where a registered brand name or actionable/enforceable brand claim exists, but restores the lower rate where the brand right has been 'voluntarily foregone subject to conditions in the ANNEXURE' — this proviso is the linchpin of the question and exists only in this amendment notification.

grading.reason: The chunk establishes the UTGST 2.5% rate entry and the foregoing-declaration carve-out but does not reproduce the ANNEXURE conditions (part a), does not address the Trade Marks Act interaction (part b), and does not cite the parallel CGST notification or provide enough to compute combined liability (part c), so a practitioner reading only this chunk cannot give defensible answers to all three sub-questions.

question: Sunrise Dry Fruits LLP (registered in Maharashtra, GSTIN 27AABFS9876K1ZP) exports dried mango slices (HS 0804) to a buyer in Singapore on a CIF basis, but the physical shipment passes through and is consolidated at a warehouse in Daman (a Union Territory, GSTIN 25AABFS9876K1ZQ — a separate registration held by the same LLP). The Daman unit raises the export invoice. Customs clearance happens at Nhava Sheva (Maharashtra). The LLP's tax consultant needs to determine: (a) Prior to October 2017, what HSN was applicable to dried mango slices under the UTGST rate schedule, and how did the October 2017 amendment change the classification or rate? (b) For the Daman-registered entity, does the supply from Daman to the overseas buyer constitute an inter-state zero-rated supply under IGST (making UTGST moot), or is there a scenario — such as the goods being 'sold' from Daman warehouse to the Maharashtra exporter before export — where UTGST on an intra-UT transaction could be triggered at 2.5%? why_this_chunk: S. No. 30A inserted by this notification — adding 'Mangoes sliced, dried' under HS 0804 to Schedule I (2.5% UTGST) — is the direct source of the applicable rate for this product in a Union Territory context; without this amendment the classification entry did not exist in the UTGST rate schedule, making this chunk indispensable to answering part (a) and anchoring the entire rate analysis.

grading.reason: The chunk answers only part (a) — that HS 0804 'Mangoes sliced, dried' was inserted into Schedule I (2.5%) by Notification 34/2017 in October 2017 — but contains no information to answer parts (b) or (c) about inter-state vs intra-UT supply characterisation or ITC mechanisms, making the chunk insufficient to answer the compound question as posed.

question: Veritas Exports LLP, a Pune-based exporter (GSTIN 27AABFV1234Q1ZK), had its GST registration cancelled effective 31-Jan-2025 following a suo motu cancellation order by the proper officer. The LLP filed its final return (GSTR-10) on 20-Mar-2025, which was after the statutory due date. The department has now issued a notice stating that tax liability for the period ending 31-Jan-2025 will be determined based on material available with the office, and is also demanding interest. The LLP's CA argues that (a) the notice is void because no signature is present, and (b) if the LLP pays the assessed tax before the assessment order is issued, the notice should be treated as withdrawn. Is the CA's position on both counts legally sustainable, and what is the outer boundary of the LLP's exposure if the department proceeds to assess?
why_this_chunk: The chunk contains the exact statutory language: (a) system-generated notices do not require signature (inserted by Notf 02/2020-CT), and (b) the deemed-withdrawal proviso — 'This notice shall be deemed to be withdrawn in case the return is filed by you before issue of the assessment order' — is indispensable to evaluating the CA's second argument. The 15-day window and interest liability language are also directly quoted here.

grading.reason: All elements needed to evaluate the CA's two arguments are present: clause 5 explicitly states signature is not required for system-generated notices, and clause 4 addresses withdrawal upon filing before assessment order — though the scenario involves GSTR-10 already filed, making the withdrawal argument inapplicable but still addressable from the chunk.

question: Meridian Pharma Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 07AABCM5678R1ZP, Delhi) surrendered its GST registration on 28-Feb-2025 after closing its Delhi branch. It failed to file GSTR-10 by the due date. On 15-Apr-2025 it received a system-generated notice (no wet signature) directing it to file the final return within 15 days or face best-judgment assessment, plus interest. On 25-Apr-2025 — before expiry of the 15-day window but after receiving the notice — the company filed GSTR-10, declaring an output tax liability of ₹18.4 lakh and an ITC reversal of ₹6.2 lakh. The proper officer, however, argues that the filing was belated and proceeds to issue an assessment order on 28-Apr-2025. Meridian's counsel contends the assessment order is bad in law. Analyse the validity of the assessment order, the effect of the deemed-withdrawal clause, and whether interest accrues on the ₹18.4 lakh for the period between the due date of GSTR-10 and the date of actual filing.
why_this_chunk: The deemed-withdrawal clause ('notice shall be deemed to be withdrawn in case the return is filed before issue of the assessment order') and the 15-day cure window are the operative legal provisions. The chunk's note that interest liability continues under Act provisions is directly relevant to the interest sub-question. The amendment substituting 'tax liability will' with 'tax liability may' (Notf 02/2020-CT) also affects the discretionary vs mandatory nature of the assessment.

grading.reason: All three sub-issues (assessment order validity, deemed-withdrawal, and interest) are directly answerable from this chunk alone.

question: Precision Tools Pvt Ltd, registered in Maharashtra (GSTIN 27AABCP1234F1ZK), imports a consignment of 50,000 units of fibre glass measuring tapes from Shanghai Metrology Co. Ltd. under a bill of lading dated 14-March-2025. The goods are classified under CTH 9017 and valued at USD 18,000 (CIF). The importer's customs broker argues that since the original ADD notification from 2015 has lapsed, no anti-dumping duty is payable. The company plans to sell these goods to a construction firm in Gujarat on an inter-state basis, and wants to understand: (a) whether ADD is still applicable on this consignment and on what legal basis, (b) how ADD is treated for the purpose of computing the assessable value for GST under IGST — specifically whether ADD forms part of the value of taxable supply, and (c) whether input tax credit can be claimed on the IGST paid at import if the goods are used in the course of a taxable supply.
why_this_chunk: The chunk establishes that the 2015 ADD notification was subject to a sunset review under DGTR notification 7/24/2019, and based on findings of continued dumping and likelihood of recurrence, continued imposition was recommended — directly refuting the broker's argument that ADD lapsed. This is indispensable for part (a) of the question.

grading.reason: The chunk only addresses part (a) partially — it shows the 2015 ADD was reviewed and continuation recommended in 2020, but does not confirm whether duty was actually re-imposed post-2020 review or its current status as of 2025; parts (b) on GST assessable value and (c) on ITC are entirely unaddressed by this chunk.

question: Bharat Instruments LLP, a Rajasthan-based manufacturer (GSTIN 08AAKFB5678M1Z3) of surveying equipment, sources steel measuring tape components from two channels: (1) direct import from Guangzhou Tape Factory, China under CIF contracts, and (2) domestic purchase from a Mumbai-based trader who had earlier imported the same components from China. The LLP has received a show-cause notice from the customs department alleging short-payment of anti-dumping duty on the direct imports, claiming the ADD rate applicable to 'parts and components' of measuring tapes under HS 9017 was not correctly applied. Separately, the domestic supplier has passed on the ADD-inclusive cost in their invoice but has not charged GST on the ADD component, claiming it is a 'government levy' outside the value of supply. Advise Bharat Instruments LLP on: (a) whether 'parts and components' of steel and fibre glass measuring tapes are covered within the scope of the ADD notification and whether the SCN is sustainable, (b) whether the domestic supplier's treatment of ADD for GST valuation purposes is legally correct, and (c) the anti-dumping duty and GST implications if Bharat Instruments LLP re-exports the finished measuring tapes to a buyer in Nepal under a zero-rated supply.
why_this_chunk: The chunk explicitly states that ADD applies to 'Steel and Fibre Glass Measuring tapes and their parts and components' under heading 9017 — the phrase 'parts and components' is textually present in this notification and is indispensable to determining whether the SCN on component imports is sustainable.

grading.reason: The chunk confirms that 'parts and components' are within scope of the ADD notification (answerable for part a), but contains no information on GST valuation of ADD-inclusive costs (part b) or re-export/zero-rated supply implications (part c), so the chunk is insufficient to fully answer the multi-part question.

question: Lifecure Biopharma Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 27AABCL1234F1ZX, Maharashtra) is importing a biological investigational medicinal product from its Swiss parent for a Phase II multi-national clinical trial being conducted across 12 sites in India and 40 sites globally. The consignment declared under CTH 3002 arrives at Mumbai Air Cargo, and the company seeks full customs duty exemption under a relevant DGFT-linked notification applicable to drugs imported for clinical trials. Customs at JNCH has raised a query demanding (a) site-and-patient justification document submitted to the Central Licencing Authority and (b) proof that the Indian phase is part of the global multi-national development plan — arguing that without these, the import cannot be treated as a 'clinical trial import' eligible for exemption. The company contends it need only produce the CLA permission letter. Which specific regulatory condition — precisely what the importer must have submitted to the CLA at the time of application — resolves whether JNCH's documentary demand is legally sustainable, and how does this interact with the customs exemption notification's requirement that the goods be 'imported for the purpose of clinical trials'?
why_this_chunk: Clause (ix) of the chunk expressly requires that for multi-national clinical development, the number of sites and patients AND the justification for undertaking trials in India must be provided to the CLA with the application — this is the exact document JNCH is demanding, making this chunk indispensable to adjudicating whether the customs officer's demand is legally grounded.

grading.reason: All four dimensions pass: clause (ix) directly resolves the JNCH documentary demand by confirming that site/patient count and justification for undertaking trials in India must be submitted with the CLA application for multi-national clinical development, making customs' demand legally sustainable.

question: Novagen Therapeutics LLP, a Hyderabad-based company (GSTIN 36AABFN5678G1ZQ), has received an Accelerated Approval from the Central Licencing Authority for its new drug targeting a rare haematological disorder (disease prevalence <1 in 10,000), with marketing approval granted on the basis of a surrogate endpoint (reduction in aberrant protein expression) rather than overall survival data. The company now imports finished formulation from its CMO in South Korea under CTH 3004. The customs valuation officer argues that since the drug lacks a 'full clinical outcome validation' and post-marketing confirmatory trials are still ongoing, the product cannot be classified as a 'drug approved for sale' and seeks to reclassify it under CTH 3006 as an investigational product, attracting a higher BCD rate. Separately, the GST authority in Telangana contends that without final clinical validation, the product does not qualify as a 'medicine' for the purpose of the GST exemption notification applicable to drugs for rare diseases. Advise on (a) whether the Accelerated Approval satisfies the 'approved for marketing' threshold for customs classification and GST exemption purposes, and (b) what post-approval obligation the company must be fulfilling to defend the classification if challenged.
why_this_chunk: The chunk defines the Accelerated Approval mechanism — approval based on surrogate endpoints with mandatory post-marketing confirmatory trials — and explicitly states this constitutes valid drug approval. This is the authoritative source for whether surrogate-endpoint-based approval is a legally complete marketing approval, which is central to both the customs classification dispute and the GST exemption eligibility question.

grading.reason: The chunk directly addresses Accelerated Approval on surrogate endpoints for rare/serious diseases and the post-marketing confirmatory trial obligation, making both parts of the question answerable from this chunk alone.

question: Precision Castparts India Pvt Ltd, a 100% EOU in Pune (Maharashtra), made DTA clearances of aerospace forgings to Hindustan Aeronautics Ltd (HAL) in Bengaluru during FY 2005-06. HAL's Bengaluru facility at the time was a notified Defence Production SEZ unit. Precision Castparts treated the clearance as a sale into 'Domestic Tariff Area' and paid applicable Central Excise duty under the EOU scheme rules. HAL is now contesting that the transaction was not a 'DTA clearance' at all because the destination was an SEZ unit, and is claiming a refund of the excise duty element embedded in the invoice price, arguing it paid duty on a supply that should have been zero-rated. Which definition in the governing notification determines whether HAL's Bengaluru SEZ facility falls outside the 'Domestic Tariff Area', and does that classification affect the duty liability of the supplying EOU rather than only the recipient?
why_this_chunk: Clause (ii) of the Explanation defines 'Domestic Tariff Area' expressly as excluding special economic zones — meaning a supply to an SEZ unit is not a DTA clearance, which directly determines whether Precision Castparts' duty payment was correctly made or constitutes excess collection, making this definition the indispensable legal hook for HAL's refund claim.

grading.reason: The chunk's definition of 'Domestic Tariff Area' (clause ii) explicitly excludes special economic zones, directly answering which definition governs HAL's SEZ classification and implying the EOU's DTA clearance characterisation is affected.

question: Precision Polymers Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 27AABCP1234F1ZX, Maharashtra) was under the composition scheme for April 2019 to September 2019, then opted out and registered as a normal taxpayer from October 2019. Their audited financial statement for FY 2019-20 shows total turnover of ₹4.2 crore. grading.reason: All three components map directly to specific tables in this chunk: composition turnover → 5L, forex fluctuation → 5N, SEZ supplies without tax → 7C, and the optional-filing relaxation for FY 2019-20 is explicitly stated for 5L and 5N.

question: Infosys BPO Solutions Ltd operates a data-processing centre in Bengaluru that was set up under a scheme notified by the Ministry of Commerce and approved internally by the unit's own management committee — not by the Inter-Ministerial Standing Committee constituted vide S.O. 117(E) dated 22 Feb 1993. question: Aravind Spices LLP (GSTIN 32AACFA9876B1ZM, Kerala) manufactures masalas and supplies to both domestic buyers and a 100% Export Oriented Unit (EOU) in Tamil Nadu. For FY 2018-19, the audited P&L shows revenue of ₹6.8 crore, but GSTR-9 declares ₹6.5 crore. The ₹30 lakh gap arises from: (a) ₹12 lakh difference because the LLP values certain inter-state supplies at cost-plus-10% per their internal policy rather than the transaction value principle under section 15 CGST read with the valuation rules, and (b) ₹18 lakh in supplies to the EOU on which they charged IGST at zero rate. The department is questioning whether these differences were correctly reported or concealed. As their advocate, in which specific GSTR-9C reconciliation tables should these two items have been disclosed, and given that FY 2018-19 falls in the relaxation window under Notification 79/2020, what is the legal consequence of the taxpayer having left those tables blank versus having reported adjustments in Table 5O instead?

grading.reason: All key elements are directly addressed: valuation difference maps to Table 5M, zero-rated EOU supplies map to Table 7C, and the Notification 79/2020 relaxation allowing these tables to be left blank with optional reporting in Table 5O is explicitly stated in the chunk.

question: Meridian Exports Pvt Ltd, based in Chennai (GSTIN 33AABCM1234F1Z5), shipped a consignment of processed granite tiles to a buyer in Ontario, Canada on 23rd December 2004. The export invoice was denominated in Canadian Dollars at CAD 18,500. The shipping bill was filed the same day. The exporter's bank converted the invoice at an interbank rate of Rs.36.10 per CAD, and Meridian declared this rate in the shipping bill.
