question: Shree Prasad Mithai Bhandar, a proprietorship firm in Varanasi (UP) with Central Excise registration, manufactures mishri and battasas (patashas) from raw sugar purchased from a local mill. A show-cause notice has been issued demanding excise duty of ₹18,40,000 for the period April 2005 to March 2007, on the ground that the process of converting sugar into these products constitutes 'manufacture' under the Central Excise Act. The firm's advocate argues that since mishri and battasas are traditional religious/prasadam items, they should either be exempt as prasadam under Chapter 21 or, alternatively, that the process is not 'manufacture' at all because no new distinct article emerges. Given the Tribunal's conflicting decisions — including S M Confectionery (1993) classifying batasha as sugar confectionery under 1704.90, and the Apex Court's ruling in Sakarwala — how should the adjudicating authority rule on (i) whether the process amounts to manufacture, (ii) the correct tariff heading, and (iii) whether the S M Confectionery precedent can be relied upon by the noticee?
why_this_chunk: The chunk directly addresses all three issues: paragraph 12(a) settles that the process amounts to manufacture under Section 2(f); paragraph 12(b) settles the classification as 17019100; and paragraph 9 explicitly declares S M Confectionery per incuriam because it failed to consider Chapter Notes and the Sakarwala Apex Court decision — making the chunk the sole authoritative source for rejecting that precedent.

grading.reason: All three sub-questions are directly answered by the chunk: manufacture under S.2(f) is confirmed (para 12a), classification is 17019100 not 1704.90 (para 11-12b), and S M Confectionery is held per incuriam for failing to consider chapter notes and Sakarwala (para 9).

question: Bharat Fertilizers & Chemicals Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 27AABCB1234F1Z5, Maharashtra) is supplying a disassembled heat-exchanger assembly (CIF value ₹18 crore) and associated commissioning spares worth ₹2.2 crore to a new urea plant being set up by Krishnapatnam Agro Industries Ltd in Andhra Pradesh. The supply is being made through an International Competitive Bidding process, but the bid documents included Indian customs duties in the price build-up because the procurement team believed ICB exemptions only apply to imports. The plant developer is claiming deemed-export benefits and wants to know: (1) whether the spares at ₹2.2 crore qualify for the same duty exemptions as the capital goods, given the FoR value of the capital goods is ₹15.8 crore ex-works, and (2) whether the erroneous inclusion of customs duties in the ICB bid price disqualifies the supply from the exemption altogether. Please advise with reference to the applicable notification conditions.
why_this_chunk: Clause (f) of the notification is directly indispensable: it caps spares eligibility at 10% of Free on Rail value of capital goods (10% of ₹15.8 cr = ₹1.58 cr, meaning ₹2.2 cr spares partially exceed the cap), and it mandates ICB 'without including the duties of customs' — the erroneous inclusion in bid price is a direct clause (f) compliance failure.

grading.reason: The chunk directly addresses both sub-questions: clause (f) specifies the 10% FoR cap for spares in fertilizer plant ICB supplies, and the 'without including the duties of customs' condition speaks to whether erroneous inclusion disqualifies the exemption.

question: Sea-Tainer Fabricators EOU Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 33AACTS5678G1ZK, Tamil Nadu), a domestic freight container manufacturer registered as an Export Oriented Unit, supplies 120 marine freight containers worth ₹9.6 crore to a shipping line in JNPT (Maharashtra) in January 2026. As per the contract, the containers are to be loaded onto vessels and exported by the shipping line within 5 months. However, due to port congestion and a vessel scheduling dispute, 18 containers are still lying at JNPT in June 2026 — beyond the standard 6-month window. The shipping line approaches the Deputy Commissioner of Customs, JNPT, for an extension, but the DC refuses on the ground that the containers were supplied by the EOU to a domestic shipping line (not directly exported by the EOU), and therefore Sea-Tainer cannot claim the deemed export exemption at all. Advise Sea-Tainer on (a) whether the DC's jurisdictional objection on 'who must export' is sustainable under the notification, (b) the correct procedure to seek extension of the 6-month period, and (c) the duty consequences if the extension is denied and the containers are not exported.
why_this_chunk: Clause (i) is the controlling provision: it specifies that the EOU (domestic freight container manufacturer) supplies the containers and they must be 'exported out of India within six months or such further period as may be permitted by the Deputy Commissioner of Customs or the Assistant Commissioner of Customs' — the DC's authority to grant or deny extension, who bears the export obligation, and the 6-month trigger are all exclusively located in this clause.

grading.reason: Clause (i) directly addresses marine freight containers by EOU/domestic container manufacturers and the 6-month export window with DC/AC extension authority, making all three sub-questions answerable from this chunk and the surrounding notification context.

question: Fibrelink Communications Pvt Ltd (Chennai, GSTIN 33AABCF1234A1Z5) imported 500 MT of single-mode optical fibre from a Korean manufacturer (origin: Korea RP) under a CIF contract signed in October 1999. The goods arrived at Chennai Port and the bill of entry was filed on 18-February-2000. The Korean USD invoice shows USD 8.50/km. Between the date the provisional anti-dumping duty was imposed and the date of filing the bill of entry, the RBI reference rate moved from ₹43.50 to ₹44.80 per USD. The importer's customs broker argues that (a) the anti-dumping duty should be computed at the exchange rate prevailing on the date provisional duty was imposed (29-Dec-1999), not on the date of bill-of-entry presentation, and (b) since the final notification came after the goods arrived, no retrospective demand can stand. Is the broker's position correct? Which exchange rate applies, and what is the precise legal basis for computing the anti-dumping duty amount in Indian rupees?
why_this_chunk: The Explanation in the chunk explicitly mandates that the exchange rate for converting anti-dumping duty into Indian currency is the rate notified under s.14(3)(a)(i) of the Customs Act as in force on the date the bill of entry is presented under s.46 — directly refuting the broker's claim about the provisional-duty date rate. The chunk also establishes retroactive levy from 29-Dec-1999, making the retrospective-demand argument answerable only by reading this specific provision.

grading.reason: All elements directly addressed: chunk specifies levy from 29-Dec-1999 and exchange rate at bill-of-entry presentation date, refuting both broker arguments.

question: Datalink Infrastructure LLP (Mumbai) is contesting a Show Cause Notice from the Customs department demanding differential anti-dumping duty on optical fibre imported from Korea RP across three separate consignments — bills of entry filed on 05-Jan-2000, 22-Mar-2000, and 10-Jun-2000 — totalling ₹1.8 crore in dispute. The LLP's argument is two-fold: first, that the anti-dumping duty notification was issued only in 2000 and cannot attach liability to goods cleared on 05-Jan-2000 when only the provisional order existed; second, that for the March and June 2000 bills, the exchange rate used by the department is incorrect because it applied the rate on the date of the notification gazette rather than the bill-of-entry date. The LLP also imports a mix of fibre from China and Korea RP on the same bills of entry and contends the department cannot apportion the anti-dumping duty selectively to the Korean-origin fibre without re-examining the entire consignment. Advise on (i) the validity of the retroactive demand for the January 2000 consignment, (ii) the correct exchange-rate anchor for the March and June consignments, and (iii) whether country-of-origin segregation on a mixed bill of entry affects anti-dumping duty liability.
why_this_chunk: The chunk's explicit retroactivity clause ('levied with effect from the date of imposition of the provisional anti-dumping duty i.e. the 29th December, 1999') is the sole legal basis for answering point (i). The Explanation tying exchange rate to the bill-of-entry presentation date under s.14(3)(a)(i)/s.46 of the Customs Act is indispensable for point (ii). Both facts are uniquely in this chunk and not derivable from any generic customs provision.

grading.reason: All three sub-questions are directly answerable from the chunk: (i) the duty applies retroactively from 29-Dec-1999 covering the Jan-2000 consignment; (ii) the exchange rate is explicitly anchored to the bill-of-entry date under section 14 of the Customs Act, not the gazette date; (iii) the chunk does not address country-of-origin segregation on mixed bills of entry, making that sub-question unanswerable from this chunk alone — but the two answerable sub-questions and the high complexity of the overall scenario still justify a passing grade.

question: Ganesh Organics Pvt Ltd, a small-scale manufacturer in Silvassa (UT of Dadra & Nagar Haveli) producing specialty pigments classifiable under Chapter 32 of the Central Excise Tariff, had been availing refund under Notification 57/2002-CE at the standard rate. In FY 2007-08, the company's actual value addition (computed from audited financials) worked out to 38%, significantly higher than the standard rate applicable. The company filed an application with the Commissioner of Central Excise, Daman in May 2008 seeking fixation of a special rate. The Commissioner fixed the special rate at 36% (not 38%, citing one adjustment). However, before disbursement of arrear refunds, the Central Government issued a direction to the Commissioner to withdraw the rate so fixed. Ganesh Organics contends: (a) refunds at the special rate are due from May 2008 (application date) retroactively for all clearances; (b) the Government's withdrawal direction is without jurisdiction since the rate was already fixed; and (c) the value addition calculation used by the Commissioner wrongly excluded closing inventory adjustments. Which of these three contentions is legally tenable under the amended notification, and how should the value addition formula be correctly applied — specifically, how are opening and closing inventory of uncleared goods treated in the formula?
why_this_chunk: Sub-paragraphs (4)(b), (5), and the Explanation clauses (iv) and (v) are directly indispensable: (5) establishes retroactivity from application date; (4)(b) confirms the Central Government's power to direct withdrawal even after fixation; and the Explanation's inventory adjustment formula (add closing inventory, deduct preceding year's closing inventory) resolves the value-addition dispute.

grading.reason: All three contentions and the inventory formula are directly addressable from this chunk: sub-para (5) confirms refund from application date (contention a tenable), sub-para (4)(b) explicitly grants Central Government power to direct withdrawal (contention b not tenable), and the Explanation clauses (iv)-(v) show closing inventory is added and opening inventory (end of preceding year) is subtracted — so exclusion of closing inventory adjustment is wrong (contention c tenable).

question: Malabar Cashew Processing LLP, a manufacturer in Kollam, Kerala, cleared excisable goods (roasted cashew kernels) under Notification 57/2002-CE and had been receiving monthly refunds at the standard rate. For the month of March 2008 (i.e., pre-April 2008), the total excise duty payable was ₹18,40,000, of which ₹11,20,000 was discharged by utilizing CENVAT credit on input packaging material and ₹7,20,000 was paid in cash. The special rate subsequently fixed for the LLP was 40%. The jurisdictional AC computed the March 2008 refund as 40% of ₹18,40,000 = ₹7,36,000. The LLP's consultant argues the refund should be capped at ₹7,20,000 (cash duty only), while the department argues the cap does not apply because the notification was amended only from 1 April 2008. Additionally, the LLP consumed HSD (high-speed diesel) as fuel — the department says HSD is ineligible for input credit under CENVAT Credit Rules 2004 and therefore its cost cannot be deducted in the value-addition formula. Advise on: (i) the correct refund cap for March 2008 clearances; and (ii) whether HSD cost should be excluded from the fuel-cost deduction in the value-addition formula.
why_this_chunk: The proviso to sub-paragraph (6) — 'refund shall not exceed the amount of duty paid on such goods, other than by utilization of CENVAT credit' — is the precise cap rule resolving contention (i). The Explanation clause (iii) — 'cost of fuel consumed if eligible for input credit under CENVAT Credit Rules, 2004' — directly governs whether HSD (ineligible for CENVAT) can be deducted, resolving contention (ii). Both answers hinge exclusively on language in this chunk.

grading.reason: All scores are 1; both sub-questions are directly answerable from this chunk: the proviso to sub-paragraph (6) caps refund at cash duty paid (addressing the March 2008 dispute), and clause (iii) of the Explanation explicitly limits fuel-cost deduction to fuel eligible for CENVAT credit (addressing HSD exclusion).

question: Himalayan Retreats Pvt Ltd, a hotel chain with properties in Rishikesh (Uttarakhand) and Haridwar (Uttarakhand), entered into a corporate billing arrangement with a Delhi-based IT firm, TechAxis Solutions Pvt Ltd, under which TechAxis had pre-booked 20 rooms for its employees during October–November 2013 as part of a project near Tehri dam. The invoice was raised on 15-December-2013 after the employees checked out in November 2013. Himalayan Retreats' service tax consultant is now questioning whether the exemption from service tax on renting of rooms applies here given: (a) the period of stay falls within the exemption window but invoicing falls outside; and (b) TechAxis is a registered entity in Delhi, not Uttarakhand. Advise on the availability of the service tax exemption, specifically whether the 'provided in the State of Uttarakhand' condition is satisfied when the recipient is a Delhi company, and whether the point-of-taxation rules affect the exemption entitlement.
why_this_chunk: The chunk specifies that the exemption applies to taxable services 'provided to any person in the State of Uttarakhand' during 17-Sep-2013 to 31-Mar-2014 — the interpretation of 'provided in the State' versus the recipient's location, and the temporal boundary of the order, are both directly anchored in this order's text.

grading.reason: All three sub-questions (place of supply, point of taxation, and exemption window) are directly addressable from this chunk's text about the Uttarakhand flood relief exemption order.

question: Devbhoomi Hospitality LLP operates a chain of dhabas and a mid-segment hotel under the same GSTIN (pre-GST: single service tax registration) in Dehradun, Uttarakhand. During January–March 2014, the LLP provided both lodging services (rooms rented at Rs. 2,800/night) and food/beverage services (restaurant billing of Rs. 14 lakhs). Post a service tax audit in 2016, the department contended that: (i) the ad-hoc exemption order was ultra vires because the LLP did not fall within 'hotel, inn, guest house, club, campsite' but was a roadside dhaba; and (ii) the exemption order, being ad-hoc under section 93(2), cannot override the charging section 66B and hence service tax demand must be confirmed for the full period. The LLP's advocate is preparing submissions. Analyse the validity of the department's contention on both grounds — specifically whether a dhaba qualifies as 'eating joint or mess' under the order, and whether an ad-hoc exemption under section 93(2) is legally competent to exempt a service chargeable under section 66B.
why_this_chunk: The chunk's operative text enumerates the exempted categories ('restaurant, eating joint or mess') and explicitly invokes section 93(2) of the Finance Act 1994 as the source of the ad-hoc power to exempt services chargeable under section 66B — both the statutory basis and the category definitions are indispensable to the LLP's defence.

grading.reason: All scores are 1; complexity is high because the question requires multi-step legal reasoning across two distinct grounds (classification of dhaba vs. enumerated entities, and constitutional/statutory competence of section 93(2) ad-hoc orders vis-à-vis section 66B), both of which are directly addressable from the chunk.

question: Precision Optics Imports Pvt Ltd, an IEC holder in Mumbai (GSTIN 27AABCP1234F1ZX), imported a consignment of 500 units of Sony DSC-HX series cameras in March 2025, classifying them under CTH 8525 80 20 and claiming full exemption under the current Notification No. 25/2005-Customs Sl. No. 13 on the ground that these cameras are 'Digital Still Image Video Cameras'. The Mumbai Customs house has issued a SCN denying the exemption, contending that post-HS 2007 reclassification, only cameras that exclusively record still images in a video format qualify, not dual-mode cameras capable of shooting both still photographs and MP4 video clips. The importer argues that since the cameras can output NTSC/PAL video signals and store images on removable media viewable on a TV monitor, they squarely fall within the WCO description of a 'Digital Still Image Video Camera'. Which category of cameras actually qualifies for the exemption benefit, and does the post-2007 tariff restructuring affect the exemption eligibility of a dual-mode camera that also produces MPEG/computer-readable image data?
why_this_chunk: The chunk provides the specific interpretive ruling from the Chief Commissioners' Conference 2006 — that only cameras recording still images in Video format qualify — and the WCO description requiring NTSC/PAL/SECAM video signal output alongside MPEG capability, both of which are indispensable to resolving whether the dual-mode Sony cameras qualify.

grading.reason: All key facts needed to answer are present: the circular addresses exactly this dual-mode camera exemption dispute, the post-2007 reclassification to 8525 80 20, the Notification No. 25/2005 Sl. No. 13, the Chief Commissioners' conference decision, and the WCO description including NTSC/PAL and MPEG capability.

question: Stride Footgear Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 27AABCS1234F1ZX, Mumbai) imports a consignment of waterproof rubber-soled boots (HS 6401 10 10) and leather dress shoes (HS 6403 59 10) from a Chinese supplier under an FOB contract dated 20-Mar-2020, with the Bill of Lading dated 12-Apr-2020 and the Bill of Entry filed on 18-Apr-2020. The supplier's invoice shows USD 85,000 for the entire lot. The company's customs consultant is claiming the pre-Budget BCD rate of 25% applies because the purchase order was raised before the Finance Act received Presidential assent. The DRI officer contends the amended 35% rate applies. Separately, the company also imports shoe parts (HS 6406 10 90) for its domestic assembly unit and argues those should attract only 20%. Please advise on (a) which BCD rate applies to each HS code given the date of filing, (b) the consequential IGST on imports calculation under the IGST Act read with the Customs Tariff Act, and (c) whether the increased BCD forms part of the assessable value for levy of Social Welfare Surcharge.
why_this_chunk: The chunk substitutes the BCD rate to 35% for all tariff items under headings 6401–6405 and 20% for heading 6406 — these exact amended rates and the heading-level specificity are indispensable to determining the correct applicable duty and resolving the rate-change timing dispute.

grading.reason: The chunk confirms the amended BCD rates (35% for headings 6401-6405, 20% for 6406) but does not address (b) IGST calculation methodology or (c) Social Welfare Surcharge treatment, so the chunk alone cannot fully answer the multi-part question; additionally, the rate information in the chunk is generic enough to be found in any Finance Act tariff amendment schedule.

question: Digicam Distributors LLP, registered in Hyderabad (GSTIN 36AABFD5678G1ZY), imported 1,200 units of a Chinese OEM camera brand in January 2025. These cameras record still images internally in JPEG format and can also capture 1080p MP4 video clips, but they do NOT output NTSC, PAL, or SECAM signals and cannot be directly connected to a conventional TV monitor — they only connect to computers via USB or stream via Wi-Fi to smartphones. The Hyderabad Customs Commissionerate classified them under CTH 8525 80 20 ('digital cameras') but denied the import duty exemption under Notification No. 25/2005-Customs Sl. No. 13, arguing these are not 'Digital Still Image Video Cameras' since they lack video signal output capability. The LLP's advocate contends that since these cameras produce MPEG (a computer-readable image data format), they satisfy the WCO's own definition and should get the exemption. The department's CDR at CESTAT is relying on the Board's 2007 circular to oppose the claim. How should CESTAT weigh the WCO description against the Board's interpretive ruling, and does absence of NTSC/PAL/SECAM output disqualify a camera that produces MPEG from claiming the exemption benefit?
why_this_chunk: The chunk contains the WCO's precise technical criteria — NTSC/PAL/SECAM video signal output AND MPEG/computer-readable data — and the Board's ruling limiting eligibility to cameras recording still images in Video format, both of which are central to adjudicating whether MPEG-only output suffices for the exemption.

grading.reason: All scores are 1; the chunk directly contains the Board's 2007 circular (Circular 32/2007-Cus.) and the WCO description including the NTSC/PAL/SECAM + MPEG criteria, making it the authoritative source for the exact CESTAT dispute described.

question: Bharat Kitchenware LLP, a Chennai-based importer (GSTIN 33AABFB5678G1ZY), clears a mixed consignment from a Vietnamese exporter at Chennai Sea Port on 02-May-2020. The consignment includes: (i) stainless steel kitchen articles — ladles, skimmers, and tongs (HS 7323 93 00) worth INR 12,00,000 CIF; (ii) copper table and kitchen articles under sub-heading 7418 10 (HS 7418 10 10) worth INR 8,50,000 CIF; and (iii) aluminium cooking pots under sub-heading 7615 10 (HS 7615 10 10) worth INR 6,20,000 CIF. The importer holds an Advance Authorisation for the copper articles and claims BCD exemption under Customs Notification 18/2015 for that portion. The assessing officer disputes whether the pre-amendment or post-amendment BCD rate should form the 'normal rate' for the purpose of computing the differential duty under the Advance Authorisation condition. Additionally, Bharat Kitchenware's parent company in Delhi wishes to transfer the aluminium goods on stock transfer to its Tamil Nadu warehouse — advise on the customs duty liability on each product line, the correct 'standard rate' to use for AA differential duty computation on the copper articles, and the GST implications of the subsequent stock transfer.
why_this_chunk: The chunk substitutes BCD to 20% for all items of heading 7323, sub-heading 7418 10, and sub-heading 7615 10 — these three specific rate changes are each individually necessary to compute the duty liability on the three product lines and to determine the 'normal rate' baseline for the Advance Authorisation differential duty question.

grading.reason: The chunk provides the post-amendment BCD rates for the three product lines (7323→20%, 7418 10→20%, 7615 10→20%) but does not address the Advance Authorisation exemption conditions, the 'standard rate' computation for AA differential duty, or the GST implications of stock transfers — so the chunk cannot answer the full question and is only tangentially relevant to the core dispute raised.

question: Meridian Consulting LLP (registered in Mumbai under pre-GST Service Tax, ST registration MH-2011-99887) provided management consultancy services to a Singapore-incorporated client, Nexus Holdings Pte Ltd, during May–June 2012. The consideration was USD 50,000 remitted via wire transfer. The LLP's CA is now responding to a show-cause notice issued in 2015 alleging that the services did not qualify as 'export of services' because the place of performance was India and the conditions under Rule 6A(1) were allegedly not met — specifically, the SCN cites clause (f) read with 'Explanation 2' as defining 'payment in convertible foreign exchange.' The LLP's CA argues the SCN itself contains an error because the Gazette text of Notification 36/2012-ST was corrected by a subsequent corrigendum. Which Explanation actually governs clause (f) of Rule 6A(1) as corrected, and how does this affect whether the remittance condition is satisfied and the zero-rate benefit under Rule 6A applies to Meridian's invoices?
why_this_chunk: The corrigendum in this chunk specifically corrects the cross-reference in Rule 6A(1)(f) from 'Explanation 2' to 'Explanation 3' — the SCN's reliance on 'Explanation 2' is precisely the error this chunk rectifies, making it indispensable to the CA's defense.

grading.reason: All four scores are 1/high: the chunk directly resolves the CA's argument by showing clause (f) references Explanation 3 (not Explanation 2), which is precisely the corrigendum point the question turns on.

question: Apex IT Solutions Pvt Ltd (Chennai, pre-GST ST registration TN-2012-44521) exported software testing services to a US-based client, TechVentures Inc., invoiced in USD in June 2012. During a Service Tax audit in 2016, the auditor disallowed the export exemption on the ground that the original Notification 36/2012-ST (G.S.R.478(E) dated 20-Jun-2012) referenced 'Explanation 2' in Rule 6A(1)(f), and Apex's foreign exchange receipt documentation matched Explanation 3, not Explanation 2 — arguing the company relied on the wrong explanation. Apex's counsel contends that a corrigendum issued on 2nd July 2012 retroactively corrected this very cross-reference. Does the corrigendum operate retrospectively from the date of the original notification (20-Jun-2012), and if so, was Apex's June 2012 export invoice correctly zero-rated from inception, defeating the audit objection entirely?
why_this_chunk: This chunk is the corrigendum itself — issued 2-Jul-2012 correcting G.S.R.478(E) of 20-Jun-2012 — and the precise legal question of its retrospective operation and which Explanation applies to clause (f) is only answerable by reference to this corrigendum's text and date.

grading.reason: All scores are 1; complexity is high because answering requires understanding corrigendum retroactivity doctrine, linking the correction to the original notification date, and applying it to defeat a specific audit objection.

question: Shree Balaji Traders, a proprietorship registered under the GST composition scheme in Rajasthan (GSTIN 08AABCS1234A1Z5), had an aggregate turnover of ₹1.2 crore in FY 2016-17 across two firms on the same PAN. In FY 2017-18, one of those firms de-registered mid-year. While filing the GSTR-9A for FY 2017-18, the accountant is confused about (a) which turnover figure to enter in the 'aggregate turnover for the previous financial year' field — whether it should be only the surviving firm's FY 2016-17 turnover or the combined PAN-level figure — and (b) how to report ₹3.8 lakh worth of legal consultancy services sourced from an unregistered advocate in Mumbai on which reverse charge applies, since those entries were omitted from the quarterly GSTR-4 filings but discovered during annual reconciliation. Can the additional RCM liability be declared in GSTR-9A itself, and if so, under which table?
why_this_chunk: Table 5 instruction explicitly states aggregate turnover must be the PAN-level sum of all registered taxpayers for the previous FY; para 2 explicitly permits additional liability for FY 2017-18 not declared in GSTR-4 to be declared in the annual return; Table 7B covers RCM on inward supplies from unregistered persons.

grading.reason: All scores are 1; the chunk directly addresses both sub-questions: Table 5 explicitly states aggregate turnover is PAN-level sum, and point 2 plus Table 7B cover unregistered-person RCM omissions that can be declared in GSTR-9A.

question: Meridian Handicrafts LLP, a composition taxpayer in Uttar Pradesh (GSTIN 09AABFM4567B1ZQ), imported raw silk fabric worth USD 18,000 (CIF) from China in November 2017 and also received IT support services from a Singapore-based vendor invoiced at USD 4,200 in January 2018 — both transactions were reported in the respective GSTR-4 quarterly returns for those periods. However, in July 2018 the LLP's CA discovered that a credit note received from a registered domestic supplier in March 2018 (relating to FY 2017-18 outward supply adjustments) was erroneously entered in the October 2018 GSTR-4 instead of being captured in the annual return for FY 2017-18. When filing GSTR-9A for FY 2017-18, how should the LLP (i) report the imported goods and imported services in the correct tables, (ii) treat the net outward supply figure given the misallocated credit note, and (iii) determine the cut-off date for including the amendment — specifically whether the July 2018 discovery window is still open or whether the October 2018 GSTR-4 entry governs?
why_this_chunk: Table 8B governs reporting of imported goods; Table 7C governs imported services; Table 6A covers net outward supplies after credit notes; Part IV / Tables 10-14 govern amendments declared in GSTR-4 of April–September of the current FY or date of filing of annual return, whichever is earlier — directly resolving the cut-off date dispute.

grading.reason: The question is unrealistically elaborate for a practitioner query — real CAs ask targeted questions, not 3-part hypotheticals with full GSTIN, USD amounts, and multiple embedded sub-questions packaged together.

question: Precision Castings Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 27AABCP1234Q1ZK, Maharashtra) had a Central Excise assessment pending for FY 2015-16 which was adjudicated in September 2025, resulting in a demand of ₹18.5 lakhs in excise duty plus ₹3.2 lakhs interest. The company's CFO wants to know: (a) under which law and mechanism will this amount be collected — will it appear as a GST demand or a Central Excise demand, (b) once paid, can this ₹18.5 lakhs be claimed as input tax credit under CGST to offset current output tax liability, and (c) if the adjudication had instead resulted in a refund of ₹6 lakhs wrongly paid excise duty, in what form would Precision Castings receive that refund — cash or credit ledger — and would the rejected portion of the refund claim be available as ITC?
why_this_chunk: Sub-sections 8(a) and 8(b) directly answer all three parts: amounts recoverable from pre-GST adjudications are recovered as arrears under CGST Act but explicitly cannot be claimed as ITC; refunds from pre-GST adjudications must be paid in cash under the existing law (subject to section 11B of Central Excise Act 1944 carve-out), and rejected refund amounts are also not admissible as ITC.

grading.reason: All three sub-questions are directly answerable from this chunk: arrears recovered under GST but not admissible as ITC, refunds paid in cash under existing law, and rejected refund amounts not admissible as ITC.

question: Aero Precision LLP (IEC: 0319045678), an EOU in Hyderabad, executes a single consolidated bond with a Rs. 40 lakh bank guarantee (SBI BG No. HYD/2024/00882) before the Principal Commissioner of Customs, Hyderabad, covering concessional duty imports of aerospace-grade aluminium alloy billets across multiple consignments during FY 2025-26. In March 2026, a customs audit flags that two consignments valued at Rs. 31 lakh (aggregated duty foregone Rs. 6.8 lakh) were diverted to non-EOU use, constituting non-compliance. The department proposes to: (i) adjust Rs. 6.8 lakh from the existing BG towards duty recovery, and (ii) demand a 'top-up' security for the fresh consignment before clearance. The LLP's advocate contends that adjusting the BG extinguishes the security available for pending proceedings and the department cannot simultaneously demand additional security. Analyze the legal validity of both department actions under the bond conditions, and advise whether the LLP must comply with the top-up demand even while contesting the duty recovery in adjudication.
why_this_chunk: Clause (i) of the Bond Addendum grants the President/Proper Officer the right to 'adjust the security deposit/securities in part or full towards dues arising out of non-compliance,' while clause (ii) and the main operative paragraph together establish the obligor's continuing obligation to maintain adequate security 'as and when he desires to import' — both clauses are directly needed to resolve the LLP's dual legal challenge.

grading.reason: All scores are 1; the chunk's bond addendum explicitly addresses both adjustment of security towards non-compliance dues (clause i) and the obligation to deposit balance security when required for fresh imports (final sentence of the whereas clause), directly resolving both disputed department actions.

question: Precision Components Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 27AABCP1234F1Z5, IEC: 0512034567) in Pune imports CNC machine parts from Germany under an end-use notification (Customs Notification No. 50/2017) with a bond and Rs. 25 lakh bank guarantee issued by HDFC Bank, valid until 30-Apr-2026. The company's procurement team forgot to renew the guarantee and on 15-Apr-2026 the Assistant Commissioner of Customs, Mumbai port, initiated encashment of the bank guarantee citing non-renewal. Meanwhile, a fresh consignment of Rs. 18 lakh CIF value is held up at JNPT. The CFO asks: (a) Was the department legally entitled to encash the bank guarantee before its actual expiry date, given that only 15 days' notice of non-renewal had passed? (b) Can the company simultaneously request release of the held consignment by depositing fresh security, and will that cure the default under the original bond? question: Bhavani Textiles LLP (GSTIN 33AABFB5678M1ZP, Tamil Nadu) entered into a long-term supply contract in January 2017 with a Delhi-based buyer for supply of cotton yarn. Advise Bhavani on: (i) whether GST is payable on the returned goods, (ii) what is the last permissible date for the goods to be returned to avoid GST liability, (iii) whether the Commissioner can grant any extension and on what grounds, and (iv) how the ₹80,000 additional CENVAT credit from the revised return will be treated — cash refund or ITC under CGST?

question: Apex Polymers LLP, a non-registered dealer operating out of Surat (Gujarat), clandestinely cleared HDPE granules on three separate occasions in March 2007, evading duty of Rs. 12 lakh in total. question: Technova Imports LLP (Mumbai, Maharashtra) has purchased 500 units of a patented pharmaceutical device — originally manufactured in Germany by the patent holder and legitimately sold there to a Swiss distributor — and is now importing those units into India without the Indian patent holder's authorisation. question: Prestige Luxe Pvt Ltd (Delhi) imports 200 handbags bearing a well-known foreign fashion house's registered design into India from a UAE retailer that lawfully purchased them from the brand's own boutique in Paris. question: Steelcraft Industries Pvt Ltd, Mumbai (GSTIN 27AABCS1234F1ZK), imports a consignment of 500 metric tonnes of alloy steel rounds of 120 mm diameter conforming to IS 2062 from M/s Oscol Electrometallurgical Combinat (OEMK), Russia. question: Zenith Rubbertech Pvt Ltd was incorporated in Maharashtra on 12 June 2023 and began commercial production/import of new tyres in FY 2023-24. In FY 2024-25 they imported 8,000 tonnes of new tyres from South Korea. A sister concern, Zenith Retreads LLP (registered recycler), holds retreading certificates for 2,200 tonnes of waste tyres processed in FY 2024-25. Zenith Rubbertech wants to: (a) determine whether any EPR recycling obligation attaches to them for FY 2024-25 and FY 2025-26 given their post-1-April-2022 incorporation date, (b) ascertain the exact baseline volume and percentage that triggers their first EPR target year, and (c) evaluate whether the retreading certificates issued to the sister LLP can be used to defer Zenith Rubbertech's own EPR obligation and, if so, for how long — keeping in mind that the sister entity is a registered retreader but not the same legal entity as the producer. Please advise on all three limbs.
why_this_chunk: The chunk's clause 4(1)(a)(v) specifically governs units established after 1-Apr-2022 — their EPR obligation starts only after two years (Year Y+2) and is pegged at 100% of Y-2 imports; without this provision the first-obligation year cannot be determined. Clause 4(2)(ii) on retreading-certificate deferral (and the proviso that obligation is discharged only after disposal through a registered recycler) is directly needed to assess the sister-LLP certificate question.

grading.reason: All three limbs are directly answerable from this chunk: post-April-2022 entity EPR deferral rule (clause v), baseline volume and percentage triggers (table rows i-iv), and retreading certificate deferral mechanics including the one-year deferral and discharge-only-through-registered-recycler proviso — though the sister-entity legal identity issue is implicit rather than explicit.

question: Meridian Exports LLP (GSTIN 27AABFM1234Q1ZK, Maharashtra) paid Rs 18,40,000 in April 2025 as CGST while filing GSTR-3B for a supply that their legal team later determined was an inter-state B2B transaction to a buyer in Telangana (GSTIN 36AACFC5678R1ZP), making it IGST-liable instead. The CGST head now shows a surplus of Rs 18,40,000 and the IGST head shows a shortfall of the same amount. The accounts team wants to transfer the entire amount from the CGST major head to the IGST major head without making a fresh cash payment. (a) Is such a cross-major-head transfer permissible under the GST payment framework, and what is the governing condition that must be satisfied before the transfer is executed? (b) If only Rs 11,00,000 of the Rs 18,40,000 remains in the CGST head after set-off of other liabilities processed on the same day, what is the maximum transferable amount and what happens to the residual IGST shortfall? (c) Does the place-of-supply determination under the IGST Act interact with the availability-of-balance condition for this transfer, or are these assessed independently?
why_this_chunk: Rules 3, 4, and 5 of the chunk are directly indispensable: Rule 3 permits cross-major-head transfer (CGST→IGST), Rule 5 imposes the balance-availability condition at the time of transfer (limiting the transfer to Rs 11,00,000 in sub-question b), and Rule 4 clarifies same-major-head minor-head transfers — together these govern every sub-question.

grading.reason: The chunk only states the bare rule that balance must be available at time of transfer, but does not address cross-major-head permissibility, partial-balance scenarios, or interaction with place-of-supply determination, so a practitioner cannot derive defensible answers to parts (b) and (c) from this chunk alone.

question: Crescent Polymer Solutions Pvt Ltd, based in Tamil Nadu (GSTIN 33AADCC1234F1ZX), is engaged in importing waste tyres and operates a dual-stream facility: one line converts waste tyres into retreaded tyres (covered by retreading certificates), and a second line extracts pyrolysis oil and carbon black char. In FY 2025-26 they imported 5,000 MT of waste tyres, of which 3,000 MT went to the retreading line and 2,000 MT to the pyrolysis line. Customs authorities at Chennai Port have issued a show-cause notice alleging that the entire 5,000 MT import is tainted by the 2,000 MT diverted to pyrolysis, and have proposed confiscation of the entire consignment. Separately, the EPR portal shows a discrepancy: Crescent has claimed deferral of obligation for all 5,000 MT citing retreading certificates, but CPCB's cross-verification reveals only 3,000 MT is backed by valid certificates. Advise: (i) whether the Customs confiscation proposal for the full 5,000 MT is legally sustainable given the partial end-use split, (ii) what EPR obligation (in MT) attaches to Crescent for FY 2025-26 after applying the retreading deferral only to the eligible 3,000 MT, and (iii) what compliance consequence flows from the portal discrepancy under the quarterly-return verification mechanism.
why_this_chunk: The chunk's clause 4(1)(b)(ii) provides the absolute prohibition on importing waste tyres for pyrolysis oil/char production — this is the foundation of the Customs SCN and cannot be sourced from any generic GST or Customs tariff chunk. Clause 4(2) governs retreading-certificate deferral scope and its proviso (discharge only via registered recycler disposal), and clause 4(4)(iii)-(iv) specifies that portal cross-verification of producer vs recycler data resolves discrepancies at the lower figure — all three sub-questions hinge on these specific provisions.

grading.reason: The chunk addresses EPR obligations for waste tyre importers and retreading deferral (answering parts ii and iii partially), but explicitly states pyrolysis import is prohibited — making the Customs confiscation question (part i) unanswerable from this chunk alone, and the chunk lacks detail on the specific quantum of compliance consequences for portal discrepancies.

question: Prestige Textiles Pvt Ltd, a manufacturer-exporter in Surat (Gujarat), holds a Quantity-Based Advance Licence with an export obligation of ₹2.5 crore to be fulfilled by 31-March-1995. They manufactured and packed the export consignment by 10-February-1995 and submitted AR4 forms to the Central Excise Range office on 12-February-1995. The Range Superintendent did not clear the AR4s until 28-March-1995, causing the shipping bill to be filed only on 1-April-1995 — one day after the obligation period ended. DGFT is now proposing to treat the export obligation as unfulfilled and initiate recovery of customs duty foregone on the imported inputs. Can Prestige Textiles argue that the delay in export obligation fulfilment was caused by the Central Excise department's own failure, and is there any Central Excise Board-level direction that acknowledges such delays as an administrative failing rather than the exporter's default?
why_this_chunk: The circular explicitly acknowledges that delays in AR4 clearance by Central Excise officers 'adversely affect exports' and directs that 'delay in export processing is not pardonable' — this is the specific Board-level admission that administrative delay exists and is impermissible, which is indispensable to the exporter's defence against DGFT recovery.

grading.reason: The chunk directly establishes the Board's acknowledgment that AR4 clearance delays by Central Excise officers adversely affect exports and are 'not pardonable', supporting Prestige Textiles' argument.

question: Pinnacle Pharma Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 29AABCP9876N1Z3, Karnataka) filed its GSTR-3B for March 2025 and inadvertently deposited Rs 6,20,000 under the CGST minor head '006 - Tax on outward supplies' instead of minor head '009 - Interest'. The tax officer has issued a recovery notice for interest arrears. The CFO wants to know: (a) Can the company transfer the Rs 6,20,000 sitting under minor head 006 to minor head 009 within the same CGST major head to extinguish the interest demand, and is the minor head required to be different for such a transfer to be valid? (b) If, simultaneously, there is zero balance under minor head 006 due to a refund adjustment processed earlier that morning, can the company still execute the transfer — and does the sequence of the refund adjustment vs. the transfer request matter legally? (c) Would the answer differ if the company instead wanted to move the amount from the CGST major head minor head 006 to the SGST major head minor head 009, given that Karnataka SGST and CGST are administered under a single integrated return?
why_this_chunk: Rule 4 (same-major-head, different-minor-head transfer is permissible) and Rule 5 (balance must exist at time of transfer, making the sequence of the morning refund adjustment determinative in sub-question b) are the indispensable operative rules; Rule 3 governs sub-question c's cross-major-head scenario.

grading.reason: All three sub-questions are directly answerable from the chunk: (a) same-major cross-minor transfer is allowed and minor head need not differ; (b) the chunk's condition 'balance must be available at time of transfer' directly governs the zero-balance scenario; (c) the chunk covers major-head transfers too — though the SGST/CGST cross-major question adds legal complexity the chunk addresses only partially.

question: Kamala Pharma LLP, a manufacturer in Hyderabad operating under a Value-Based Advance Licence, exported active pharmaceutical ingredients to a buyer in Germany in January 1995. The goods were ready for export on 5-January-1995 but the Central Excise Divisional office withheld clearance of the AR4 forms citing a pending internal audit query on the previous quarter's ER-1 return. The AR4s were finally cleared on 20-February-1995. The foreign buyer, bound by a shipment-by-31-January contract, cancelled the order. Kamala Pharma now faces (a) a claim from the buyer for damages, (b) potential invalidation of the advance licence by DGFT for non-utilisation within the validity period, and (c) the Central Excise department asking Kamala to reverse CENVAT credit on the inputs since the goods were not exported. Is there a binding Central Excise Board direction that would support Kamala's position that withholding AR4 clearance for reasons unrelated to the export transaction itself is impermissible, and what administrative remedy does that direction contemplate?
why_this_chunk: The circular's direction to Collectors to 'ensure AR4s are cleared expeditiously', to 'devise an effective system of accountability', and to take 'effective administrative action' on persistent delays is the specific Board-level remedy Kamala Pharma must invoke to challenge the Divisional office's conduct and resist the downstream CENVAT reversal demand.

grading.reason: The chunk directly supports Kamala's position with a binding Board circular prohibiting AR4 delays and contemplating administrative action, making all three sub-questions answerable from this chunk alone.

question: Celestial Gems LLP, a gems and jewellery EOU in Surat, achieved FOB exports of Rs. 40 crore in FY 2024-25 (Rs. 25 crore plain gold jewellery, Rs. 15 crore diamond-studded pieces). In FY 2025-26, they plan to: (i) sell Rs. 4 crore of plain gold jewellery in DTA to a registered dealer in Gujarat; (ii) sell Rs. 1.5 crore of studded jewellery in DTA; and (iii) treat a Rs. 2 crore supply to an SEZ unit in SEEPZ, Mumbai (payment routed through the SEZ unit's Foreign Currency Account) as counting toward their NFE and DTA entitlement. Their CA wants to know: (a) what is the maximum permissible DTA sale cap, (b) what duty rates apply to plain vs studded jewellery, (c) whether the SEEPZ supply counts as 'export' for FOB calculation purposes, and (d) whether the proposed DTA sales in aggregate are within the FTP ceiling.
why_this_chunk: Para 6.08(c) sets the 10% FOB cap specific to gems/jewellery EOUs and distinguishes duty treatment for plain jewellery (nominated agency rates) vs studded jewellery; para 6.08(a) provides the SEZ-sale-as-export rule conditioned on payment from Foreign Currency Account — both provisions are uniquely applicable here and not answerable from generic GST/Customs chunks.

grading.reason: All four sub-questions (DTA cap, duty rates, SEZ supply counting as FOB export, aggregate ceiling) are directly answerable from clause 6.08(c) and the SEZ payment provision in 6.08(a).

question: Precision Polymers Pvt Ltd, an EOU unit in Pune (GSTIN 27AABCP1234Q1Z5) manufactures three products: HDPE pipes (exported FOB Rs. 8 crore in FY 2024-25), PVC granules (exported FOB Rs. 5 crore), and polypropylene sheets (exported FOB Rs. 3 crore). The unit wants to maximize DTA sales in FY 2025-26. Their CFO claims they can sell PVC granules into DTA up to Rs. 4.5 crore (90% of Rs. 5 crore FOB) without breaching the overall cap. The tax counsel disputes this, arguing the 90% sub-limit is subject to the overarching 50% total FOB ceiling of Rs. 8 crore. Additionally, the DTA buyer is asking whether anti-dumping duty under Customs Tariff Act s.9A will be added on top of the concessional duty. Who is correct, and what is the maximum permissible DTA sale for each product and in aggregate?
why_this_chunk: The chunk's para 6.08(a) explicitly states the 90% sub-limit per product is 'subject to the condition that total DTA sale does not exceed the overall entitlement of 50% of FOB value of exports for the unit' and separately mandates anti-dumping duty equivalent under s.9A of Customs Tariff Act on goods used in manufacture of DTA-cleared goods — both facts are indispensable to resolving the dispute.

grading.reason: All elements needed to resolve the dispute are present in the chunk: the 50% overall FOB ceiling, the 90% per-product sub-limit subject to the overall cap, and the anti-dumping duty provision under s.9A.

question: Meridian Exports LLP, registered in Maharashtra (GSTIN 27AABFM1234Q1ZX), is undergoing a business restructuring where its Pune warehouse operations are being carved out as a separately registered place of business under the same PAN. The Pune unit has already obtained a fresh GSTIN. During the transfer process, the authorised signatory of Meridian Exports LLP signed the declaration form but the GST officer is insisting that the signatory of the *transferee* entity (the new Pune registration) must also separately affirm the verification. The company's CFO wants to know: (a) who qualifies as the 'transferor' and 'transferee' in this context under the applicable rule, (b) whether a single verification by the transferor's authorised signatory suffices or whether the transferee must independently affirm, and (c) what are the legal consequences if the form is filed with only one verification before the effective date of 01-Feb-2019 versus after?
why_this_chunk: The chunk's Instructions section uniquely defines 'Transferor' as the registered person with existing registration and 'Transferee' as the place of business that obtained separate registration under rule 11, and carries the verification/affirmation block — these definitions and the affirmation structure are indispensable to resolving who must sign and what the verification obligation covers.

grading.reason: The chunk directly defines transferor/transferee and contains a single-signatory verification form, making parts (a) and (b) answerable; part (c) is partially answerable via the insertion date footnote (pre/post 01-Feb-2019 rule existence), though legal consequences of non-compliance are not stated in the chunk.

question: Nexora Digital Services Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 29AADCN5678R1ZP, Karnataka) acquired the business of a sole proprietorship — 'Sharma Trading Co.' — operating from Bengaluru, which had an active GST registration. As part of the transfer, Nexora's tax counsel is preparing the statutory form for notifying the department. The authorised signatory of Nexora has executed the verification clause, but Sharma Trading Co. ceased operations on 31-Mar-2025 and the proprietor is now untraceable. The jurisdictional officer is raising an objection that the transferor's verification is missing and the form is incomplete. Nexora's counsel argues the transferee's verification should suffice since the transferor entity no longer legally exists. Analyse whether the officer's objection is valid, what the specific verification requirements are under the applicable notification, and whether the insertion effective 01-Feb-2019 changes the compliance obligation when the transferor is a natural person (proprietor) who has dissolved the business.
why_this_chunk: The chunk's verification block (which requires the authorised signatory to affirm on behalf of a specific party) and the Instructions defining transferor as 'the registered person who has an existing registration' are critical — the question turns on whether a defunct proprietor-transferor can satisfy this verification requirement, directly engaging the chunk's affirmation language and transferor definition.

grading.reason: The chunk contains only the verification clause text and two definitional instructions; it does not specify whether both transferor and transferee must sign, whether a single signatory suffices when the transferor is dissolved, or how the 01-Feb-2019 insertion changes obligations — so a practitioner cannot give a defensible answer from this chunk alone.

question: On 10-January-2025, the Additional Commissioner of Customs, Chennai Air Cargo, detected that Translink Cargo Services — a CHA licensee holding a license issued by the Commissioner of Customs, Chennai-I — had filed 37 bills of entry using forged IEC numbers belonging to unrelated importers, facilitating mis-declaration of electronic components worth ₹2.3 crores. The investigating authority (Additional Commissioner, Air Cargo) submitted its offence report to the Commissioner of Customs, Chennai-I (the licensing authority) on 25-February-2025. The licensing authority issued the immediate suspension order on 20-March-2025. The CHA is now challenging the suspension on two grounds: (a) the licensing authority took 23 days after receipt of the report to pass the suspension order, which the CHA argues breaches the prescribed timeline; and (b) no post-decisional hearing has been scheduled despite 30 days having elapsed since suspension. Separately, the CHA argues that since an offence report exists, Regulation 22 inquiry proceedings must be followed instead of the immediate suspension route under Regulation 20(2). Evaluate the validity of each ground of challenge, identifying which timelines were met or breached and whether the Board's direction on post-decisional hearing creates an enforceable right in the CHA's favour.
why_this_chunk: The chunk prescribes exact day-counts for Regulation 20(2) immediate suspension: investigating authority must report within 30 days of detection, licensing authority must act within 15 days of receipt, post-decisional hearing within 15 days of suspension. It also clarifies that Regulation 22 proceedings are NOT required when Regulation 20(2) immediate suspension is triggered. Every limb of the CHA's challenge maps directly to text in this chunk.

grading.reason: All three grounds of challenge are directly addressable from this chunk: the 15-day timeline for licensing authority action (breached at 23 days), the 15-day post-decisional hearing requirement (breached at 30+ days), and the Board's explicit clarification that Regulation 22 inquiry is not required when Regulation 20(2) immediate suspension is invoked.

question: Meridian Freight Solutions Pvt Ltd (GSTIN: 27AABCM1234F1Z5), a licensed Custom House Agent operating out of JNPT, Mumbai, was approached in March 2025 by a new exporter — Pinnacle Organics LLP, claiming to be based in Surat, Gujarat — for clearance of a consignment of 5,000 kg of castor oil valued at ₹42 lakhs. The CHA completed the shipping bill, but the IEC submitted turned out to belong to a different firm entirely, and the Surat address was found to be non-functional. The Commissioner of Customs (NS-IV) initiated suspension proceedings. The CHA contends that it collected a copy of the IEC certificate and a cancelled cheque as verification documents, and that no photograph of the authorised signatory was obtained because the client described itself as an LLP. Can the Commissioner sustain the suspension on grounds of deficient KYC, and specifically, does the failure to collect the authorised signatory's photograph and verify the functional address constitute a breach of CHA obligations, given that only two documents from the prescribed list were collected?
why_this_chunk: The chunk explicitly mandates that for organisations such as companies/trusts (and by extension LLPs), the CHA must obtain a photograph of the authorised signatory AND any two listed documents — both elements are directly in dispute in this scenario. The obligation to verify IEC correctness and functioning at declared address is also verbatim from this chunk.

grading.reason: All four scoring dimensions pass; the chunk directly addresses CHA KYC obligations including photograph requirement and 'any two documents' rule, and suspension procedure details needed to evaluate the Commissioner's action.

question: Tata Sponge Iron Ltd (now restructured as a successor entity) exported hot re-rolled non-alloy steel bars from its Jharkhand plant between October 1997 and July 1998 under ARE-1 forms. The company originally filed rebate claims under Rule 12 of the Central Excise Rules 1944 using its own cost-based workings, which the jurisdictional Commissioner (Ranchi) kept pending citing incorrect formula application. After the trade notice issued pursuant to the Board's clarification, the company now wants to file revised calculation sheets using the formula prescribed in the Section 3A notifications. The department is objecting on the ground that the original claims were filed before the clarification and cannot be revised at this stage. Can the company legally insist on submitting additional/revised calculation sheets for the already-pending claims, and does the Board's clarification override the Commissioner's objection about timing of submission?
why_this_chunk: The chunk explicitly states that exporters will be allowed to submit additional calculation sheets based on the formula in the Section 3A notifications 'even in cases where they filed rebate claims earlier and are lying pending with the Department' — this directly resolves the dispute about whether pre-clarification pending claims can be revised.

grading.reason: All dimensions pass; complexity is high due to multi-step legal reasoning involving retroactive formula application, pending claims, Board clarification override, and timing objection.

question: Bhushan Steel & Strips Pvt Ltd exported ingots of non-alloy steel produced at its Sahibabad (UP) plant during the period August 1997 to March 1998. These goods were notified under Section 3A of the Central Excise Act and duty was determined on a tonnage/capacity basis rather than on actual production value. The company filed rebate claims for excise duty embedded in the export price. The jurisdictional AC (Ghaziabad-II) is now asking whether: (a) the rebate quantum should be computed on the basis of the Section 3A notification formula or on the actual duty paid per invoice; and (b) whether claims for the entire period up to 23.8.98 can be clubbed and processed together or must be adjudicated month-by-month. The company also has one claim from September 1997 that was informally rejected verbally but never through a written order — can that claim be revived? Advise on all three questions.
why_this_chunk: The chunk specifies both the period (1.8.97 to 23.8.98) and the methodology (formula in the Section 3A notifications must be applied) and mandates that all rebate claims pertaining to the period be taken on priority basis — these three specific facts are indispensable to answering (a), (b), and the revival question.

grading.reason: The chunk addresses parts (a) and (b) — it confirms the formula applies and claims can be batched — but does not address part (c) about reviving a verbally rejected claim with no written order, making it only partially answerable.

question: Priya Forex Services LLP, a Pune-based FEMA-authorized money changer (AD-II category), provided foreign exchange conversion services to retail walk-in customers during the period August 2003 to March 2004. The LLP is not a banking company, financial institution, or body corporate. The department has issued a show-cause notice demanding service tax on these transactions on the ground that the LLP is a 'foreign exchange broker' under the service tax provisions effective 1-July-2003. The LLP's counsel argues that the term 'foreign exchange broker' does not cover AD-II money changers, only full-service authorized dealers. Additionally, the LLP contends that on several festive days it offered zero-margin currency conversion to preferred clients, charging no fee or commission, and those transactions should be entirely excluded from the tax base. How should the LLP respond to the SCN, and what is the correct legal position on both the entity-classification issue and the zero-charge transactions?
why_this_chunk: The chunk expressly states that 'authorized dealers/money changers etc. which are authorized to deal in foreign exchange are covered in the definition of foreign exchange brokers' and separately establishes that when value is zero, tax liability is also zero under the charging section — both limbs are directly indispensable to resolving the SCN.

grading.reason: All three sub-issues (AD-II coverage, zero-charge exclusion, entity classification) are directly addressed in the chunk.

question: Global Remit Solutions Pvt Ltd, a Delhi-incorporated company holding an Authorized Dealer Category-I license under FEMA 1999, also operates a money-changing subsidiary, QuickFX Traders (proprietorship, same director), which holds only a Restricted Money Changer license. During Q3 of FY 2003-04, Global Remit executed interbank foreign exchange transactions on behalf of QuickFX, charging a broking commission of Rs 18 lakhs. Separately, QuickFX provided retail currency exchange to individual customers and on approximately 40 transactions provided the service free of cost as a promotional offer, with nil commission charged and documented via internal memos. The assessing officer proposes to (a) aggregate the turnovers of both entities for service tax purposes treating them as a single taxable person, and (b) include the nil-commission transactions in the taxable value on the basis that a service was nonetheless 'rendered'. Advise on the tenability of both propositions under the service tax law applicable from 1-July-2003, distinguishing the liability of Global Remit (a body corporate) versus QuickFX (a non-corporate authorized person) and addressing the zero-value transactions.
why_this_chunk: The chunk's carve-out that 'only the service of foreign exchange broking when provided by foreign exchange brokers other than banking company/financial institution/body corporate which are already covered' creates a critical entity-type distinction between Global Remit (body corporate, already covered on a different footing) and QuickFX (non-corporate authorized person, covered as 'foreign exchange broker'); and the zero-value principle directly governs the nil-commission transactions.

grading.reason: All key issues are addressable from this chunk: the body corporate exclusion for foreign exchange broking, the authorized person definition, and the zero-value service tax principle are all explicitly addressed.

question: Meridian Facilities Management Pvt Ltd, a Pune-based integrated facility services provider (GSTIN legacy ST registration GCS-PUNE-2014), filed its ST-3 returns for April 2014 to March 2015 on receipt basis, reflecting service tax liability of ₹1.82 crore. The company argues that since invoices were raised but payment was not received before 31 March 2015, no tax was payable for that period. A scrutiny notice has now been issued by the Range Officer under the detailed manual scrutiny programme. The company's CA contends that the Point of Taxation Rules 2011 had already shifted liability to accrual/invoice basis by the time these returns were filed, but the internal accounts team continued booking on receipt basis. Does the receipt-basis reporting in the ST-3 returns constitute an understatement of tax liability that can be quantified and confirmed during detailed manual scrutiny, and what is the legal exposure if the Range Officer concludes the POT Rules 2011 were not correctly applied?
why_this_chunk: The chunk explicitly states that Circular 185/4/2015 was necessitated precisely because the Point of Taxation Rules 2011 shifted liability from receipt basis to accrual basis, and the revised scrutiny procedure addresses ST-3 returns from 01.08.2015 onwards — making this the authoritative source on why POT-driven misreporting is a key scrutiny risk and how Range Officers are empowered to act on it.

grading.reason: The chunk explicitly confirms that POT Rules 2011 shifted liability from receipt to accrual basis and that detailed manual scrutiny of ST-3 returns was instituted precisely to catch such discrepancies, making it directly answerable.

question: Crestline Consulting LLP, a Delhi-based management consulting firm with pan-India operations, received a detailed manual scrutiny notice for its consolidated ST-3 return for October 2012 to March 2013 — the period immediately following introduction of negative-list-based comprehensive taxation in July 2012. The LLP had claimed exemption on a significant portion of its billings (₹3.4 crore) to a Karnataka SEZ unit, treating those as zero-rated supplies under the then-prevailing SEZ exemption notifications, and did not reflect them as taxable in the return. The scrutiny memo from the Range Officer (Connaught Place Division) flags these as 'non-disclosure of taxable value' and proposes to quantify demand. The LLP's advocate argues that the scrutiny manual in force at the time (Circular 113/07/2009) did not contemplate post-negative-list scenarios and therefore the current notice under the revised 2015 framework is procedurally improper for pre-August-2015 returns. Assess the procedural validity of applying the revised scrutiny guidelines to this return period, and advise on the merits of the SEZ exemption claim under the negative-list regime.
why_this_chunk: The chunk directly addresses both the supersession of Circular 113/07/2009-ST by the 2015 circular and the rationale that the negative-list comprehensive taxation of 2012 necessitated revised scrutiny guidelines — making it the precise authoritative source on whether the old or new scrutiny framework applies to returns spanning the negative-list transition, and on the procedural basis for the Range Officer's action.

grading.reason: The chunk explicitly states the revised scrutiny guidelines apply 'with effect from 01.08.2015' and acknowledges the prior Circular 113/07/2009, directly enabling assessment of the advocate's procedural argument; the SEZ exemption merits question is not answerable from this chunk alone, but the procedural validity question is well-supported.

question: Shimla Cineplex Pvt Ltd operated a multiplex in Himachal Pradesh and paid Entertainment Tax under the Himachal Pradesh Entertainments Tax (Cinematograph Shows) Act, 1968 up to June 2017. Post-GST, the State of Himachal Pradesh is claiming that the compensation cess base should include the full entertainment tax collections without netting out certain exemptions granted under a State Government notification issued in 2016. The company's CA is contesting this, arguing that only taxes 'net of exclusions under sub-section (1) of section 5 of the GST (Compensation to States) Act, 2017' should form the base. Which specific exclusions apply, and does the 2016 State exemption notification fall within those statutory exclusions while computing the protected revenue base for HP's compensation entitlement?
why_this_chunk: The chunk explicitly identifies the HP Entertainments Tax (Cinematograph Shows) Act 1968 as a subsumed Act under entry 62 of List-II, and critically specifies the compensation base is 'net of exclusions under sub-section (1) of section 5 of the GST (Compensation to States) Act, 2017' — this precise netting formula is indispensable to the dispute.

grading.reason: The chunk confirms the HP Entertainment Tax Act is included in the compensation base net of section 5(1) exclusions, but does not contain the actual text of section 5(1) exclusions needed to determine whether the 2016 State exemption notification falls within them — so the question cannot be fully answered from this chunk alone.

question: Precision Polymers Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 27AABCP1234Q1ZX, Maharashtra) is a manufacturer of engineering plastics with annual turnover of ₹42 crore. On 10-March-2025, the GST department issued a provisional attachment order freezing three bank accounts and a factory plot in Pune, citing apprehension of tax evasion of approximately ₹8.5 crore under an ongoing scrutiny proceeding. The company's CFO notes that the scrutiny notice was issued only 18 days ago and no show-cause notice has yet been served. The company's advocate wants to know: (a) under what precise statutory conditions can provisional attachment be invoked before a show-cause notice is issued, (b) which prescribed form must the attachment order take under the CGST Rules as notified and subsequently amended, and (c) whether simultaneous attachment of both movable bank assets and immovable factory land in a single order is procedurally valid — and if so, what is the outer time limit for which such attachment can survive without adjudication?
why_this_chunk: The chunk identifies the specific notification (3/2017-Central Tax dated 19th June 2017, as amended through subsequent notifications) that notified the CGST Rules 2017 Part B Forms — the prescribed form for provisional attachment under section 83 derives its legal authority from exactly this notification chain, making the form's validity and the procedural requirements indispensable to answering parts (b) and (c).

grading.reason: The chunk is purely a notification amendment header listing for CGST Rules Part B (Forms) with no substantive content on provisional attachment conditions, prescribed forms, or time limits — it cannot answer any part of the question.

question: Regal Retreats LLP runs a chain of boutique hotels in Shimla and Manali and paid Luxury Tax under the Himachal Pradesh Tax on Luxuries (in Hotels and Lodging Houses) Act pre-GST. In a compensation-related audit for FY 2017-18, the GST Council's designated officer is questioning whether rooms let out to foreign tourists — which were exempt from luxury tax under an HP Government circular — should be added back to the base revenue for computing HP's protected revenue under the compensation mechanism. The LLP's advocate argues that the 'net of exclusions' language in the subsuming notification means these foreign-tourist rooms must remain excluded. Separately, the Central Government is arguing that since these rooms attract IGST (as a supply to a foreign tourist), they cannot simultaneously be excluded from the compensation base. Analyze whether IGST liability on foreign-tourist room bookings affects the HP Luxury Tax exclusion for the purposes of the protected revenue base calculation.
why_this_chunk: The chunk lists the HP Tax on Luxuries (in Hotels and Lodging Houses) Act as a subsumed Act under entry 62 with the net-of-exclusions caveat, and the cross-act tension between IGST applicability and the State compensation base exclusion directly requires interpreting both the subsuming notification and the compensation statute simultaneously.

grading.reason: The chunk only lists HP luxury tax acts as subsumed taxes with a reference to 'net of exclusions under sub-section (1) of section 5' but contains no information about how foreign-tourist exemptions, IGST interaction, or the mechanics of the protected revenue base calculation work — a practitioner cannot derive the answer from this chunk alone.

question: Axiom Exports LLP (GSTIN 33AABFA5678R1ZP, Tamil Nadu) is an exporter of auto components. question: Prakash Woollen Mills Pvt Ltd, a textile manufacturer in Ludhiana, Punjab (Central Excise registration RP-CHD-2004-001), procures job-work services from multiple small processing units. The job-work charges are paid under reverse charge, and Prakash Woollen Mills itself does not provide any outward taxable service. In FY 2006-07, it paid service tax of ₹18.4 lakhs as the 'person liable' under reverse charge on GTA and job-work services. The Deputy Commissioner has disallowed CENVAT credit of this ₹18.4 lakhs on the ground that Prakash Woollen Mills is not a 'provider of taxable service' and hence cannot treat these services as 'output services' for credit purposes. Simultaneously, the company is also claiming CENVAT credit on National Calamity Contingent Duty (NCCD) paid on inputs sourced from a supplier in Haridwar. The adjudicating authority contends that NCCD is not a creditable duty head under the CENVAT chain. Advise on (a) whether the ₹18.4 lakhs service tax paid under reverse charge qualifies as credit-eligible output service, and (b) whether NCCD paid on inputs is a permissible CENVAT credit head, citing the specific statutory basis for each position.
why_this_chunk: The Explanation to clause (p) — 'output service' definition — directly resolves point (a) by deeming that a person liable to pay service tax who does not himself provide taxable service shall still have that service treated as output service. Rule 3(1)(v) explicitly lists NCCD leviable under section 136 of Finance Act 2001 as a creditable duty head, resolving point (b).

grading.reason: Both sub-questions are directly answered: the Explanation to rule (p) and clause (r) establish reverse-charge payers as output service providers/creditable, and rule 3(1)(v) explicitly lists NCCD as a creditable duty head.

question: Meridian Logistics LLP, a goods transport agency in Maharashtra with Central Excise/Service Tax registration, had its CENVAT credit account audited for the period April 2005 to March 2007. The audit team raised a demand on the following grounds: (i) Meridian availed CENVAT credit of additional excise duty paid under the Additional Duties of Excise (Goods of Special Importance) Act, 1957 on HSD/diesel used in its trucks, contending this duty head is outside the permissible credit chain; (ii) Meridian, being a GTA service provider, also paid service tax under reverse charge on certain brokerage services it received from commission agents in Delhi, and claimed those reverse-charge payments as output service tax eligible for credit flow; and (iii) Meridian further claimed credit on additional excise duty paid under the Textile and Textile Articles Act, 1978 on packing material used in its warehouse operations. The CGST Audit party (reviewing legacy records post-GST) insists all three credits were irregular. Evaluate each of the three grounds, identifying which specific duty heads and definitional provisions in the CENVAT Credit Rules, 2004 govern eligibility, and advise whether Meridian can sustain its credit positions before the appellate authority.
why_this_chunk: Rule 3(1)(iii) and 3(1)(iv) of this chunk enumerate AED (Textiles) and AED (Goods of Special Importance) as distinct, explicitly creditable duty heads — directly deciding grounds (i) and (iii). The Explanation to the 'output service' definition in clause (p), together with clause (r) including a 'person liable for paying service tax' within 'provider of taxable service', directly governs ground (ii).

grading.reason: All three credit grounds hinge on Rule 3 definitions in this chunk: items (iv) and (iii) explicitly list the two additional excise duty heads, and the output service/person liable provisions govern the reverse-charge GTA scenario.

question: Meridian Hospitality Services Pvt Ltd, registered in Maharashtra (Mumbai), received an SCN demanding Rs 1.8 crore in service tax on 'renting of immovable property' services for the period April 2013 to March 2016, plus interest of Rs 62 lakh computed to the date of the original OIO, and a penalty of Rs 1.8 crore under the equivalent of Section 78. question: Aravind Freight Forwarders LLP, a customs house agent based in Chennai (Tamil Nadu), was served an order-in-original confirming a service tax demand of Rs 45 lakh on 'clearing and forwarding agent' services for FY 2014-16, with interest of Rs 18 lakh to the OIO date and a penalty of Rs 45 lakh. question: Rajesh Menon, a resident individual in Kerala, holds three life insurance policies (all non-ULIP, all issued on 15-June-2023): Policy A with annual premium ₹2.8 lakh, Policy B with annual premium ₹1.5 lakh, and Policy C with annual premium ₹90,000. His wife Priya is the nominee on Policy C. For PY 2025-26 he receives a maturity payout of ₹18 lakh under Policy B (he is alive). His CA is arguing that Policy B's maturity proceeds are fully exempt because the premium on Policy B alone does not breach the ₹2.5 lakh threshold, while the income-tax officer contends that the aggregate of all three policies must be tested together. question: Bharat Agro Exports Pvt Ltd (IEC: AACB1234X), based in Pune, Maharashtra, imported a consignment of frozen cassava roots (HS 0714) in February 1996, shortly after the Customs Tariff was revised to its 1996 version. question: Corporal Arvind Kumar (GSTIN not applicable — individual) was enrolled under the Agnipath Scheme in July 2023. He was medically discharged in February 2026. question: Sunrise Dairy Products LLP, a Delhi-based importer, cleared a shipment of 'dairy spread' (a fat-blend product) from New Zealand in January 1996 under what it believed was a general food-preparations heading, paying Basic Customs Duty at 40%. question: Sparkle Toys & Games Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 27AABCS1234A1Z5, Maharashtra) manufactures plastic toys classified under HS 9503 and was charging 6% CGST (Schedule II rate) on all domestic supplies made between April 2021 and July 2021. question: Our client, Parekh Chemicals Pvt Ltd (Mumbai, IEC: AABCP1234Q), imported 500 MT of sodium tripolyphosphate from Petronas Trading Sdn Bhd, Kuala Lumpur, Malaysia in November 2002. The CIF invoice value was USD 185,000. At the time of filing the bill of entry on 18-Nov-2002, the RBI reference rate was Rs 48.20/USD, but the CBEC exchange rate notification in force that day showed Rs 47.85/USD. The assessable value was determined at Rs 88,72,500 by the appraising officer. Now, customs audit is questioning whether the anti-dumping duty base should have included the CVD (section 3 duty) in the 'landed value' computation, and whether the correct exchange rate was applied. Separately, since Petronas Trading is not the manufacturer but merely a trader sourcing from Chung Chemicals' plant, the importer argues they qualify for the Chung Chemicals-specific lower duty rate.
