Explanation clause (b) mandates the CBEC exchange rate notification under section 14(3)(a)(i) as of bill of entry date under section 46, resolving question (b). The deeming provision that any exporter 'other than M/s Chung Chemicals SDN BHD' falls under S.No.3 directly resolves question (c) — a trader is not Chung Chemicals even if sourcing from them.

grading.reason: All three sub-questions are directly answerable from this chunk: landed value excludes section 3 duty per the Explanation, exchange rate is CBEC notification rate at bill of entry date, and the trader-vs-manufacturer question is addressed by the deemed S.No.3 assignment clause.

question: Pharma Distributors LLP (GSTIN 29AABFP9876B1ZK, Karnataka) purchased 500 kg of Diethylcarbamazine (DEC) API from a manufacturer in Hyderabad under an invoice dated 15-May-2021 and paid CGST + SGST at the rate applicable at that time. question: Rajasthan Detergents LLP (Jaipur, IEC: AABFR5678M) entered into a contract in October 2002 to import 1,200 MT of sodium tripolyphosphate from M/s Chung Chemicals SDN BHD, Kuala Lumpur, directly, with shipment in two tranches: 600 MT shipped on 10-Dec-2002 (bill of entry filed 14-Dec-2002) and 600 MT shipped on 20-Dec-2002 (bill of entry filed 24-Dec-2002). question: Himalayan Logistics Pvt Ltd, incorporated in Delhi with its CHA licence, has been engaged by Kathmandu-based Everest Trading Co to move a consignment of 500 MT of rice from Kolkata port to Nepal via the Electronic Cargo Tracking System. question: GlobalEdge Solutions GmbH, a German software firm with no permanent establishment in India, obtains a temporary non-resident taxable person registration (GSTIN 27TEMP1234A1Z5) in Maharashtra to provide on-site technical services at a client's Mumbai facility during March and April 2026. question: Shree Vyankatesh Exports LLP, a Pune-based merchant exporter (GSTIN 27AADFS1234B1ZV), has entered into a tripartite arrangement where goods (auto components valued at ₹2.4 crore) manufactured by a third-party job worker in Ludhiana are to be shipped from Amritsar ICD to Nepal, with the Nepal importer being Pashupati Auto Parts Pvt Ltd of Kathmandu. question: Nakamura Electronics India Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 27AABCN1234F1Z5, Maharashtra) exported precision sensors to Osaka Denki KK in Japan under the India-Japan CEPA, claiming preferential duty at 0% by furnishing a certificate of origin. question: Tata Precision Components LLP, a manufacturer in Pune (Maharashtra), supplies automobile gear shafts to a Toyota group entity in Japan under CEPA preferential tariff. question: Meridian Exports LLP (GSTIN 27AABFM1234C1ZX, Maharashtra) received an adverse order from the adjudicating authority dated 12-Jan-2025 confirming a demand of ₹18.5 lakh IGST on alleged mis-classification of goods exported to a Dubai buyer, plus a penalty of ₹3.7 lakh. question: Meridian Pharma Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 27AABCM1234F1Z5, Maharashtra) receives a credit note dated 18-Mar-2025 from its API supplier in Hyderabad for ₹14.2 lakh relating to a February 2025 supply. question: Arjun Castings LLP (GSTIN 29AABFA5678K1ZQ, Karnataka) is both a recipient of GTA services (liable on reverse charge) and an ISD distributing ITC to its three manufacturing units across Karnataka, Tamil Nadu, and Andhra Pradesh. question: Meridian Infracon Pvt Ltd, a Karnataka-incorporated company (GSTIN 29AABCM1234F1Z5), entered a 25-year BOT concession agreement with NHAI in FY 2024-25 to develop and operate a national highway stretch in Rajasthan. question: Softura Solutions LLP, a Chennai-based STPI unit (GSTIN 33AABFS5678K1ZP) that exports customised ERP software to a US client, filed its Income Tax return for AY 1996-97 claiming deduction under section 80HHE without applying the erstwhile proviso that had restricted the deduction only to cases where the export turnover exceeded a specified threshold — relying on the Finance Act 1995 amendment that omitted that proviso effective 1-Apr-1996. question: Precision Polymers Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 27AABCP1234F1ZK, Maharashtra) received a demand order under section 74 for ₹38 lakhs (tax + interest + penalty) for FY 2021-22 on account of alleged ITC reversal. question: Aravalli Infracon LLP (GSTIN 08AACFA5678B1ZP, Rajasthan) had proceedings initiated against it under both section 129 (detention/seizure of goods in transit) and section 130 (confiscation) for a consignment of steel coils intercepted in Jodhpur in December 2023. question: Horizon Fuels Pvt Ltd, a licensed petroleum importer based in Kandla, Gujarat (IEC: 0819012345), has imported a consignment of Petroleum Class A in 200-litre steel drums via sea. question: Kiran Gems Pvt Ltd, a Surat-based diamond merchant with IEC 0816012345, purchased rough diamonds worth USD 2,00,000 from the domestic open market (not imported under a REP licence) and now wishes to export them to a buyer in Antwerp, Belgium, receiving payment in Euros. question: Nakshatra Polymers Pvt Ltd, a Central Excise assessee registered under the Thane-II Commissionerate (Maharashtra), attempted to discharge its March 2010 duty liability of ₹18,42,000 across seven accounting heads — covering Basic Excise Duty, Education Cess, SHE Cess, NCCD, AED (GSI), AED (TTA), and a compounded levy — in a single e-payment session through the EASIEST portal using their ICICI Bank net-banking account. question: Cauvery Fragrance Works, a proprietorship manufacturing perfumery compounds (Chapter 33, CETA) in Mysore under the Central Excise Commissionerate Mysore, uploaded its ER-1 return for Q4 FY 2009-10 on the ACES portal on 10-Apr-2010 — within the due date — but due to a server timeout the return remained in 'submitted' status and was never accepted by the application. question: Navratna Clearances Pvt Ltd, a licensed Customs House Agent operating out of Chennai (GSTIN 33AABCN1234F1ZX), was acquired by a larger logistics conglomerate, Bharat Trade Solutions LLP, in January 2025. question: Falcon Freight Services, a proprietorship CHA licensed at Nhava Sheva (JNPT), is handling a consignment of pharmaceutical APIs imported by BioSynth Laboratories Pvt Ltd (Mumbai, GSTIN 27AABCB5678G1ZP) valued at USD 2.1 million. question: Sunrise Traders, a proprietorship based in Port Blair (Andaman & Nicobar Islands) with GSTIN 35AABCS1234A1ZX, was a registered dealer under the pre-GST Central Sales Tax regime and had made inter-state sales to buyers in Tamil Nadu on Form C declarations and to an export-house in Chennai using Form H certificates, with aggregate CST credit claims of ₹8.4 lakhs pending as of 30-Jun-2017. question: Marigold Fisheries LLP, registered in Port Blair under UTGST (GSTIN 35AACFM5678B1ZP), operated as a consignment agent for a principal based in Kolkata, holding stock of frozen fish worth ₹22 lakhs in its cold-storage on 1-Jul-2017 (the GST appointed day). question: Precision Hydraulics Pvt Ltd, a manufacturer of industrial pumps in Pune (Maharashtra), sells 500 units to a bulk buyer — Bharat Infrastructure LLP in Chennai — at Rs. 48,000 per unit. question: AeroDyne Solutions LLP (Hyderabad, GSTIN 36AAHFA5678B1ZP) paid ₹3,40,000 as administration fees to their QCI-accredited certification body for a 5-year UAS certification cycle in January 2025, covering their role as both a manufacturer of nano-category UAS and an importer of micro-category UAS from Taiwan. (b) is the administration fee paid to the certification body for the 5-year cycle a 'supply of service' attracting 18% GST, and can AeroDyne LLP claim full ITC given the fee covers both manufacturing and import activity? why_this_chunk: Clause 1.10 is indispensable — it specifically obligates the applicant to notify QCI in writing of 'any significant change in name, ownership, or location' for which certification was obtained; this is the precise trigger for determining whether a partial business transfer splits the certification and creates a fresh fee/ITC reversal obligation, a determination no generic GST chunk can provide.

grading.reason: The chunk covers QCI UAS certification scheme general conditions (clause 1.10 on significant change notification is partially relevant), but contains no GST/ITC rules, proportionate reversal provisions, or fee apportionment guidance needed to answer sub-questions (b) and (c); the chunk is also in Hindi and only tangentially addresses sub-question (a) without resolving it authoritatively.

question: Electrotech Components Ltd, a registered Central Excise assessee in Bengaluru, manufactures PCB assemblies and supplies them to three different categories of buyers: (a) OEM clients at Rs. 1,20,000 per batch under long-term contracts that bundle in a 3-year warranty and on-site calibration, included in the negotiated price; (b) spot-market distributors at Rs. 95,000 per batch with no warranty, but with a separately recovered 'after-sales support levy' of Rs. 8,000 billed post-delivery; and (c) an export-linked EOU client in Noida at Rs. 1,05,000 per batch where warranty is optional and only 30% of buyers in that segment opt in, paying Rs. 6,000 additionally. The excise officer proposes to use a uniform 'normal wholesale price' across all three categories for valuation. Electrotech contests this, arguing transaction-specific valuation applies. Advise Electrotech on (i) whether a single assessable value can be applied across all three buyer categories, (ii) how the after-sales support levy in category (b) should be treated, and (iii) whether the optional warranty in category (c) should be included only in transactions where it is actually recovered or uniformly across all EOU-category transactions.
why_this_chunk: The chunk directly addresses: (1) the rejection of the Normal Wholesale Price concept in favor of transaction-specific valuation; (2) that amounts recovered 'by reason of sale or in connection with sale' (such as the after-sales support levy) are includable; and (3) the explicit clarification that optional warranty charges are included only in those specific transactions where they are actually recovered — making this chunk the primary authority for all three sub-questions.

grading.reason: All three sub-questions are directly and authoritatively answered by this chunk: (i) transaction-specific valuation vs uniform NWP, (ii) post-delivery levies as part of transaction value, and (iii) optional warranty inclusion only where actually recovered.

question: Sunrise Auto Components Pvt Ltd, a Pune-based manufacturer of automotive parts with a DGFT-recognized 'One Star Export House' status, wants to set up a private export warehouse in JNPT SEZ to store excisable goods duty-free pending shipment to its buyer in Germany. The consignment is valued at ₹4.2 crore and the company has not yet signed any MoU with DGFT. Their CE consultant is arguing that as an automobile-sector exporter they qualify under the export warehousing scheme. Does Sunrise Auto qualify to set up the warehouse, and if not, what is the precise path to eligibility — including whether signing a DGFT MoU alone would suffice or whether the MoU route is restricted to automobile manufacturers in a specific sense?
why_this_chunk: The chunk specifies that automobile manufacturers who have signed an MoU with DGFT qualify as a distinct category — separate from the Star Export House track — which is indispensable to evaluating whether Sunrise Auto's sector alone confers eligibility or whether the MoU is the operative trigger.

grading.reason: All scores are 1; chunk directly answers both sub-questions: One Star Export House does not qualify (minimum is Two Star), and the MoU route is explicitly restricted to 'automobile manufacturers' — not merely automobile-sector exporters — so signing a DGFT MoU alone would not suffice for Sunrise Auto as a component manufacturer rather than an automobile manufacturer.

question: Meridian Exports LLP, a Chennai-based textile exporter, held 'Star Trading House' status under the old Foreign Trade Policy and had been operating an export warehouse under the Central Excise export warehousing scheme since 2003. Following the Foreign Trade Policy 2004-09, its status was administratively relabelled as 'Four Star Export House' by DGFT. A Central Excise audit in 2007 is now questioning whether Meridian's warehouse authorisation remains valid post-renaming, arguing that the original approval was granted to a 'Star Trading House' and no fresh approval was obtained after the rename. Meridian's counsel contends continuity of status. Who has the stronger legal position, and what is the interaction between the FTP 2004-09 renaming, the amended circular, and the original Notification No. 46/2001-CE(NT) on the question of warehouse authorisation continuity?
why_this_chunk: The chunk explicitly records that 'Star Trading House' was renamed 'Four Star Export House' under FTP 2004-09 and that the departmental circular was amended to align with this — the authoritative basis for arguing status continuity and whether a fresh approval is legally required.

grading.reason: The chunk directly addresses the FTP 2004-09 renaming of 'Star Trading House' to 'Four Star Export House' and the consequent amendment to the circular governing export warehouse eligibility, making it the authoritative source for resolving the continuity question.

question: Meridian Exports Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 27AABCM1234F1ZX, Mumbai) shipped a consignment of processed marine products worth ₹1.8 crore to a buyer in Rotterdam in January 2017 under the then-applicable 1995 Drawback Rules, but due to a port labour strike and subsequent COVID-precursor customs freeze, they could not file the drawback claim before the 2017 Rules came into force on 01-Oct-2017. The shipping bill was dated 15-Aug-2017 and the drawback claim was filed only on 20-Nov-2017. The jurisdictional Commissioner has rejected the claim saying it must now be processed under the 2017 Rules, while the company argues the export pre-dates the 2017 Rules and the 1995 Rules should govern. Additionally, they argue that the delay in filing was due to reasons entirely beyond their control (port strike + systems migration). Can Meridian Exports Pvt Ltd simultaneously invoke the transitional savings clause and seek discretionary relief from the Central Government for the procedural delay, and which set of rules — 1995 or 2017 — will actually govern the substantive determination of the drawback amount?
why_this_chunk: Rule 20(2)(b) directly governs whether a claim for exports made before 01-Oct-2017 but filed after commencement is processed under 1995 or 2017 Rules; Rule 19 is indispensable for the force majeure / beyond-control relief argument for the procedural delay — both rules appear verbatim in this chunk.

grading.reason: All key provisions are present: Rule 19 (discretionary relief for beyond-control delays) and Rule 20 (savings clause preserving 1995 Rules for pre-commencement exports not yet disposed), directly resolving both prongs of the question.

question: Pinnacle Pharma LLP (Hyderabad, GSTIN 36AAFCP5678K1ZM) exported active pharmaceutical ingredients to a distributor in Nairobi under a Letter of Credit dated 10-Sep-2017. The export took place on 25-Sep-2017 — five days before the 2017 Drawback Rules came into force. Due to an RBI-mandated banking channel change for Kenya-bound remittances, the foreign exchange realisation certificate (FIRC) could not be obtained in time, and Pinnacle was unable to comply with the export proceeds realisation condition required for drawback eligibility. The bank confirmed the funds were received but routed through an intermediary correspondent bank in UAE, causing a 14-month delay in FIRC issuance. The drawback application (filed under the 1995 Rules per their understanding of the savings clause) was rejected on two grounds: (a) the claim disposition should follow 2017 Rules since it was filed post-commencement, and (b) non-realisation of proceeds disqualifies drawback. Pinnacle now wants to know: does the savings clause protect their right to have the claim adjudicated under 1995 Rules, and separately, can they petition the Central Government to condone the FIRC-related non-compliance as a circumstance beyond their control, given that the banking restriction was a regulatory directive and not voluntary?
why_this_chunk: Rule 20(2)(b) is the pivotal provision on whether post-commencement filing of a pre-commencement export claim is governed by 1995 or 2017 Rules; Rule 19's 'reasons beyond his control' standard is the only legal basis for condoning the FIRC non-compliance caused by the RBI banking directive — both are sourced exclusively from this chunk.

grading.reason: All scores are 1; chunk directly addresses both the savings clause (Rule 20) protecting 1995 Rules adjudication for pre-commencement exports and Rule 19 power to condone non-compliance for reasons beyond control.

question: Shree Textile Udhyog LLP (GSTIN 24AAFFS1234A1ZX), a Surat-based manufacturer of imitation zari thread (HSN 5605) using metallised polyester film, sizing chemicals, dyes, and carton packaging, filed a refund claim for accumulated ITC of ₹38 lakhs for the quarter ending December 2023. grading.reason: All scores are 1; the chunk is the exact notification creating S.No. 6AA with the Explanation that limits ITC refund eligibility to polyester/plastic film only, directly resolving the officer-vs-CA dispute and enabling computation analysis.

question: Hindustan Petro Extractions Ltd (GSTIN 27AABCH1234F1Z5, Maharashtra) operates an upstream crude oil production block in Rajasthan and lifts 18,000 tonnes of Petroleum Crude during the billing fortnight 19-Dec-2023 to 31-Dec-2023. question: Zari Creations Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 27AABCZ5678B1ZQ), Maharashtra, manufactures imitation zari thread (HSN 5605) and supplies it on an inter-state basis to a garment exporter in Tamil Nadu — the garment exporter uses the zari thread in apparel that is then physically exported. question: Bharat Fuels & Chemicals LLP, a refinery in Gujarat (GSTIN 24AAKFB9876Q1ZA), produces motor spirit (petrol) and also blends it with ethanol before dispatch ex-refinery. No. 2 from 19-Dec-2023 — but contains nothing to answer (ii) blended-fuel volume apportionment or (iii) the legal-validity analysis of s.5A/s.147 interplay for unlisted blended fuels; those require the principal notification table, the Finance Act text, and interpretive guidance not present here.

question: Aravind Polymers Pvt Ltd, a Central Excise registered manufacturer of plastic granules (ECC No. AAACA1234AXM001) operating from a factory in Silvassa (Dadra & Nagar Haveli), underwent a private equity-driven merger in March 2006 where it was absorbed into Reliance Plastics LLP, with the LLP subsequently opening a second manufacturing unit in Pune. The audit team is now proposing to treat all clearances made from both the Silvassa and Pune units during this period as clearances without registration, invoking extended period of limitation. The LLP's advocate argues that since Aravind Polymers' registration was never formally revoked, the LLP's operations were covered. question: Bharat Forge Components Pvt Ltd, registered under Central Excise at its Aurangabad plant (CEID: AABCB5678CXM002) for manufacture of forged steel auto parts, underwent a Board resolution in January 2004 converting itself from a private limited company into a partnership firm — Bharat Forge Components & Associates — with three partners, two of whom were the original promoter-directors. Clause (7) defines the exhaustive grounds for revocation (breach of Act/rules or IPC conviction), which is the linchpin for evaluating whether non-intimation alone can support revocation proceedings.

question: Reliance Petro Imports Pvt Ltd (Mumbai, IEC AABCR1234A) has contracted to import 50,000 MT of crude petroleum from a Brunei-based supplier, Brunei Energy Co Ltd, at USD 72/bbl CIF Nhava Sheva, scheduled for arrival on 10-Feb-2025. The supplier proposes to route the shipment through Singapore for transshipment, and the Bill of Lading will show Singapore as the port of loading. The supplier can provide a Form AI (ASEAN-India FTA Certificate of Origin) issued by the Brunei authority certifying Brunei origin. Customs at Nhava Sheva is questioning whether the nil duty exemption on crude petroleum from Brunei under the ASEAN-India FTA is still available given (a) transshipment through Singapore, (b) the fact that Notification 116/2010-Customs was the original dispensation and it is unclear if it was validly superseded, and (c) whether tariff item 2709 00 00 covers all grades of crude or only specific grades. Advise Reliance Petro Imports on each of these three issues and the risk of duty demand if the exemption is denied on a 50,000 MT shipment.

grading.reason: All three issues are directly addressed: supersession of 116/2010 is explicit in the notification text, 2709 00 00 scope is defined, and the origin rules reference (ASEAN-India FTA Rules 2009) governs transshipment/direct consignment requirements — a practitioner reading only this chunk can give a substantive first-level answer on all three prongs.

question: Bharat Hydrocarbon LLP (GSTIN 27AABFB5678M1ZK, Mumbai) imports crude petroleum from Brunei Energy Resources Sdn Bhd under a long-term supply agreement. question: Electronica Refurb LLP, registered in Tamil Nadu (GSTIN 33AABFE1234Q1ZX), is a trading house that imports second-life laptop motherboards and CPU modules — listed under Schedule VIII (Basel B1110) — on behalf of three actual users: two IT companies in Pune and one in Hyderabad. Simultaneously, the Pune actual users argue they need EPR authorisation only in their own names, not the LLP's. Please advise: (a) what is the correct authorisation architecture — one combined authorisation vs. per-user per-consignment — and which specific form and enclosure must accompany each Bill of Entry; (b) whether the EPR obligation under the E-Waste (Management & Handling) Rules 2011 attaches to the LLP as importer-trader or to the actual users as producers; and (c) what documentary gate does Customs apply before allowing clearance, and who bears the liability if documents are found deficient post-clearance?

Clause (4) specifies that Customs verifies documents per Schedule VIII column (3) before clearance — resolving (c).

grading.reason: The question is hyper-constructed with specific GSTIN, LLP names, and multiple sub-parts — no real practitioner would phrase a query this way; it reads like a law school moot problem, not a CA/advocate query.

question: Greentek Circuits Pvt Ltd, a Maharashtra-based manufacturer (GSTIN 27AABCG5678R1Z1), wants to import used printed circuit board assemblies (Schedule III Part D waste) consigned from a recycler in Germany, for in-house dismantling and metal recovery at its Nagpur facility. The company already holds valid consents under the Water and Air pollution Acts from Maharashtra SPCB. Its procurement head asks: (a) is DGFT import authorisation a mandatory pre-condition for this consignment, or can customs clear on the basis of SPCB consents alone; (b) what is the specific document-verification step that the customs authority at Nhava Sheva must perform before approving the shipment, and which schedule column governs this; and (c) if the Ministry of Environment's review process is triggered, what is the maximum statutory window before a decision must be communicated, and what external body's comments must the Ministry consider — and can Greentek's shipment be held at port during this entire window without any provisional release?
why_this_chunk: Clause (क) of the chunk establishes DGFT import authorisation as a distinct, independent requirement ('if applicable') separate from pollution-control consents — resolving (a). Clause (4) mandates Customs verify documents per Schedule VIII column (3) before approving Schedule III Part D waste shipments — resolving (b). Clause (5) specifies the 60-day window and the mandatory consideration of State Pollution Control Board comments before MoEFCC grants import permission — directly resolving (c).

grading.reason: All three sub-questions are directly addressed: (a) DGFT authorisation is required 'if applicable' per clause (क), not as a standalone replacement for SPCB consents; (b) Schedule VIII column (3) document verification is explicitly mandated for Schedule III Part D consignments per sub-rule (4); (c) the 60-day window and State Pollution Control Board comments requirement are stated in sub-rule (5), though provisional release during the window is not addressed in this chunk.

question: Sparkle Impex Pvt Ltd, a Delhi-based importer and distributor (GSTIN 07AABCS1234F1ZX), sources two SKUs from a Bengaluru manufacturer: (a) 'BabyFresh' wet wipes impregnated with chamomile fragrance and aloe-vera cosmetic extract, and (b) 'HygieneFirst' antibacterial wet wipes coated with a sodium lauryl sulphate detergent solution. Sparkle's in-house counsel suspects the classification is wrong but the manufacturer argues the textile substrate gives the essential character. A GST audit notice has now been issued for FY 2023-24 demanding differential tax. What is the correct HS classification and GST rate for each SKU, which interpretive rule determines essential character here, and what is the differential tax exposure if Sparkle's purchases were ₹4.8 crore for SKU (a) and ₹3.2 crore for SKU (b) across the audit period?
why_this_chunk: The chunk's GRI-3(b) essential-character analysis, the HSN exclusion of impregnated non-wovens from HS 5603, and the specific rule that fragrance/cosmetic wipes fall under 3307 at 18% while soap/detergent wipes fall under 3401 at 18% are indispensable to resolving the differential-tax calculation for each SKU.

grading.reason: All elements answered by the chunk: GRI 3(b) essential character rule, HS 5603 exclusion, HS 3307 for cosmetic/perfume wipes at 18%, HS 3401 for soap/detergent wipes at 18%; differential tax calculation (6% on ₹8cr total) is straightforward arithmetic the practitioner can do from the rates given.

question: Vastra Zari Works LLP, a Surat-based manufacturer (GSTIN 24AABFV5678K1ZM), produces two products on the same production line: (i) raw Kasab metallised yarn (sold in spools to embroidery units in Rajasthan), and (ii) finished embroidered Kasab strips already woven into fabric motifs (sold to garment exporters in Tirupur). The LLP has been applying a uniform 5% GST rate on both products, relying on the embroidery/zari heading, and has accumulated ₹60 lakh of ITC claims at that rate. grading.reason: The chunk covers only the classification of raw Kasab yarn (5605 vs 5809/5810) and does not address the finished embroidered strips' correct treatment, ITC reversal obligations, or export-refund entitlement under LUT/zero-rating — making the question only partially answerable from this chunk alone.

question: Fibratex Synthetics Pvt Ltd, a synthetic textile manufacturer in Surat (GSTIN 24AABCF1234A1Z5), operates spinnerette-based filament extrusion lines. Their German OEM, Enka GmbH, offers an exchange programme: Fibratex ships worn-out spinnerettes (containing 18g gold, 4g platinum, 0.5g rhodium each) and receives refurbished ones back. The refurbished units contain 20g gold, 4.5g platinum, and 0.6g rhodium each. Enka charges: (a) EUR 210 per unit as labour/reworking, (b) EUR 85 per unit for the precious-metal top-up (excess gold/platinum/rhodium), and (c) EUR 30 per unit handling. Ocean freight both ways is EUR 40/unit and insurance is EUR 6/unit. The consignment is cleared at Nhava Sheva and then dispatched to Fibratex's plant. Their Central Excise consultant in Surat is arguing that only the labour charge (EUR 210) forms the assessable value because the spinnerettes 'belong' to Fibratex throughout. The jurisdictional AC-CE disagrees. Under the conditions annexed to the concessional-rate notification for Chapter 84-98 goods, what is the correct method to arrive at the value on which Central Excise duty is leviable for the exchanged spinnerettes, and which specific cost components must be included or excluded?
why_this_chunk: Condition 1 of the Annexure sets out the exhaustive valuation formula for spinnerette exchange: labour charges + price of precious-metal excess quantities (gold/platinum/rhodium above what was in the worn-out units) + any other charges + insurance and freight both ways. This formula directly resolves the dispute — the precious-metal top-up and freight/insurance both ways are expressly included, refuting Fibratex's consultant's position.

grading.reason: All scoring criteria met: the chunk's Condition 1 directly and completely answers the valuation question including which components (labour, excess precious metal, other charges, two-way freight/insurance) must be included, making it fully answerable from this chunk alone.

question: Ashok Motors Ltd, a commercial-vehicle manufacturer in Pune, cleared 120 units of a 16-tonne chassis-mounted refrigerated vehicle in March 2006 under a concessional-rate Central Excise notification for Chapter 84-98 goods. The applicable concessional rate was 8% ad valorem. However, at the time of clearance the company's ERP system erroneously applied the standard Schedule-I rate of 16% and debited the excess duty to its Account Current as required. The additional controller of accounts now wants to file a refund claim for the excess duty paid (approximately Rs. 1.14 crore) but the claim is being filed in October 2006 — seven months after the date of duty payment on the first batch. The DC-CE (Pune Commissionerate) is refusing the refund, citing time-bar. Separately, the company's internal audit has flagged that CENVAT credit was availed on the compressor units fitted to these vehicles. Advise Ashok Motors on: (i) whether the October 2006 refund claim is maintainable given the 6-month window stipulated in the Annexure conditions, and (ii) whether the prior availment of CENVAT credit on the compressors independently disentitles the company from the concessional rate, even if the time-bar issue were resolved in its favour.
why_this_chunk: Condition 8 of the Annexure prescribes the exact mechanism — pay at Schedule-I/II rates, credit the excess to Account Current, then file a refund claim 'before the expiry of six months from the date of payment of duty.' This is the specific rule that creates the time-bar question. Condition 5 independently bars the concessional rate if CENVAT credit has been taken on the chassis or compressor, creating the second prong of the analysis.

grading.reason: The chunk directly addresses both issues: Condition 5 explicitly bars CENVAT credit on chassis/compressor for concessional rate eligibility, and Condition 8 sets out the 6-month refund window — making this chunk highly specific and fully answerable.

question: Bharat Infra Components Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 27AABCB1234F1Z5, Maharashtra) is filing its GSTR-9 for FY 2019-20. During the year it availed ₹48 lakh ITC — ₹12 lakh on capital goods (two CNC machines purchased in July 2019), ₹22 lakh on input services, and ₹14 lakh on raw material inputs. Additionally, it received ₹3.2 lakh ITC from its Input Service Distributor (HO in Delhi). In table 4A of GSTR-3B, the finance team had clubbed all ₹48 lakh under 'inputs' without breaking out capital goods. Now at annual return time, the CFO wants to know: (a) Is the company legally required to report the ₹12 lakh capital goods ITC separately in GSTR-9 for FY 2019-20, or can it continue clubbing under 'inputs' as done in GSTR-3B? (b) Where exactly must the ₹3.2 lakh ISD credit be declared, and does it flow into the row 6J reconciliation? (c) If row 6J does not reconcile to zero after these adjustments, what is the exposure?
why_this_chunk: The chunk's FY 2019-20 specific instruction mandates that capital goods ITC must be broken out separately (unlike FY 2017-18/2018-19 where full clubbing under 'inputs' was permitted), while only the remaining amount has the option to be clubbed — directly answering (a). Row 6G governs ISD credit declaration and row 6J governs reconciliation — directly answering (b) and (c).

grading.reason: All three sub-questions are directly answerable from this chunk: (a) FY 2019-20 capital goods breakout is mandatory per footnote 88; (b) ISD credit goes to row 6G; (c) row 6J should ideally be zero, implying any non-zero amount is an unexplained difference.

question: Sunrise Pharma LLP (GSTIN 29AACFS9876K1ZQ, Karnataka) had availed ₹9.5 lakh ITC through FORM GST TRAN-I in FY 2017-18 on closing stock of excise-paid goods. Of this, ₹2.1 lakh was subsequently found ineligible by the GST officer during scrutiny and was reversed in a later GSTR-3B period. Also, during FY 2017-18, the LLP availed ₹6 lakh ITC on imported services from its Singapore-based parent (no PE in India), and separately reversed ₹1.4 lakh ITC on exempt supplies under rule 42 of CGST Rules. While preparing GSTR-9 for FY 2017-18, the tax manager is confused about: (a) whether the ₹2.1 lakh ineligible TRAN-I credit reversal should appear in table 7E or 7H or some other head; (b) whether the ₹6 lakh import-of-services ITC goes into row 6E or 6F; and (c) given the FY 2017-18 relaxation, can the entire ITC (including the import-of-services credit) be lumped under 'inputs' in the breakup rows, or does the relaxation apply only to domestic inputs vs capital goods?
why_this_chunk: Row 7H specifically covers ITC reversed through FORM ITC-03, while the chunk states that ineligible transition credit claimed under TRAN-I and subsequently reversed must be declared in rows 7A–7H — resolving (a). Row 6F governs import of services (excluding SEZ inward supplies) — resolving (b). The FY 2017-18 relaxation clause in the chunk (option to report entire ITC under 'inputs' row only) — resolving (c), including whether it extends to all ITC categories.

grading.reason: All three sub-questions are directly addressable from the chunk: ineligible TRAN-I reversal goes in 7A-7H (not 7E per the 4D/4A condition explained), import-of-services ITC goes in 6F, and the FY 2017-18 relaxation explicitly allows lumping all ITC under 'inputs' including import-of-services.

question: Precision Forgings Pvt Ltd (GSTIN 27AABCP1234F1ZK, Maharashtra) exported alloy-steel flanges in FY 2005-06 and FY 2008-09 under two separate DFIA authorisations. The 2005-06 authorisation was endorsed as transferable in March 2007 and subsequently sold to a Mumbai trader; the 2008-09 authorisation was used by Precision itself to import nickel alloy inputs duty-free. During a post-export audit in 2024, the jurisdictional Commissioner of Customs (JNCH) raises a show-cause notice alleging (a) that CENVAT credit on input chemicals was availed before the 2005-06 exports, making those exports ineligible for DFIA duty exemption, and (b) that the transferred 2005-06 authorisation's buyer never paid the additional customs duty required on transfer. Precision argues that the 2005-06 authorisation, having been issued prior to 1 April 2007, carries a blanket exemption from additional duty of customs even post-transfer under the DOC policy in the then-current FTP. Advise Precision on: (i) whether the pre-1.4.2007 carve-out insulates the transferred authorisation from duty recovery despite CENVAT availed on inputs; (ii) what evidentiary steps the Commissioner's questionnaire requires Precision to satisfy for the 2008-09 authorisation; and (iii) which authority (JNCH or the Chief Commissioner) must submit the recovery-status report and by what deadline.
why_this_chunk: The chunk's para (d) explicitly states that for authorisations issued prior to 1.4.2007, exemption from additional duty/excise duty continues even after transferability endorsement — this is the precise carve-out Precision relies on. The questionnaire items 2 and 6 in the chunk define the CENVAT/rule-18/rule-19(2) disqualification test and the TR-6 Challan proof requirement for transferred DFIAs. Para 11 specifies the Chief Commissioner reporting deadline of 31 May 2009 (and the reporting structure), which is the jurisdictional-supervision question.

grading.reason: All three sub-questions are directly answerable from this chunk: (i) the pre-1.4.2007 carve-out is explicitly stated in para (d); (ii) the questionnaire items 2-6 specify evidentiary requirements for 2008-09 authorisation use; (iii) para 11 names the Chief Commissioner and 31st May 2009 deadline.

question: Axiom Chemicals LLP (Rajasthan, IEC 0814012345) exported speciality dyes in 2006-07 under DFIA and received an authorisation that was later endorsed as transferable. Axiom sold that authorisation to Vertex Dyes Pvt Ltd (Gujarat), which used it to import duty-free titanium dioxide. Vertex subsequently transferred the titanium dioxide to a job-worker who used it to manufacture pigments for exempt goods (non-dutiable). In a 2024 enforcement review, the Ahmedabad Customs Commissionerate issues notices to both Axiom and Vertex, contending: (1) Axiom had availed rebate under rule 18 of the Central Excise Rules, 2002 on inputs used in the exported dyes, which bars the DFIA benefit; and (2) Vertex's use of the duty-free replenishment in manufacture of non-dutiable goods triggers full customs duty recovery. Vertex counters that since Axiom's original authorisation pre-dates 1 April 2007, any additional duty question is extinguished. Separately, Axiom argues that even if rule-18 rebate was availed, the circular's own questionnaire procedure must be completed before any recovery action can be initiated, and that a certificate from the jurisdictional Central Excise Superintendent is the required evidentiary standard. Analyse: (i) whether the pre-1.4.2007 exemption under para (d) of this circular shields Vertex from additional customs duty on the transferred DFIA; (ii) whether Axiom's rule-18 availed status independently breaks the DFIA chain regardless of the pre-2007 date; and (iii) what documentary proof Vertex must produce to the Commissioner to discharge its burden regarding the non-dutiable end-use of the replenishments.
why_this_chunk: The chunk's questionnaire item 5 specifically addresses duty liability when replenishments are used in manufacture of non-dutiable goods and requires a Central Excise Superintendent certificate or proof of duty payment — this is the precise evidentiary standard at issue for Vertex. Para (d) of the chunk is the only source for the pre-1.4.2007 transferability exemption that Vertex invokes. Questionnaire item 2 defines the rule-18/rule-19(2)/CENVAT bar which is the basis for Ahmedabad Customs' notice against Axiom.

grading.reason: All three sub-questions are directly answerable from the chunk: (i) para (d) explicitly shields pre-1.4.2007 authorisations from additional duty recovery; (ii) questionnaire item 2 identifies rule-18 rebate as a disqualifying factor; (iii) questionnaire item 5 specifies the exact evidentiary standard—certificate from jurisdictional Central Excise Superintendent for dutiable use, or proof of duty payment for non-dutiable use.
